if I were to ask you what two times three is, - well I think at this point this should be pretty easy for you. - That's the same thing as two plus two plus two, which equals six. - You didn't have to do this. - You all know that two times three is equal to six. - So what we're going to do now is - we're going to learn exponents which are the same thing - that multiplication is to addition, exponents are to multiplication. - I'll explain that in a second. - I know that probably confused you. - If I were to say what two to the third power is, - instead of two plus two plus two, - this is equal to two times two times two, which equals eight. - Or if I were to say three the second power, - that is equal to three times three. - Remember, I said three times two would be three plus three. - So the first three times three this equals nine, - and three plus three equals six. - The reason why I'm doing this is because there's always this temptation - when you first learn exponents is to multiply. - When I first learned it and I saw three to the second power or three squared, - I'd always be like oh, that's six. - But you also remember it's three times three, - which equals nine. - Let's do some more problems. - If I were to tell you negative four squared. - Once again, that's the same thing as - negative four times negative four. - Well, we all learn from multiplying negative numbers - that a negative times a negative is a positive. - And then four times four, - well this equals positive sixteen. - You don't have to write the positive, - I'm just doing that for emphasis. - If I would ask you what negative four to the third power is, - well that equals negative four times negative four - times negative four. - Well we know already that negative four times negative four, - that this equals sixteen, positive sixteen, - and then we multiply that times negative four. - And then that equals minus sixty-four. - So something very interesting here to observe. - When I took a negative number, - and we call this the base, - when the base is negative, - in this case, negative four, - and I raise it to an even power, - I got a positive number, right? - Negative four to an even power is positive sixteen, - and when I took a negative number to an odd power, to three, - I got a negative number. - And that makes sense because every time - you multiply by a negative number again, - it switches signs. - I'll show you the, I guess you'd call it - the simplest example. - Negative one to the one power is equal to negative one, right? - Because that's just negative one times itself one time. - And if I said negative one squared, - well that's negative one times negative one, - well that equals positive one. - But if I said negative one to the third power, - once again that's negative one times negative one times negative one. - Well now this equals negative one times negative one is positive one - times negative one equals negative one again. - So I could tell you - what negative one to the fifty-first power is. - Because fifty-one is odd, - we know that that is equal to negative one. - If it was a fifty then it would be a positive one. - Hope I didn't confuse you too much. - Let's do a couple more problems. - If I asked you what five to the third power is, - well that will go into five times five times five, - which equals one hundred and twenty-five. - So really if I were ask to ask - what negative five to the third power is, - that would be negative five times negative five times negative five, - which would be negative one hundred and twenty-five. - Now one principle of exponents - that might not seem completely intuitive to you at first - is when I raise something to the zero power. - So let's say I had two to the zero power. - It turns out that anything to the zero power is equal to one. - So two to the zero power is one, - three to the zero power is equal to one, - negative nine hundred to the zero power is equal to one. - And let me see if I can give you a little bit of intuition of - why that is actually the case. - So if I were to ask you, - let's do three to the fourth power. - That equals three times three times three times three, - which equals eighty-one. - three to the third power is equal to twenty-seven, - this three times three times three. - three to second power is equal to nine. - three to the first power is equal to three. - Now we're going to say what's three to zero power? - Well we already know, I already told you the rule, - anything with the zero power is equal to one, - but this will hopefully give you some intuition. - When we went from the fourth power to the third power, - we divided by three, right? - eighty-one divided by three is twenty-seven. - When you went from the third power to the second power, - we divided by three. - When we went from the second power from nine to three, - we divided by three. - So it kind of makes logical sense that - when we go from the first power to the zero power, - we'll just divide by three again. - So three divided by three is one. - Hopefully that gives you a little bit of an intuitive sense. - You might want to replay that and think about why that is. - And there's actually other aspects of exponents that - why this also makes sense, - why something to the zero power - is equal to one. - But let's just do some more problems in the time we have. - I don't want to get you too confused. - So if I were to ask you seven squared, - well that's seven times seven, that's forty-nine. - If I asked you negative six to the third power - -- parentheses around here so you know it's a whole negative six to the third power-- - that equals negative six times negative six times negative six. - Negative six times negative six is positive thirty-six times negative six, - and that equals what? - It's one hundred and eighty and thirty-six, - that's minus two hundred and sixteen, - if my mental math is correct. - You could have actually multiplied it out. - I think you're getting the point at this point. - Oh, and another thing, - if I told you zero to the hundredth power, - well, that's pretty easy. - That's zero times itself one hundred times, - which is still equal to zero. - If I were to ask you one to the thousandth power, - well that's just equal to one, right? - You can multiply one by itself as many times as you want - and you're still going to get one. - And remember, if I had negative one to the one thousandth power, - well, this is an even exponent so you're still going to get one. - If it was negative one to the one thousand and one, - then it would be negative one. - I think you remember why this is, - because when you multiply a negative times itself an even number of times, - the negatives cancel out. - And then if you multiply it by a negative one more time - it becomes a negative number again. - Well let's just do some normal problems. - I just want to make sure you get the basics of exponents down. - If I were to tell you - -- let me think of a good one-- - eight squared, - that equals eight times eight equals sixty-four. - If I were to tell you twenty-five squared, - that's twenty-five times twenty-five, which equals six hundred and twenty-five. - Powers of two is always very interesting. - It's especially interesting - if you one day go into computer science. - So two to the fourth power, - that's two times two times two times two. - So two times two is four so this equals sixteen. - And I did something very interesting here - kind of on purpose. - Notice that two to the fourth is equal to four times four, right? - Because we did four times four here. - I'm going to detail this more later on, - but I want to think about what that means. - Because four itself is the same thing as two squared. - So we learned, just real fast, - two to the fourth is the same thing - as two squared times two squared. - So I'll let you sit and think about that, - but other than that I think you have the general idea - of how basic exponents work, - and I think you're ready to try the level one exponent module. Title: How to Bend Pipe Without a Pipe Bender Introduction: Bending pipe without a specialized tool like a pipe bender can be challenging but is definitely achievable with some basic tools and know-how. This guide will walk you through the process of manually bending pipes using simple equipment found around most homes or garages. We'll focus primarily on metal pipes, as they are more common and difficult to bend than PVC ones. Materials Needed: 1. Metal pipe (ensure it's not under any pressure) 2. Protective gloves and eyewear 3. Marker pen or chalk 4. Measuring tape 5. Hacksaw or power saw 6. Sandpaper or file 7. Two sturdy pieces of wood 8. Clamps (C-clamp or G-clamp) 9. Propane torch (optional) Step 1: Safety First Always wear protective gear when handling sharp objects and open flames. Gloves protect your hands from cuts and burns while safety glasses shield your eyes from debris. Step 2: Measurement and Marking Using your measuring tape, measure and mark the point where you want your bend to begin and end. Make sure these marks are clear and visible. If possible, draw a line connecting them using your marker pen or chalk. Key Tip: The radius of the bend should be at least three times the diameter of the pipe for strength and longevity reasons. Step 3: Cutting the Pipe Cut the pipe along the marked line using a hacksaw or power saw. After cutting, smooth out rough edges with sandpaper or a file to prevent injuries and ensure a clean bend. Step 4: Preparation for Bending Place one piece of wood under the area that needs bending. Position the other piece on top so that the cut section is between the two blocks of wood. Clamp down securely to hold everything in place. Step 5: Applying Heat (Optional) If the pipe material allows, heating up the section to be bent may make the process easier. Use a propane torch for this, applying heat evenly across the surface until it turns slightly red. Be careful not to overheat or warp the pipe. Allow time for cooling before proceeding if you choose to do this step. Key Guideline: Not all metals can be heated and bent safely. Some materials such as stainless steel could crack or become brittle when exposed to high temperatures. Always check compatibility before attempting to heat the pipe. Step 6: Bending the Pipe Apply force slowly and steadily onto the part of the pipe protruding above the upper wooden block. As you push down, the clamped section beneath will start to curve upward. Keep pushing until you reach the desired angle. Remember, patience is key here; rushing could result in kinking or breaking the pipe. Step 7: Checking Progress Regularly stop and check whether the bend matches your original plan. You might need to adjust your positioning or exert additional effort depending on what you see. It's better to correct mistakes early rather than after completing the bend. Step 8: Final Touches Once satisfied with the bend, remove the clamps and unsupported wood pieces. Smooth any burrs or sharp edges left behind by the bending process with sandpaper or a file. Conclusion: While manual pipel bending requires more time and effort compared to machine-assisted methods, it provides an affordable alternative for small-scale projects. With practice, anyone can master this skill and create professional-looking results. Her brother and her mother knew both what they wanted. This is the first time I have talked of all that, the first time since I took the train from that place. I am not going to tell you all about the day. A Visual Model of the Doppler Effect Grade Level: kindergarten to 2nd; Type: Physics This project uses a toy race car moving through paper circles representing soundwaves to visually model the Doppler Effect. - What causes the Doppler Effect? - How does the distance between soundwaves effect pitch? Vrooooooom! Vrooooooom! Here we are folks in the final lap of the Doppler 500 and look at those cars speed by. Let's cheer them on by making racecar sounds Vroom! Vroom! Let's do it again Vroom! Hey, did you notice how your racecar voice starts out high and ends up low? Let's do it again. Vrooom! That's right, that's how race cars really sound when they go by. But why? It is because of something called the Doppler Effect. Sound moves from the car to you in waves. Lets make a model of these waves. - Construction paper - Small toy car - Cut the construction paper long-ways into strips about 1" wide. - Cut the strips into lengths of decreasing size, each strip being about 2" shorter that the previous. (If you are working with smallish paper, begin by taping strips together so as to get more length. It doesn't matter exactly how long the strips are as long as they regularly decrease in length). - Now tape the ends of each strip together to make circles. - Lets pretend these paper circles are sound waves. When the car is still, sound waves move outward from the car. Lay the circles on the floor inside each other and put the racecar right in the middle. This is a model of sound coming from an idling, not moving, racecar. - Now, we're goig to race the car. Pretend it's going fast, but we'll do it in slow motion so we can see what's happening. Drive the car in one direction, letting it push on the rings in front of it. What's happening to the rings? (The rings, being pushed by the toy car, should be getting bunched together in front of the car and getting farther apart behind it.) That is exactly what happens to the sound waves as a racecar speeds by you. The waves in front of the car get closer together and the waves behind the car get further apart. Close together waves make a high sound. Far apart waves make a low sound. Thus, the Doppler Effect. Vroom! Terms/Concepts: doppler effect, sound waves, pitch, high, low, model References: Galactic Cookie Dough, 50 Hands-On Science Experiments That Explore Astronomy, by B.K. Hixson, pp. 174-177 (Loose in the Lab, Inc., 2003). Warning is hereby given that not all Project Ideas are appropriate for all individuals or in all circumstances. Implementation of any Science Project Idea should be undertaken only in appropriate settings and with appropriate parental or other supervision. Reading and following the safety precautions of all materials used in a project is the sole responsibility of each individual. There are three major positive outcome of forgetting: Imagine: you were asked: "What is the name of your best friend?". Let's pretend that: - Your brain remembers everything. - In different periods of your life you had different friends. In this case your brain will pick up several names instead of one. As a result: Let's pretend now that your brain gradually forgets old information. That is the brain still remembers all friends, but remembrances about old friends require some mental concentration. In this case the brain will return exact answer (for the question "What is the name of your best friend?"). Resume: forgetting helps to find exact answer. Let's pretend that John Doe is 30 years old now (2003 year). So his brain keeps this information ("I'm 30 years old"). What will happen if you ask John about his age today? Most probably John will say "I'm 30 years old". What will happen if you ask John about his age 10 years later (2013 year)? Will John answer that he "is 30 years old"? Most probably - he will not. At 2013 John will forget that he "is 30 years old" and will remember that he "is 40 years old". Resume: forgetting helps to easily update obsolete information. Today, let's explore a fun inequality problem that involves some clever thinking and a little bit of geometry. We will try to understand why the following inequality holds true for any positive numbers \(x\_1, x\_2, ..., x\_n\): \[ (x\_1x\_2...x\_n)^{1/n} \leq \frac{1}{n}(x\_1 + x\_2 + ... + x\_n) \] Don't worry if the formula looks intimidating; it becomes more accessible once we break it down into smaller pieces. Let's first consider what each part means: * \((x\_1x\_2...x\_n)\) represents multiplying \(x\_1, x\_2, ..., x\_n\) together. This product is called the geometric mean of these numbers. * \(\frac{1}{n}(x\_1 + x\_2 + ... + x\_n)\) calculates the average of \(x\_1, x\_2, ..., x\_n\), then multiplies it by \(\frac{1}{n}\). This result is called the arithmetic mean of those numbers. * Both sides get raised to the power of \(\frac{1}{n}\), making the quantities easier to compare. Now, imagine having a collection of \(n\) balloons with volumes \(x\_1, x\_2, ..., x\_n\). Our goal is to arrange them so that their total volume is maximized while keeping the same "roundness." To make sense of roundness here, envision taking a cross-section of the grouped balloons along one axis (say, left to right). In order to keep things fair, we require the cross-sectional area to have the shape of a regular polygon with \(n\) sides-each side being of length \(\sqrt{\frac{(x\_1^2 + x\_2^2 + ... + x\_n^2)}{n}}\). With our requirements set up, we now need to find out how to best inflate the balloons to achieve maximum overall volume under this constraint. Interestingly enough, the optimal solution comes from equally filling each balloon, meaning giving every balloon the same volume. When doing so, the resulting combined shape resembles a cube (or a square if \(n = 2\)) or another regular polyhedron (regular polygonal prism if \(n > 3\)). By setting all \(x\_i\)'s equal to each other and equaling the common value to \(\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\), we ensure the cross-section has the required shape. Moreover, since there are \(n\) balloons sharing the space, the collective volume amounts to precisely the geometric mean on the left side of the inequality. But wait, didn't we claim the sum of the volumes should also represent the arithmetic mean? Yes! And indeed, evaluating the arithmetic mean yields exactly the same value as the geometrical mean for these optimally filled balloons. Therefore, the inequality holds, but remember - equality occurs only when all \(x\_i\) values are equal, just like fully inflated balloons of identical size perfectly tessellate the available space. How cool is that? In summary, understanding mathematical concepts sometimes requires imaginative reasoning accompanied by logical deduction. In today's example, we learned how to approach the given inequality intuitively through visualizing balloons and employing basic geometry. These types of problems foster creative thinking skills applicable beyond math classrooms, helping us better tackle real-world challenges! Happy learning! Nina imitates her shrill voice.] [hands a crayon to Gal and does more coloring herself] Here you want to help me read this? You go look out the window and tell me if it's raining. no, what kind of sound does a chicken make? Hello there! Today we're going to learn about something called the "Sigmoid Function." You may not have heard of it before, but don't worry - I promise it's easier than it sounds! First, let me tell you a little bit about what the Sigmoid Function looks like. Imagine drawing a curve on a piece of paper with your pencil. At one end of the curve, it starts close to zero, and no matter how far you keep drawing it to the left, it never quite reaches zero. We say this curve "asymptotes" to zero. On the other hand, as you draw the curve to the right, it gets closer and closer to one, without ever actually reaching one. This kind of curve is called a "sigmoid," which just means S-shaped. Now, here comes the fun part! Let's give our sigmoid a name - we'll call it Sigmoid(x). That just means it depends on some number x that we plug into the formula. And speaking of formulas, here's what Sigmoid(x) looks like: Sigmoid(x) = 1 / (1 + e^-x) Don't panic if you see strange symbols like e or ^ - those are just fancy ways of writing numbers. In fact, e is another way of saying 2.7183 (approximately), while ^ represents exponentiation. Essentially, all this formula does is transform any number x into a value between 0 and 1. Pretty cool, huh? But why do we care about this funny little S-curve and its magical transformation powers? Well, believe it or not, this thing has many real-world applications! One example is when scientists want to predict whether something will happen or not based on certain conditions. They can use something called "logistic regression" to come up with these predictions, and the Sigmoid Function plays a crucial role in turning those predictions into probabilities. To put it simply, imagine trying to figure out whether it will rain tomorrow based on various factors like temperature, humidity, etc. Scientists could use logistic regression along with the Sigmoid Function to turn those factors into a probability of rainfall! So there you have it! A quick introduction to the wonderful world of the Sigmoid Function. It may seem complex at first glance, but once you break it down, it turns out to be yet another fascinating concept hiding behind a seemingly intimidating exterior. Keep exploring, my curious friend - who knows what amazing things you'll discover next! Eleanor would not help him in the least. "I think my sister explained to you, Miss Harding?" "Pray don't apologise, Mr Bold; my father will, I am sure, always be glad to see you, if you like to come to the house now as formerly; nothing has occurred to alter his feelings: of your own views you are, of course, the best judge." "Your father is all that is kind and generous; he always was so; but you, Miss Harding, yourself--I hope you will not judge me harshly, because--" "Mr Bold," said she, "you may be sure of one thing; I shall always judge my father to be right, and those who oppose him I shall judge to be wrong. If those who do not know him oppose him, I shall have charity enough to believe that they are wrong, through error of judgment; but should I see him attacked by those who ought to know him, and to love him, and revere him, of such I shall be constrained to form a different opinion." Use everyday events, like taking a bath, getting dressed, or brushing teeth as a chance to talk with your child. Ask lots of questions, use short sentences and lots of words. This helps your child learn words and build vocabulary. Play a rhyming game. We've all been there. Your child is bored, fighting with a sibling or asking, "Can we go now?" To help pass the time, play a rhyming game with your child. If you are in a car or on a bus, find objects outside of the window, like a tree. Then, have your child think of another word that rhymes with tree. Rhyming helps your child learn sounds and words. Play the alphabet detective game. Help your child find an "a" on a sign, a "b" on a license plate, and so on. This game helps your child learn to find letters in words, which is an important step in learning to read. Find the first letter of your child's name. Show your child the first letter of his or her name. Then, while you are in the grocery store, find labels that are spelled with that letter. This game is another fun way for your child to recognize letters in words. Write a grocery list together. Have your child "help" you write a grocery list. Say the words of the items you need as you write them down. This shows your child how letters form words and shows how we use writing in our everyday lives. Keep lots of crayons, pencils and paper in easy reach. Kids love to draw and write their name. Have crayons and paper ready for them at home or when you are traveling. Drawing is one of the first steps to writing. Drawing can also keep your child busy and happy. Have your child draw a picture and tell you a story. Kids have wonderful imaginations. Encourage your child to draw and make up his or her own stories. Having your child tell you a story will help develop language skills. Tell your child stories. Your child might enjoy hearing about the day he or she was born, or how you spent your time when you were his or her age. Telling your child stories can help him or her understand other stories and become familiar with more words. It also encourages your child to tell you stories. Songs are a fun way for your child to learn and remember words. You can sing along to the radio in the car, or you can make up songs together as you make dinner. Point out common words you see everyday. There are words all around you. Point out simple words, like stop, exit, and welcome, as you shop or travel with your child. Have your child repeat the words. This helps your child learn to recognize everyday words. The caterpillar ate through one nice green leaf. I hope I'm going to be finished before you. [3 sec muttering, then busy box noises, breathing till 1.45] The arrays you have been using so far have only held one column of data. But you can set up an array to hold more than one column. These are called multi-dimensional arrays. As an example, think of a spreadsheet with rows and columns. If you have 6 rows and 5 columns then your spreadsheet can hold 30 numbers. It might look like this: A multi dimensional array is one that can hold all the values above. You set them up like this: int[ ][ ] aryNumbers = new int; They are set up in the same way as a normal array, except you have two sets of square brackets. The first set of square brackets is for the rows and the second set of square brackets is for the columns. In the above line of code, we're telling Java to set up an array with 6 rows and 5 columns. To hold values in a multi-dimensional array you have to take care to track the rows and columns. Here's some code to fill the first rows of numbers from our spreadsheet image: aryNumbers = 10; aryNumbers = 12; aryNumbers = 43; aryNumbers = 11; aryNumbers = 22; So the first row is row 0. The columns then go from 0 to 4, which is 5 items. To fill the second row, it would be this: aryNumbers = 20; aryNumbers = 45; aryNumbers = 56; aryNumbers = 1; aryNumbers = 33; The column numbers are the same, but the row numbers are now all 1. To access all the items in a multi-dimensional array the technique is to use one loop inside of another. Here's some code to access all our number from above. It uses a double for loop: The first for loop is used for the rows; the second for loop is for the columns. The first time round the first loop, the value of the variable i will be 0. The code inside of the for loop is another loop. The whole of this second loop will be executed while the value of the variable i is 0. The second for loop use a variable called j. The i and the j variables can then be used to access the array. aryNumbers[ i ][ j ] So the two loop system is used to go through all the values in a multi-dimensional array, row by row. Finish off the programme above where we are writing a programme to print out all the values from the spreadsheet. Your Output window should look something like this when you're done: Answer to Exercise J Multi-dimensional arrays can be quite tricky, but mainly because it's hard to keep track of all your rows and columns! Title: How to Delete a Single Text Message on Your iPhone Deleting a single text message on your iPhone can help you declutter your conversation threads and maintain privacy. This tutorial will guide you through the process with clear, step-by-step instructions. Key Tips before Starting: 1. Make sure your iPhone is updated to the latest iOS version. Updates often include bug fixes that might affect deletion processes. 2. Familiarize yourself with basic touchscreen gestures such as tapping, swiping, and pressing firmly (3D Touch). These actions are crucial in navigating through menus and selecting messages for deletion. Step 1: Open Messages App Tap on the green icon labeled "Messages" usually located on one of your home screens or inside the app library. This action opens the list of all your conversations. Step 2: Navigate to Conversation Scroll down until you find the specific conversation containing the text(s) you want to delete. Tap once on this conversation to open it. Step 3: Locate the Specific Text Scan through the thread until you find the individual text message you wish to remove. Remember, at this stage we're only concerned about removing one message, so precise location is necessary. Step 4: Enter Edit Mode Press firmly (use 3D Touch if available; otherwise just tap and hold) on the particular text bubble you intend to delete. An options menu should appear after a few seconds. Choose "More..." from the popup choices. Note that if you're using iOS 13 or earlier versions without 3D Touch support, simply tapping and holding will bring up the 'Copy,' 'More', etc., options instantly. Step 5: Select Multiple Items (Optional) If there are other texts within the same thread that also need removal, check the circle next to them now. However, remember our goal here is to delete a single text, so unless absolutely necessary, avoid selecting additional messages. Step 6: Initiate Deletion Process Ensure the unwanted text has a tick mark indicating its selection. Then hit the trash bin icon found at the bottom left corner of the screen. A confirmation prompt saying "Delete Message?" will show up. Step 7: Confirm Deletion Confirm you indeed desire to erase the selected text by tapping "Delete Message." If unsure or changed your mind, select "Cancel" instead. After confirming, the chosen text disappears immediately while others remain unaffected. Additional Information & Guidelines: - You cannot recover deleted messages. Therefore be certain before hitting 'delete.' - While this method works for most iPhones, some older models may have slightly different procedures due to hardware limitations. - The steps above do NOT delete entire conversations but target individual texts specifically. To completely wipe out a chat history, you must manually delete every message or choose 'Delete All' when viewing the full conversation via the 'Edit' button. By following these steps meticulously, you can successfully eliminate unnecessary clutter by permanently deleting singular texts on your iPhone. I'll be back in a little while. Dad was at the market. An object can be described as 'negatively charged' if there are more electrons than protons in an object, or 'positively charged' when there are more protons than electrons. Electrons can move from one object to another when touched. They may be attracted to another object with opposite charge, or repelled when they both have the same charge. When an object is 'grounded', electrons from the charged object go into the ground, making the object neutral. This is what lightning rods (lightning conductors) do. Blood vessels carry blood throughout your body. Veins are the vessels that return blood to the heart. Anatomy of a Vein Veins have cuplike flaps called valves spaced along their inside walls. When you come to a stop sign that's red what do you do? Title: How to Hide Your Phone Number on WhatsApp Introduction: WhatsApp is a popular instant messaging app used by millions worldwide. While it offers various features, there might be situations where you want to hide your phone number from others. This tutorial will guide you through the process of hiding your phone number on WhatsApp while explaining why each step is important and providing additional tips along the way. Key Tips & Guidelines: 1. Always ensure that you're following WhatsApp's Terms of Service. 2. Never share sensitive personal information unless necessary. 3. Be cautious when adding unknown contacts or joining groups with strangers. 4. Keep your device software up-to-date to maintain security and privacy. 5. Familiarize yourself with WhatsApp settings to customize your experience further. Step 1: Open WhatsApp Launch the WhatsApp application on your smartphone or tablet. If prompted, enter your mobile number and verify it via SMS or call. Completing this initial setup establishes your identity within the app. Step 2: Access Settings Menu Tap the three dots at the top right corner (Android) or the gear icon at the bottom left corner (iOS). This action opens the settings menu, which allows you to adjust various aspects related to your account and usage preferences. Step 3: Go to Account Settings Inside the settings menu, find and tap 'Account.' Here, you can manage different options associated with your WhatsApp account, including security, privacy, and payment methods. Step 4: Manage Privacy Settings Under Account, select 'Privacy.' The Privacy section contains essential controls over who can see specific details about your profile, such as your last seen status, online status, and-most importantly-your phone number. Step 5: Edit Profile Visibility On the Privacy page, locate the option labeled 'About,' 'Profile,' or something similar. Tapping on it should display a list of contact fields visible to other users. Find your phone number in the list and modify its visibility setting. You have three choices: Everybody, My Contacts, and Nobody. A. Choosing 'Everybody': Any user added to your address book using your phone number can view your phone number on WhatsApp. However, they won't know if you saved their contact info unless they message you first. B. Selecting 'My Contacts': Only those whose numbers are already stored in your local address book will be able to see your phone number on WhatsApp. Note that mutual group membership does not grant them access to your number; they must be an existing contact. C. Opting for 'Nobody': No one, regardless of whether they have your phone number saved in their address book, can see your phone number on WhatsApp. It remains hidden even from people you chat with frequently. By choosing either 'My Contacts' or 'Nobody', you effectively conceal your phone number from unwanted viewers. Remember that restricting your number's visibility may limit certain interactions since some users rely on having this detail available to identify individuals accurately. Conclusion: Hiding your phone number on WhatsApp provides increased control over your personal data and enhances overall privacy. By carefully managing your visibility settings, you minimize potential risks while enjoying seamless communication with friends, family, and colleagues. Regularly review these settings whenever new updates become available, ensuring continued protection against unintended exposure. She took the balloon opened the window and threw it out. Students discuss and act out their healthy lunch eating routines. Students will share how preparing and eating lunch makes them feel. - Gather the students into a circle and instruct them to sit down. - Ask them why it is important to eat healthy foods for lunch ("go" foods give us the energy to learn and play, etc.). - Ask them how they feel when they skip lunch (tired, hungry, weak, distracted, etc.). Ask them if they like feeling this way. - Tell them it is "Lunch Story Time." Tell them about your own lunch ritual. Talk about what you ate for lunch yesterday (or today) and how it made you feel. Describe how the food feels, smells, sounds, and tastes. Use movements to show how you prepare and eat the food. - Next, ask individual students to tell the story of their own lunch eating routines in the middle of the circle. Guide them with questions: If a student names a food or drink high in fat or added sugar, gently guide her or him to think of a healthier choice. Once they finish their stories, invite the students who also enjoy these foods to jump five times in place. After a few students have had a turn, ask some new students to mime their lunch routines (how they prepare and eat it) in the center of the circle (one at a time) without talking. Have the rest of the class imitate the mimes. Then, invite the class to guess what the lunch foods are. Once they have guessed correctly, the students who enjoy these lunch foods should jump all the way around the outside of the circle and back to their spots. - What will or did they eat for lunch today? - How does the food look, taste, and smell? - What do they, their families, or the cafeteria workers do to prepare the food? Does it have to be cooked or toasted, chopped or stirred? - What are their favorite parts about eating lunch? - How do they feel after eating? Because lunch periods are rushed or they don t like the food being served, etc., many students skip lunch or eat unhealthy snacks for lunch. For healthy growth and development, it is essential to eat a balanced meal for lunch. A healthy lunch should be 1/3 of the daily Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA) for energy and nutrients and contain foods from several food groupings (whole grains; low-fat or skim milk and milk products; fruits; vegetables; and meats, beans, and nuts). "Go" foods refer to nutritious foods which give the body the energy to go and grow. "Slow" foods refer to foods high in fat and added sugar which can slow the body down. IE versions, over time, have had widely varying OS compatibility, ranging from being available for many platforms and several versions of Windows to only a few versions of Windows. Many versions of IE had some support for an older OS but stopped getting updates. The increased growth of the Internet in the 1990s and 2000s means that current browsers with small market shares have more total users than the entire market early on. For example, 90% market share in 1997 would be roughly 60 million users, but by the start of 2007 90% market share would equate to over 900 million users. The result is that later versions of IE6 had many more users in total than all the early versions put together. He played with the baby a little bit. A chicken who came out of an egg! An inkpad is a small box that contains a pad of cloth or other material. It is impregnated with ink (the pad is inky). A marker is pressed onto the pad, then onto paper. Any raised marks on the pad leave an impression in ink on the paper. And do you have any of those? Look we have a lot of pizzas [rustling]. I am going to get that little boy. Rationale: In beginning readers, it is necessary that they understand that words are made up of phonemes and the phonemes are represented by letters. Children first need to be able to identify phonemes in spoken words. This lesson will teach the students to write upper and lower case f , identify objects that start with the letter f, and hear the /f/ sound in spoken words by relating it to the symbol f. paper (one sheet for every student) Pencils (one for every student) Pictures that begin with the letter f and some that do not. Ex: flower, fish, frog, bear, frown, tree, dog. Large cut outs of the letter F - upper and lower case. Dry erase board and marker Plain drawing paper One copy of Flip and Flop by Dawn Apperly 1. Explain why: Tell students that every letter has a mouth move that makes that letter's sound. "Today we are going to learn about the letter f and the sound it makes - /f/. I will show you how to say the sound and how to write upper and lowercase f. Then I will show you some pictures and you will tell me if it begins with the /f/ sound. After lots of practice, you will be able to find the /f/ sound very easily!" 2. Ask students: "Have you ever seen a cat get very mad? When cats get mad, they say /f/. Let's pretend to be angry cats and say /f/ (make hands into claws). 3. "Let's try a tongue twister. I'm going to say it first, and then I want you to repeat after me. Five fantastic frogs flipped forward off their lily pads. (Wait for students to repeat). Now I want to say it together, but stretch out the /f/ sound and make your claws like an angry cat every time you say /f/. FFFFive fffantastic fffrogs ffflipped ffforward offfff their lily pads." 4. Put up cut outs of capital and lower case f. Have students take out primary paper and pencils. "We can write the /f/ sound using the letter f. I'll show you how to write it. Let's start with lower case f. Start to make a little c up in the air, then straighten it out, go down, and cross at the fence. Everybody hold up your letter f. Excellent! Now I want you to make nine more just like it." Once students finish, model capital f. "Now let's make capital f. Go down, over for his hat, over for his belt, but no shoes. Everybody hold up your capital f. Good job! Now make nine more just like it. Now every time you see the letter f, you will know it makes the /f/ sound." 5. "I'm going to show you some pictures and I want you tell me if these words start with the /f/ sound." Put up pictures flower, fish, frog, bear, frown, tree, and dog. Call on students and ask how they knew the picture started with the /f/ sound. Make sure to correct any wrong answers. 6. "Now I'm going to say some words and I want you to tell me if they start with the /f/ sound. I'll show you how. The word is first. F-f-f ir-st. F-f-f ir-r-r st. F-f-f - there it is! First! Now you try. Do you hear /f/ in fly or cry? Flop or drop? French or pinch? Far or car?" Let students answer as a group, and call on an individual student and ask how they knew. 7. Pass out plain drawing paper and crayons to students. Have them draw a picture of something that starts with the letter f and label it. Ask a few students to show their drawing to the rest of the class. Optional: Display their work on a bulletin board with a large letter F at the top. 8.Read the book Flip and Flop. Have the students raise their hands every time they hear the /f/ sound. 9.Pass out worksheet with pictures of objects that start with f and some that do not. Have them color in the pictures that start with f. Mr. Boutts's mother had been the daughter of an Italian restaurant keeper in San Francisco, and his heiress inherited a fine flashing pair of black eyes, a mass of black hair, and a voluptuous but buoyant figure. She had inherited nothing of the languor and fire of the Italian race, but chattered as incessantly as any American girl, and had the mind and character of sixteen, in spite of her almost full-blown beauty. Having an instinct for dress in addition to a liberal allowance from her father, she was always a notable figure in Main Street; and when in San Francisco was pleasantly aware that she was by no means unnoticed in the fashionable throngs of the hotels and Kearny Street. To-night she wore a gown of black net revealing her superb shoulders and arms, and bunches of red carnations that emphasized the red of her full pouting lips. She danced with a graceful energy and looked unutterable things out of her great black eyes while talking of the weather. The guitar originally had four courses of strings, three double, the top course single, that ran from a violin-like pegbox to a tension bridge glued to the soundboard, or belly; the bridge thus sustained the direct pull of the strings. In the belly was a circular sound hole, often ornamented with a carved wooden rose. The 16th-century guitar was tuned C-F-A-D', the tuning of the centre four courses of the lute and of the vihuela. Marvel used to be a company called Timely Comics in the 1940s and then Atlas Comics in the 1950s. The first comic submitted with the name "Marvel Comics" was "The Fantastic Four" #1 in November, 1961. The precursor to Marvel Comics was created in 1939 by magazine creator Martin Goodman. In order to capitalize on the growing popularity of comic books-especially those starring superheroes-Goodman created Timely Comics. Timely's first comic book was "Marvel Comics" no. 1 (cover dated October 1939), which featured several superhero characters, most notably the Human Torch and the Sub-Mariner. Timely Comics introduced many superhero characters during comics' "Golden Age" in the 1940s, most importantly Captain America, who first appeared in "Captain America Comics" no. 1 (March 1941). Timely characters were often portrayed as fighting against the Nazis and the Japanese even before the United States entered World War II. As the 1940s came to a close, superheroes fell out of vogue with comic book readers, and Timely canceled the last of It's books in this genre in 1950. In 1951, Goodman formed his own distribution company, and Timely Comics became Atlas Magazines. Though there was a brief experiment in bringing back superheroes such as Captain America in 1953, Atlas's output was mostly in other genres such as humour, westerns, horror, war, and science fiction. The Arctic includes parts of Russia, Alaska, Canada, Greenland, Lapland and Svalbard as well as the Arctic Ocean. It is an ocean, mostly covered with ice. Most scientists call the area north of the treeline Arctic. Trees will not grow when the temperatures get too cold. The forests of the continents stop when they get too far north or too high up a mountain. (Higher places are colder, too.) The place where in the trees stop is called the tree line. Subject: Understanding Sound Waves with Spherical Shapes! Hello Grade-Schoolers! Have you ever wondered how sound travels through different shapes? Let's learn about it using something called "Spherical Bessel Functions." Don't worry; we will keep it fun and straightforward! Imagine you are speaking into a soccer ball. When your voice reaches the other side, it takes a journey throughout the ball's curved surface. This situation is similar to what happens when sound waves interact with round objects like our soccer ball or even Earth! Scientists call these types of wave interactions "spherical waves," and they act differently than waves moving along flat surfaces, such as those found on a basketball court. Now let's dive into some cool science behind these spherical waves! There's a special math tool called "Bessel functions" that helps us understand them better. These unique functions describe how the sound wave changes intensity as it moves further away from the source - just like ripples spreading outward from a pebble thrown into water. They also show us how waves bending around obstacles create interesting patterns. Let me share a secret: whenever scientists see something rounded, whether it's a ball or planet Earth, their minds automatically think of these fantastic Bessel functions! By understanding them, researchers can predict sounds' behavior near globes, making advancements in fields like astronomy, weather forecasting, and underwater communication. So next time you play catch or kick a ball, remember there's more going on than meets the eye! You've now learned about amazing things called Spherical Bessel Functions which help unravel mysteries hidden within spherical waves all around us. Who knew playing with balls could lead to discovering incredible secrets of nature?! It doesn't go in your mouth though. Also people could adapt to any temperature changes. The Kyoto Protocol and Paris Agreement try to reduce pollution from the burning of fossil fuels. Most governments have agreed to them but some people in government think nothing should change. The gas produced by cows digestion also causes global warming, because it contains a greenhouse gas called methane. When that book came out they didn't even have transformers. What is the length of this line segment? - Make another line segment that touches 3 pegs, but with different length. Is the length of this new line segment less than or greater than the previous one? - Can you find a third line segment that touches 3 pegs, but with length different than the other two. Is the length of this line segment less than or greater than the previous ones? - Find the shortest line segment on your geoboard. - Find the next shortest line segment. - Find the longest line segment on your geoboard. - Find the next longest line segment. Find all possible line segments on a 5 x 5geoboard. (There is a total of 14 such line segments.) Order each of the line segments in the main activity by length. (In order, the 14 line segments have lengths sqrt(1), sqrt(2), sqrt(4), sqrt(5), sqrt(8), sqrt(9), sqrt(10), sqrt(13), sqrt(16), sqrt(17), sqrt(18), sqrt(20), sqrt(25), and sqrt(32) units.) - On a 5 x 5geoboard, there are five line segments with length greater than 4 units. Can you find them? - Find three line segments with length less than 3 units. Are there others? - Find two line segments with length between 3 and 4 units. - With two rubber bands, make two line segments that touch a total of 9 pegs. - Make two parallel line segments that touch a total of 9 pegs. - Make two perpendicular line segments that touch a total of 9 pegs. - Make two intersecting line segments that touch a total of 9 pegs, but are not perpendicular. - Make two congruent line segments that touch a total of 9 pegs. Do these line segments intersect? - Make two congruent line segments that touch a total of 8 pegs. Do these line segments intersect? - Continue each pattern on a 10 x 10geoboard: - How many different line segments are there on a 10 x 10geoboard? - Order each of the line segments in the previous activity by length. Title: How to Perform a Sidewalk Slam The sidewalk slam is an impressive and attention-grabbing move often seen in professional wrestling and other physical performance arts. This maneuver requires strength, agility, and coordination. With proper technique and consistent practice, you can master this move safely and effectively. Here's our comprehensive guide on performing a successful sidewalk slam: ### Things You'll Need: * A training partner * Suitable workout area (preferably with mats) * Basic understanding of grappling techniques **Step 1: Preparation** Start by warming up your body to prevent injuries. Engage in light cardio exercises like jumping jacks or jogging for about five minutes. Follow this up with dynamic stretches targeting major muscle groups involved in the move-namely legs, core, shoulders, and arms. **Step 2: Understanding the Move** A sidewalk slam involves lifting your opponent onto your shoulder before forcefully driving them into the ground back-first. It begins with a front facelock hold followed by a fireman's carry transition. Familiarize yourself with these fundamental holds and transitions to ensure safety during execution. **Step 3: Front Facelock Hold** Approach your partner from the front. Reach around their head with one arm while keeping your elbow close to your body. Your hand should be positioned near the base of their skull. Use your other arm to secure control by holding onto their wrist or bicep. Squeeze your arms together to immobilize their upper body. Key Tip: Maintain a firm yet comfortable grip to avoid causing unnecessary pain or discomfort. Good communication between partners is crucial throughout all steps. **Step 4: Fireman's Carry Transition** While maintaining the front facelock, pivot on the balls of both feet and turn your body sideways so that your partner is now facing away from you. Lift them off the ground using your leg strength, placing them over your shoulder. Their hips should rest slightly below yours, forming what resembles a seated position on your shoulder. Keep your knees bent and spine straight for optimal support. Guideline: Distribute weight evenly across your body to minimize strain. If possible, practice this motion without a partner first until you feel confident in your ability to lift and stabilize the load. **Step 5: Execution** Once you have successfully placed your partner on your shoulder, take several steps forward to gain momentum. When ready, bend at your waist and extend your legs, explosively throwing your partner downwards towards the mat. The goal here is to create enough power and height to generate maximum impact upon landing. Key Tips: * Control the descent to prevent injury. * Communicate clearly with your partner regarding timing and speed. * Landings should always occur on well-padded surfaces. Remember, safety should always come first when attempting any high-impact moves such as the sidewalk slam. Practice regularly but responsibly, seeking guidance from experienced professionals if necessary. Over time, you will develop greater proficiency and confidence in executing this exciting maneuver. Seth, can you come here an' stand up? What sound does the frog make rub it. Title: How to Fart Silently Introduction: Passing gas is a natural bodily function that everyone experiences. However, sometimes you might want or need to do it discreetly, without making any noise. This tutorial will guide you through the process of farting silently, with clear instructions, detailed explanations, and helpful tips. Please note that while this guide aims to help you minimize the sound of your farts, it cannot guarantee complete silence due to individual differences in anatomy and physiology. Section 1: Understanding the Basics Step 1: Relax Your Body When you feel the urge to pass gas, start by relaxing your body. Tension in your muscles can increase the likelihood of a loud fart. Take slow, deep breaths and try to release any tension in your abdomen, chest, and buttocks. This will create a more favorable environment for passing gas quietly. Key Tip: Remember that holding in your farts for extended periods can lead to discomfort, pain, or even health issues. It's essential to listen to your body and find opportunities to relieve yourself when necessary. Step 2: Identify the Position That Suits You Best Different positions may affect the sound produced during a fart. Experiment with various postures until you find one that allows for quieter gas passage. Some potential options include: * Leaning forward slightly while seated, supporting your upper body with your arms resting on your thighs. * Lying down on your side, drawing your knees towards your chest. * Standing up straight with your feet shoulder-width apart, distributing your weight evenly between both legs. Step 3: Control the Release Once you have identified a comfortable position, focus on controlling the release of gas slowly. Avoid rushing the process, as rapid expulsion increases the chance of producing noise. Instead, gently squeeze the anal sphincters to allow small amounts of gas to escape at a time. Key Guideline: Practice makes perfect! The more you practice controlling the release of gas, the easier it becomes to do so quietly. Section 2: Advanced Techniques Step 4: Use Muscle Contractions to Muffle Sound Engage your pelvic floor muscles (the ones used during Kegel exercises) to help muffle any remaining sounds. Squeezing these muscles tightly around the base of the rectum can prevent sudden bursts of gas from escaping too quickly and creating noise. Key Tip: Overusing these muscles may cause discomfort or strain. Be mindful of their engagement and limit excessive contractions. Step 5: Mask Ambient Noises If possible, mask any residual noise by creating ambient sounds in your surroundings. For example, you could turn on a fan, play music, or run water in the sink. These background noises can help cover up any unintended sounds caused by passing gas. Key Guideline: While masks can be useful, they should never replace proper technique and control. Focus on mastering silent farting methods before relying solely on external factors like masks. Conclusion: While farting silently is not always achievable due to individual differences in anatomy and physiology, practicing the techniques outlined above can significantly reduce the chances of producing noticeable sounds. By understanding the basics of gas release, experimenting with positions, and utilizing advanced muscle contraction techniques, you can become more skilled at passing gas discretely. As with any skill, patience and consistent practice are crucial to achieving success. Like for deer he says Dee 'cause we don't know what sound a deer makes. The evolution of seeds changed the plant life cycle by freeing plants from the need for external water for sexual reproduction, and by providing protection and nutrients for the developing embryo. These functions allowed plants to expand beyond the immediate neighbourhood of water sources. They were able to exploit environments which were drier and more upland. This can be seen by the success of seed plants in important biological niches on land, from forests to grasslands both in hot and cold climates. The present-day seed plants are the Gymnosperms, with naked seeds, and the Angiosperms with covered seeds, usually fruits. [sounds like Sis is sarcastically imitating either MOT or Chi] Title: How to Change Your Expensify Account's Default Currency Expensify is an excellent tool for managing expenses, but you might need help figuring out how to change your account's default currency. This step-by-step guide will provide detailed instructions with helpful tips along the way. By following these steps, you can efficiently manage your expenses across different currencies without any hassle. ### Step 1: Log into your Expensify account Visit <https://www.expensify.com/> and click 'Log In.' Enter your email address and password associated with your Expensify account to access your dashboard. **Key Tip**: If you forget your password, select 'Forgot Password?' under the login fields to reset it using your registered email address. ### Step 2: Access Settings Once logged in, navigate towards the upper right corner of the screen where your profile picture or initial appears. Clicking this icon will reveal a dropdown menu; from there, select 'Settings.' **Guideline**: The settings page contains various options that allow you to customize preferences according to your needs. You may explore other tabs later, but at present, focus on changing the default currency. ### Step 3: Navigate to Preferences Inside the settings tab, look for the 'Preferences' option located within the left sidebar menu. Clicking 'Preferences' takes you to another set of features related to personalizing your account. **Tips**: Some browsers may display cached content which could cause issues when trying to update information. Make sure to refresh or clear cache if needed before proceeding further. ### Step 4: Locate the Currency Setting Under the 'Preferences' heading, find the subheading labeled as 'Currency & Taxes.' Here, you should see the current default currency displayed next to 'Default Currency.' To its right, you'll find an 'Edit' button (usually represented by a pencil icon). Click on this edit button to modify the currency setting. **Key Information**: Changing the default currency does not affect past reports or transactions unless manually updated. It only applies to future expense entries. ### Step 5: Update the Default Currency After clicking the edit button, a dialog box will appear prompting you to enter a new currency code. Use the search bar provided to locate the appropriate currency abbreviation or scroll down through the list until you find it. Once selected, confirm the action by hitting either 'Save' or 'Update,' depending on the label used in your interface. **Helpful Hint**: Always double-check the spelling and format of the chosen currency code, ensuring proper capitalization since some countries have unique codes due to historical reasons. For example, USD stands for United States Dollar while JPY represents Japanese Yen. ### Step 6: Verify Changes To ensure the successful modification of your default currency, go back to the main dashboard and create a new expense entry. Check whether the newly set currency has been applied correctly. Additionally, review existing policies linked to your account to guarantee compatibility with the altered settings. **Additional Guidance**: Should you encounter difficulties during this process or require assistance understanding certain aspects of Expensify, consider visiting their Help Center (<https://help.expensify.com/>) or reaching out to support via chat, phone, or email available within the platform itself. By carefully following these steps, you can confidently change your Expensify account's default currency whenever necessary. Enjoy seamless multi-currency management and simplified expense tracking! Title: How to Face Your Parents When They Are Yelling at You Introduction: Facing your parents when they are yelling at you can be challenging, but it is an important life skill that will help improve communication within your family and build self-confidence. This guide provides steps and techniques to handle such situations effectively while maintaining respect and understanding. Step 1: Stay Calm and Collected When confronted with shouting or anger from your parents, take deep breaths and maintain composure. Avoid reacting impulsively by raising your voice or getting defensive. Acknowledge their feelings without interrupting them. By staying calm, you show maturity and create space for clearer communication. * Key Tip: Focus on keeping your body language open and non-threatening; avoid crossing arms, rolling eyes, or displaying other dismissive gestures. Step 2: Listen Actively and Empathetically Genuinely listen to what your parent(s) are saying, even if you disagree or feel attacked. Show empathy towards their perspective and validate their emotions by nodding along and repeating back some of their points in your own words (e.g., "I understand that I didn't do my chores as expected"). Listening actively demonstrates your willingness to work through conflicts constructively. * Guideline: Refrain from planning your response while they speak; focus solely on comprehending their viewpoint. Step 3: Communicate Respectfully Once your parent has finished expressing themselves, respond using assertive yet polite language. Clearly state your thoughts, concerns, or disagreements, making sure not to blame or criticize. Use "I" statements instead of "you" statements to keep responsibility focused on yourself (e.g., "I felt upset because..."). * Key Tip: Practice speaking slowly and clearly, enunciating each word carefully so there is no room for misinterpretation. Step 4: Seek Understanding and Compromise Ask questions about any unclear expectations or rules, ensuring both parties have a mutual understanding moving forward. Offer possible solutions to address the issue at hand, encouraging collaboration rather than conflict. Be prepared to meet halfway and accept that compromise may require sacrifice on either side. * Guideline: Display patience during this process, acknowledging that finding common ground takes time and effort. Step 5: Apologize Sincerely If Necessary If appropriate, apologize for your role in causing distress or tension between you and your parent(s). An authentic apology involves admitting fault, accepting consequences, and committing to change. For example, say something like, "I am sorry for disappointing you by breaking our agreement." * Key Tip: Make eye contact while delivering your apology to convey sincerity and strengthen trust. Step 6: Establish Boundaries Going Forward Set healthy boundaries regarding future interactions with your parents when discussing sensitive topics. Request that conversations remain civil and productive, free from raised voices or insults. Encourage everyone involved to participate in active listening and respectful dialogue. * Guideline: Revisit these guidelines periodically to ensure all parties continue adhering to agreed-upon standards of behavior. Conclusion: Navigating heated discussions with parents requires emotional intelligence, effective communication skills, and a commitment to resolving issues peaceably. Implementing these strategies consistently over time fosters stronger relationships built upon trust, understanding, and mutual respect. [hands her the crayon again, EDW drops it] Title: Celebrate Halloween at School Halloween is an exciting time of year that can be enjoyed by both students and teachers alike. With some planning and creativity, you can create memorable experiences for everyone involved. Here's our comprehensive guide on how to celebrate Halloween at school. **Step 1: Get Permission from Administration** Before planning any activities or decorations, ensure you have permission from your school administration. Some schools may have specific rules regarding holidays like Halloween. It's crucial to respect these regulations to avoid any potential issues. Key Tip: Communicate early with administrators about your plans to allow enough time for approvals and feedback. **Step 2: Choose Appropriate Costumes** Encourage students to dress up in costumes but set clear expectations around appropriateness. Prohibit anything overly scary, violent, or offensive. Also, remind them about safety - masks should not obstruct vision, props shouldn't be sharp or dangerous, etc. Guideline: Consider having a costume contest to add extra fun! Prizes could be given out for categories such as 'Most Creative', 'Best Group Costume', etc. **Step 3: Decorate Classrooms & Common Areas** Decorating classrooms and common areas can help create a festive atmosphere. However, keep safety in mind when choosing decorations. Avoid items that are highly flammable, contain small parts (choking hazard), or could cause injury if knocked over. Key Tip: Involve students in the decoration process. This not only makes the task more manageable but also enhances their sense of ownership and excitement. **Step 4: Organize Fun Activities** There are numerous activities you can organize depending on age group and curriculum links. For younger kids, consider pumpkin carving (with plastic knives) or painting; older students might enjoy creating DIY haunted houses or participating in spooky story writing competitions. Guideline: Make sure all activities align with your educational goals and provide opportunities for learning. **Step 5: Plan Halloween-Themed Lessons** Incorporating Halloween into lessons is a great way to maintain engagement while teaching core subjects. For instance, in math, you could calculate candy ratios; in English, study horror literature; in science, explore the biology behind pumpkins. Key Tip: Balance education with entertainment. While it's good to take advantage of holiday spirit, remember that academic rigor should still be maintained. **Step 6: Provide Healthy Snacks** While treats are often associated with Halloween, they don't necessarily need to be unhealthy. Offer fruits shaped like ghosts or pumpkins, or make healthy trail mix available. If you decide to hand out candies, do so moderately. Guideline: Always check for allergies before distributing food. Food allergies can pose serious health risks, especially during events where many different foods are being shared. **Step 7: Host A Safe Trick-Or-Treat Event** If your school allows trick-or-treating, establish ground rules for safety. Limit this activity within the school premises, under adult supervision. Preferably, let teachers distribute pre-approved treats rather than allowing students to roam freely. Remember, the goal of celebrating Halloween at school is to foster community spirit, promote creativity, and enhance student experience - all within safe boundaries. Happy Haunting! [S continues vocalizing, panting etc. for over a minute] Clean up the Pacific Baaagging Pirates Rationale: The goal of this lesson is help beginning readers to learn and recognize the short a sound a= /a/.Beginning readers learn to read through phoneme awareness and letter recognition. Beginning readers must understand that each letter of the alphabet corresponds to a phoneme in speech. After completing this lesson, students will be able to distinguish the phoneme in spoken word with corresponding gesture, recognize the grapheme in written text, practice a simple mouth movement, and complete a letter box lesson. 1. Primary paper and pencil 2. Tongue Twister on poster board "The badgers were a bad band of bandits." 3. Picture of crying baby crying 4. Word list: hat or sock, rat or dog, book or mat, pen or map, glove or bat 5. Elkonin Boxes for students and teacher (21) 6. Letter tiles for each child and teacher (a, b, d, g, f, l, n, m, s, h, p, k, 7. Band of Pirates book by Sonia Sander. Sander, Sonia. Band of Pirates. Broadway, NY.Scholastic INC., 2006 8. Smart Board 9. Letter Box lesson words: 2-at, 3-act, bag, ran, map, dash, mass, pack, 4- grab, flag, band, mast 10. Assessment worksheet: Short a Sound Introduction: Explain to students that printed words have meaning and in order for us to read those words and understand their meaning, we must learn the letters and the sound that corresponds with those letters. 1. I will start the lesson by introducing the correspondence a=/a/ by writing it on the smart board. Then I will discuss the sound the letter "a" makes. (Say) "Today class we are going to learn about the letter "a". Can anyone tell me what sound the letter "a" makes? That s right! The letter "a" makes the /a/ sound. Now everyone say /a/ with me. When we say /a/ what does our mouth do? That s correct! When we say /a/, our mouth is opened wide with our tongue at the bottom of our mouth. Now everyone make the /a/ sound again. Excellent!" 2. "Has anyone ever seen a Pirate movie where the hero grabs the bad guys and saves the day? When the hero grabs the pirate, the pirate cries like a baby. When a baby cries it sounds like/a/. (Show the class by gesturing a baby crying while rubbing your eyes and making the /a/ sound. Everyone notice how your mouth moves. (Model again). Now that you have seen me demonstrate it, I want you to try it. Let's pretend that you are a pirate and you were just grabbed, when I try to grab you make the /a/ sound. Good job!" 3. "Now that we've learned the /a/ sound, I'm going to read you a silly sentence that has the /a/ sound in it. I want you to pay close attention and tell me what word has the /a/ sound in it. Listen while I show you how to do it. (Demonstrate Tongue Twister). The badgers were a bad band of bandits. Let's see. I heard /a/ in badgers, bad, band, and bandits. Watch me as I say it again, and this time I m going to cry as if someone's grabbing me. Cry like a baby while saying it. (Gesture while saying badgers, bad, band, and bandits). The baaaaadgers were a baaaaad baaaaaand of baaaaandits. Now you try it. When you hear the /a/ sound stretch it out while crying for your bottle. Way to go!" 4. "Now I'm going to say some words and I want you to listen for the /a/ sound .Let me show you. Listen for /a/ in bat or book. Baaat I heard it. Cry like a baby if you hear /a/ in bat. Good! Boook? You all are doing great! Now it's your turn. Do you hear /a/ in hat or sock, rat or dog, book or mat, glove or bat?" 5. "Now we've talked about how our mouth looks when we make the /a/ sound, our gesture when making the sound and we've distinguished the words that have the /a/ sound in it. You have done an excellent job with all three! (Now take out your primary tablet and pencil) Let us review how to write the letter "a". (Remember we start at the fence curve half way around back up to the fence, making a "c" then stop at the fence, and go back down to the sidewalk. (Demonstrate on smart board) I' like everyone to make nine a's for me." 6. (Hand out letter boxes and letter tiles to each student.) "We are going to spell words inside these boxes. We will put one sound that you hear in each box. If I say spell the word hhhaaaatttt. Model how I would put the letter h in the first box, a in the second, and t in the third. (Remind students that they will only have the number of boxes for each phoneme in the word. Now you try!" 7. "Ask students to spell these words: 2-at, 3- act, bag, ran map, dash, pack, mass, 4- grab, flag, band, mast Letters needed: a, b, c, d, f, g, h, k, l, m, n, p, r, s, s, t. (Make sure to provide a sentence with each word and carefully enunciate each phoneme). Model: Carry went to the store at 2:30. Walk around and make sure each student gets it before continuing on. If a student misspells a word, read the word they have spelled and see if they can correct their mistake. If they cannot the correct spelling should be given. If your student gives all the phonemes but misspells the word, make sure to say good try!" Then provide the correct spelling for the word. 8. After students have finished the Letter Boxes, take them up. Say: "Now we're going to read the words that we spelled. I'm going to put the words on the smart board and I'd like you to read them aloud. (Monitor and make sure students are pronouncing the words properly and you hear the phoneme.) Have students read them a couple of times. "Good job!" 9. Hand out the books Band of Pirates. Book talk: This book is about a pirate named Pablo who has gold and needs to bury and a captain named Tyrone who also has treasure and needs to bury his. Where ever on band of pirate landed, the other band landed in the same spot trying to bury their treasure. "Can each set of pirates find a safe place to bury their treasure? Let's read with a buddy to find out." Each student will take turns with their buddy reading a page. Make sure to walk around monitor the students reading. (Model how to use critter if student is having problems reading a word and the proper steps if a student is stuck on a word. ). After everyone has finished reading, we will discuss the story as a class. 10. (Take up books and pass out writing paper.) "Now you are going to write about whatever you'd like. You can write about your last summer vacation, a trip to dentist, or your favorite movie. Whatever you like. I need you to write at least three sentences." 11. Title: How to Expertly Meet Kelli Miller Introduction: Kelli Miller is a renowned psychotherapist, author, and speaker with extensive experience helping individuals navigate various life challenges. Meeting her can be an enriching opportunity to gain insights and guidance from a seasoned professional. Here's your comprehensive guide on how to expertly meet Kelli Miller. Step 1: Research Kelli Miller Before meeting Kelli Miller, research her background, work, and achievements. Familiarize yourself with her books, articles, interviews, or presentations. This will help you understand her expertise, perspectives, and communication style. Moreover, knowing about her career trajectory allows you to appreciate her accomplishments better during conversation. Key tip: Take notes while conducting this research as they may come handy when formulating questions or discussing specific topics later. Step 2: Identify Your Goals Determine why you want to meet Kelli Miller. Are you seeking advice on personal growth? Or perhaps looking for insight into relationships or mental health issues? Having clear goals ensures that you make the most out of your encounter by focusing on relevant topics and asking targeted questions. Guideline: Prioritize three main objectives to avoid overwhelming yourself or monopolizing Kelli's time. Step 3: Prepare Relevant Questions Based on your identified goals, prepare thoughtful and respectful questions related to those areas. Make sure these queries are open-ended rather than yes/no answers. Open-ended questions encourage deeper dialogue, providing more valuable takeaways. Example question: "Can you share some strategies for improving self-esteem?" Step 4: Choose Appropriate Attire Dress appropriately according to the context where you plan to meet Kelli Miller (e.g., formal event, casual coffee shop). Proper attire shows respect for both the situation and the person. It also contributes positively towards first impressions. Key Tip: Err on the side of being slightly overdressed instead of underdressed. Step 5: Practice Active Listening Skills Active listening involves fully concentrating on what someone says, showing engagement through body language, asking clarifying questions, and summarizing points back to ensure understanding. Samos is a Greek island. It is the eighth largest Greek island. It is in the east of Aegean sea. Opposite Samos is the west coasts of Turkey. Samos is one of 51 prefectures of Greece. The prefecture in addition includes the Ikaria island and Fournoi Island. The capital of Samos is Samos City, commonly referred to by the locals as Vathi. The area of the island is 478 km squared. It is separated from Asia Minor, by the approximately 1 mile wide Mycale Strait. While largely mountainous, Samos has several relatively large and fertile plains. A great portion of the island is covered with vineyards, from which wine is made. The island of Samos is a lush, very green island with forests and with many places to visit. It is also the birthplace of the mathematician Pythagoras. Today Samos is an island which attracts tourists from all over the world for the wonderful nature, the impressive mountainous and verdant landscape and the plethora of archaeological sites. I want to see my other Ninja Turtles. Imagine you are drawing a picture by moving your hand along a path. The speed at which you move your hand is like the "rate of change" or slope of a mathematical function. Now let's say we want to draw a really tall mountain on our paper. We start at the bottom of the page and begin drawing upwards. As we get closer to the top of the mountain, the line we're drawing gets steeper and steeper. In math language, this means that the slope of our function is getting bigger and bigger. In fact, it's approaching a certain value called "positive infinity." But here's where things get interesting. What if instead of drawing a single mountain, we drew two smaller mountains right next to each other? At the very peak, where the two mountains meet, the slope would still be increasing rapidly - but now it has somewhere to go after that! It could keep going up even higher, creating a third mountain peak. And so on. This idea relates to a concept in calculus called "the limit of the derivative as x approaches positive infinity." When we talk about the limit of something as x goes to positive infinity, we mean what happens when we keep making x bigger and bigger without ever actually reaching infinity. So imagine again that we're drawing those mountains, making them taller and taller with each new peak. Even though the slopes at each individual peak are all heading towards positive infinity, the overall shape of the function doesn't necessarily have to do the same thing. However, in this particular problem, we know that not only does f(x)/x approach 1 as x goes to positive infinity, but also its derivative f'(x) must itself head towards positive infinity too. That tells us that no matter how high we build those mountains, their peaks will just keep getting taller and taller forever! Once there was a bad man who wanted some water. He looked everywhere but he couldn't find any water. He was so angry he started to cry. Then something special happened. A big white cloud appeared in the sky and filled the air with water. The water floated down from the sky and wrapped the bad man in a big hug. The water was so soft and comforting. The bad man felt happy. He smiled for the first time in days. He jumped in the water and soaked up the sun. He swam in the water for hours and felt so clean and refreshed. The bad man thanked the cloud for bringing the water and wrapped himself up in a big, warm towel. He no longer felt bad and went on his way feeling happy and refreshed.
