Title: Create and Draw a Celtic Knot Introduction: Celtic knots are beautiful, intricate patterns that have been used for centuries as decorative elements in various forms of art. These knots are most commonly found in insular art, which was produced in Ireland and Britain during the early Middle Ages. Creating your own Celtic knot can be a rewarding experience, but it requires patience and precision. This tutorial will guide you through the process of creating and drawing a simple Celtic knot. Materials Needed: 1. Paper 2. Pencil 3. Ruler 4. Eraser 5. Pen or fine-liner (optional) Step 1: Understanding the Basics Before we start, let's understand what makes a Celtic knot. At its core, a Celtic knot is made up of multiple strands interwoven together without beginning or end, symbolizing eternity. The strands are usually depicted as lines that cross over and under one another in an organized manner. Key Tip: Keep your lines smooth and continuous; avoid breaks or gaps between line segments. Step 2: Draw Guidelines Using your ruler, draw horizontal and vertical lines dividing your paper into equal squares or rectangles depending on the size you want for your knot. For beginners, a grid of 8x8 or 10x10 works well. These lines serve as guides for placing our knot elements accurately. Guideline: Make sure these lines are faint so they don't show once the knot is completed. Step 3: Design Your Framework A basic Celtic knot consists of four corner points connected by curved lines forming a loom-like structure called the framework. Mark four corners where the curves meet the guideline intersection points. Now connect these dots diagonally with two S-shaped curves - one clockwise and the other counterclockwise. Key Tip: Each curve should pass through the midpoint of every square along the diagonal. Step 4: Add Intermediate Points Add symmetry to your design by introducing intermediate points halfway along each side of the frame. Connect these new points using similar S-curves. You now have created a symmetrical pattern ready for interweaving! Step 5: Weave the Strands The magic of Celtic knots lies in their complex yet systematic interweaving. Starting from any intersection point within the loop, decide which strand passes over and which goes under at every crossing until all paths are determined. Remember, no ends must be left untied! Guidelines: - Every time a path splits, maintain consistency in overlapping order. - Wherever possible, try to alternate 'over & under' sequence around successive crossings. Step 6: Refine Your Knock Once you've established the weaving pattern, trace over your pencil marks with a pen or fine-liner if desired. Go slowly here, ensuring accuracy since this final tracing becomes the finished product. Key Tips: - Press lightly when erasing unnecessary construction lines. - If mistakes occur, correct them before moving onto permanent ink. Step 7: Admire Your Work Congratulations! After careful planning, designing, and execution, you've successfully drawn your first Celtic knot. Hang it proudly or share digitally, knowing the effort put behind such a timeless piece of artwork. Remember, practice makes perfect. Don't be discouraged if your initial attempts aren't flawless. With more practice, you'll master the technique and create increasingly elaborate designs. Happy crafting! Once upon a time, there lived a little girl named Daisy. Daisy was only three years old, but she was very brave. One day, Daisy and her mum went to a new park. Everything seemed normal, but Daisy noticed that something strange was happening. As they walked along the path, Daisy saw a collar lying on the ground. She excitedly pointed it out to her mum, who paused and said, "Let's have a look at this collar, Daisy". They examined the collar together, and Daisy noticed that it was a very special collar. Suddenly, she heard a low growl from behind them and looked back. A big, scary dog was standing there, gazing at Daisy. She clutched her mum's hand and started trembling. She told her mum about this, and her mum smiled and said, "Maybe it did, Daisy. We'll have to find the owner". The next day, Daisy and her mum returned to the park with the collar. The Black Keys So far, all of the examples in this book use only the white keys to create music. You only used the black keys as a reference point to find the letter names (A through G) of the white keys. However, the black keys play an important role in music, and they are just as important as the white keys. Rather than having a different letter name for all of the black keys, they borrow their letter name from one of their white key neighbors. For example, the black note just above middle C is called C-sharp, borrowing the letter from the neighboring white key. Another way to think of it: if you raise the note C one-half step, you get the black key C-sharp. If you raise the note D one-half step, you get the black key D-sharp, and so on. The shorthand way to write a sharp is with the # symbol, so D-sharp is written D#. Something interesting happens when you raise the note E one-half step: you land on the white key F. That is because there is no black key in between E and F on the piano keyboard. Nevertheless, the note E# is the same as the note F, even though it is a white key. Instead of raising a white key one-half step to get a sharp, you can lower a white key one-half step to get a flat. The symbol because you lowered the D one-half step. Anytime you lower a note one-half step it becomes flat. It's both! Every black key has two names: one sharp and one flat. It all depends on how you think of it. If you raise the F one-half step to F#, you get the same note as if you lowered the G one-half step to G . FIGURE 7-6: Sharps and flats ) signs placed in the musical notation in front of notes are called accidentals. In musical notation, once you add a sharp sign # or a flat sign . The natural sign tells the pianist to play the natural or white key. In fact, if you just refer to the letter name of any note, A, B, C, etc., you really mean A #, B #, C #. Title: How to Block Someone on wikiHow wikiHow is an online community where anyone can contribute by sharing knowledge and experience through well-written, informative articles. However, there might be situations when you need to prevent another user from editing or commenting on your contributions due to disruptive behavior or violation of the site's policies. This tutorial will guide you through blocking someone on wikiHow while providing clear explanations, key tips, and guidelines at every step. ### Part 1: Identifying the User You Want to Block **Step 1:** Go to the profile page of the user you want to block. To do this, search their username in the wikiHow search bar, then click on their name that appears as a blue link in the search results. Make sure they are indeed the person whose edits or comments have been problematic before proceeding with any further actions. **Key tip**: Keep in mind that everyone makes mistakes sometimes; consider warning the user or discussing issues politely via talk pages first before resorting to blocking them. It is crucial to maintain respectful communication within the community whenever possible. ### Part 2: Accessing Your Block List **Step 1:** Log into your account if you haven't already done so. Once logged in, hover over your avatar (top right corner) and select "My Account" from the dropdown menu. ![My Account](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/5/5c/Log-Into-Wikihow-Step-3.jpg/aid4670984-v4- Romania has a climate that changes between temperate and continental climates. This article will guide you through the proper way to get rid of a scab without causing harm to the wound or delaying its healing process. STRATEGIES TO BECOME A BETTER READER Here are important reading strategies students can use before, during and after reading: Predict what the book is about from the title. Set a purpose for reading. Ex. I am going to read this book because I want to learn more about animals. Take a picture walk through the book. Ask, What is happening in the pictures? Visualize - make a movie in your head just like you do when listening to a story. Question - think about the story, asking yourself who, what, when, where, why, how. Clarify - understand new words - figure out words using print strategies o Use finger to point under each word to keep track of where you are reading o Use beginning sounds to figure out words o Use ending sounds to figure out words o Use pictures on the page to help figure out a word o Use word chunks (group of letters in a pattern like _ack, _ight) o Look for a smaller word within the word o Read to the end of the sentence. Sometimes the word that sentence or passage to increase understanding Make predictions - "What happens next?" Make connections o What other story is like this one? (Text to Text Connection) o Have you felt the same away as a character in the story? Did something similar happen to you? (Text to Self Connection) o Does it help you think about something in real life not directly connected to you? (Text to World Connection) React - What did you think of the story? o How did it make you o What was most important in the story? This one's sitting on a chair with its legs crossed. And the chicken gave it to Bozo to eat. Math problem solving strategies Some math problem solving strategies will be considered here. Study them carefully so you know how to use them to solve other math problems. The biggest challenge when solving math problems is not understanding the problem.It is not because you cannot do math. Make a guess and test it. The sum of 2 consecutive odd numbers is 45. What are the two integers? Before guessing, Always make sure you understand the problem. If possible, get a dictionary or look up the vocabulary words in your math textbook Sum: refer to adding numbers consecutive: In the context of this problem, it will mean that we are looking for an odd number and the next odd number that immediately follows the first one Guessing here means that you will arbitrarily pick two odd numbers, add them, and see if it is equal to 45 15 + 17 = 32. It does not work. Since 32 is smaller than 45, pick higher numbers 19 + 21 = 40. Getting closer 21 + 23 = 45. Here we go. We found the two numbers by guessing! A kindergarten class is going to a play with some teachers. Tickets cost 5 dollars for children and 12 dollars for adults Number of tickets sold amount to 163 dollars. How many teachers and children went to the play? First, make sure you understand the problem. What the problem is really asking is the following: How many adult tickets were sold? How many children tickets were sold? Guess and check! Pretend that 3 children tickets were sold. Then, 17 adult tickets were sold Total cost = 3 5 + 17 12 = 15 + 204 = 219 The total is too high. Pretend that 14 children tickets were sold. Then, 6 adult tickets were sold Total cost = 14 5 + 6 12 = 70 + 72 = 142 The total is a little too low now. Pretend that 12 children tickets were sold. Then, 8 adult tickets were sold Total cost = 12 5 + 8 12 = 60 + 96 = 156 As you can see, it is going higher again and it is getting closer to 163. May be 11 tickets for children and 9 tickets for adults will work Total cost = 11 5 + 9 12 = 55 + 108 = 163 Here we go! 11 children and 9 teachers went to the play. Sometimes, math problem solving strategies could involve making a list. This could happen if I slightly modify example #2 A kindergarten class is going to a play with some teachers. Tickets cost 5 dollars for children and 12 dollars for adults. A total of 20 people could go to the play. There must be at least 2 teachers to supervise the children, but no more than 10. Find all possible ways this could be done. How can the school minimize their cost? This problem involves making a list If 2 teachers go, then 18 children will go If 3 teachers go, then, 17 children will go and so forth... Total cost = 2 12 + 18 5 = 24 + 90 = 114 Total cost = 3 12 + 17 5 = 36 + 85 = 121 Total cost = 4 12 + 16 5 = 48 + 80 = 128 Total cost = 5 12 + 15 5 = 60 + 75 = 135 Total cost = 6 12 + 14 5 = 72 + 70 = 142 Total cost = 7 12 + 13 5 = 84 + 65 = 149 Total cost = 8 12 + 12 5 = 96 + 60 = 156 Total cost = 9 12 + 11 5 = 108 + 55 = 163 Total cost = 10 12 + 10 5 = 120 + 50 = 170 As you can see, the less teacher they send, the less the cost. The least expensive case is to send 2 teachers and 18 children. Other teachers will not be happy about it Math problem solving strategies could also include the use of a variable The use of a variable means that you will let the unknown be x, write and equation, and solve the equation. Use of a variable for example 3 Let x be number of children tickets. Then, 20 - x is number of adult tickets cost of children tickets + cost of adult tickets = total cost x 5 + (20 - x ) 12 = 163 5x + 20 12 - x 12 = 163 5x + 240 -12x = 163 5x + 240 -240 -12x = 163 - 240 5x - 12x = -77 -7x = -77 -7x/-7 = -77/-7 x = 11. Math problem solving strategies involving drawing a diagram A highway has a gas station every 2 miles, a rest area every 4 miles, and a Burger King every 3 miles. Where is the closest gas station, rest area, and burger king all at the same time? A little diagram describing the situation is all we need to tackle this problem real quick. Let red be gas station, let blue be rest area, and let green be Burger King. Draw the diagram below. Notice that every gap between the red lines represents the location of a gas station. Same idea for the blue lines and the green lines The vertical arrow is point toward the location where all 3 services can be found at the same time As you can see, it is 12 miles! You may wonder. How do we get the answer without drawing a diagram? Great question! That will be important if you are dealing with big numbers To get the 12, you need to look for the Least common multiple (LCM). The smallest number that is a multiple of 2, 3, and 4 That number is indeed 12. More information on Least common multiple Among great math problem solving strategies, is working backward One day, I woke up and feeling generous, I took all the apples in my refrigerator and I decided to give them away I went outside and I gave one-half of my apples plus one to the first stranger I met. Then, I gave one-half of the remaining apples plus one to the second person I met and one-half of the remaining apples plus one to the third person. I had one apple left at the end. How many apples did I have when I left my house (This is not a true story. I made that up) Starting backward means that you are starting with the result and work your way backward until you get what you started with Third person: Received one-half plus one. Just do the reverse of that. Give yourself one and twice 1 + 1 = 2 and 2 2 = 4. ( This makes sense because giving one-half of 4 plus one means giving 2 and then 1) Second person: received one-half plus one. Just do the reverse of that. Give yourself one and twice 4 + 1 = 5 and 5 2 = 10. First person: received one-half plus one. Just do the reverse of that. Give yourself one and twice 10 + 1 = 11 and 11 2 = 22. So you had 22 apples in your bag The math problem solving strategies I discussed above are great examples. Make sure you understand them. Hope you had fun exploring these math problem solving strategies! |Powered by Site Build It| oh, what do you think they're going to do with it? [touches the book to show GLA where to find the clothes] Title: How to Check the Ink Level of a Canon Inkjet Printer Introduction: Knowing how to check the ink level of your Canon Inkjet printer is crucial to ensure that you never run out of ink at an inconvenient time. This guide will walk you through the process step-by-step, providing detailed explanations and helpful tips along the way. By following these instructions, you'll be able to monitor your printer's ink levels with ease and confidence. Step 1: Power On Your Printer First, make sure your Canon Inkjet printer is turned on and connected to your computer or network. This allows the printer to communicate with your device and provide accurate ink level readings. Key Tip: If you recently installed new ink cartridges, give your printer some time to recognize them before checking the ink levels. Step 2: Access the Control Panel On your printer, locate and press the "Home" or "Menu" button to access the main control panel. The exact location of this button may vary depending on your specific model. Consult your user manual if necessary. Guideline: Familiarize yourself with the layout of your printer's control panel so you can quickly navigate its functions. Step 3: Navigate to the Main Menu Using the arrow buttons, scroll through the options until you reach the "Setup," "Utilities," or "Maintenance" menu. Select this option by pressing the corresponding button. Key Information: These menus contain essential settings and tools for maintaining your printer's performance, including checking ink levels. Step 4: Locate the Ink Levels Option Within the Setup, Utilities, or Maintenance menu, look for an option labeled "Ink Levels," "Cartridge Status," or something similar. This option displays the current ink levels for all installed cartridges. Helpful Hint: Some printers have a separate physical button for displaying ink levels directly on the LCD screen. Refer to your user manual for guidance. Step 5: Review the Ink Level Display Once you've selected the Ink Levels option, your printer should show a visual representation of the remaining ink for each color cartridge (e.g., cyan, magenta, yellow, black). Typically, this takes the form of bars indicating the percentage of ink left or a simple low/high indicator. Important Note: Keep in mind that ink level indicators are estimates; actual mileage may vary based on usage patterns and print quality settings. Step 6: Monitor and Replace Cartridges as Needed Based on the displayed ink levels, determine whether any cartridges need replacement soon. It's generally recommended to replace cartridges when they reach approximately 10% remaining to avoid running out mid-print job. Best Practice: Regularly monitoring your ink levels helps prevent unexpected interruptions during printing and ensures optimal print quality. Conclusion: By understanding how to check the ink level of your Canon Inkjet printer, you can maintain consistent print quality and minimize downtime due to empty cartridges. Remember to consult your user manual for model-specific instructions, and don't hesitate to contact Canon support if you encounter any issues. Happy printing! Title: How to Encrypt an Internal Drive in Mac OS X Lion Introduction: Data security is crucial in today's digital world. One way to protect your data is by encrypting your drives. This tutorial will guide you through the process of encrypting an internal drive in Mac OS X Lion using FileVault 2. FileVaut 2 is a built-in full disk encryption feature that secures your data with XTS-AES 128 encryption. It provides strong protection against unauthorized access, even if your computer falls into the wrong hands. I. Before You Begin * Make sure you have a backup of all important data before proceeding. Although rare, issues can arise during the encryption process that might result in data loss. * Understand that once you enable FileVault 2, you will need to enter your password every time you start up or wake your Mac from sleep mode. * The encryption process can take several hours depending on the size of your drive. Plan accordingly to avoid interrupting the process. Here, you want to come get it over here? So, let's talk about numbers, especially the number zero, and whether it's even or odd. You all probably already have an idea of what even and odd numbers are. Even numbers are those that can be paired up perfectly with another even number, like 2, 4, 6, and 8. On the other hand, odd numbers leave one left out when trying to pair them up, like 1, 3, 5, and 7. Now, what about zero? Is it even or odd? Well, think about this - even numbers can be divided into two equal groups without leaving anything out. And guess what? Zero works exactly the same way! You can divide zero things into two equal groups because there are no things to begin with! That means zero has the qualities of an even number. Another way we define even numbers is that they are integers (whole numbers) multiplied by 2. For example, 6 is an even number because it equals 2 times 3 (2 x 3 = 6). Now, consider zero; it also follows this rule since zero equals 2 times 0 (2 x 0 = 0), making it an even number yet again! But here's where some people get confused - sometimes they think that if a number isn't typically even OR odd, then maybe it could belong to both categories. However, every number falls neatly into either the even or odd category, including our friend zero. It may seem unusual, but just remember that zero shares all the characteristics of even numbers. To sum up, zero is indeed an even number due to its ability to form pairs and following the rule of being an integer multiplied by 2. Remembering this will help you better understand numbers and their properties as you continue learning mathematics! Farm and chicken they going to cook 'em and eat 'em. This week, our first graders started with brand new Math Switch groups and a new math unit on Halves and Doubles! We first started out sketching a whole number geometrically on our whiteboard grids. Next, we discussed what it might look like to "double" this number. We concluded that the word double also means 2 groups of something. Knowing this also helped our first graders to write equations to go along with doubles. Next, we moved to "halves". Our students were able to create a definition of their own for the quantity of half To find half of a number, you split the total into two equal groups. And that is just what we practiced! The easiest way to solve doubles and halves is to draw a simple picture such as circles. For some it was easiest to use the "dealing method". This helped our students to visually practice how to split the number into two equal groups by "dealing" one circle at a time to each of the two groups until they reach the total. Title: Draw Using Shapes - A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: Drawing using shapes is an excellent way to learn and improve your drawing skills. It involves breaking down complex objects into simpler geometric forms such as circles, squares, triangles, rectangles, and lines. This technique allows artists to create accurate representations of their subjects by focusing on basic structures before adding details. The following steps will guide you through this process with clear explanations, helpful tips, and useful guidelines. Step 1: Observe Your Subject Before starting to draw, observe your subject carefully. Identify its main features and try to break them down into simple geometrical shapes in your mind. This mental exercise will help you understand the structure of the object better and make the actual drawing process easier. Key Tip: Practice observing various objects around you and visualizing them as combinations of different shapes. Step 2: Choose Appropriate Shapes Select appropriate shapes that closely resemble the identified features of your subject. For example, use ovals for organic shapes like fruits or body parts; use straight-edged shapes like rectangles and squares for man-made objects or architectural elements. Guideline: Remember, these initial shapes do not have to be perfect; they serve only as rough guides for proportions and placement. Step 3: Outline Basic Structure Using light strokes, sketch out the chosen shapes to form the basic structure of your subject. Maintain correct perspective and proportion among the shapes. Erase any unnecessary lines after positioning all essential shapes accurately. Key Tip: Keep your pencil lead sharp for precise lines and smooth curves. Step 4: Refine Shapes Now refine the crude shapes by adjusting their edges and corners until they blend seamlessly into one another. Make sure they still represent the overall form of your subject correctly. Step 5: Add Details Begin incorporating smaller shapes within the larger ones to depict finer details. These could include patterns, textures, or additional structural components. Be careful not to overcrowd your drawing with too many details at once. Step 6: Create Depth Perception To give your drawing a sense of three-dimensionality, establish depth perception. You can achieve this by strategically placing shadows, highlights, and overlapping shapes. Use darker shades for receding areas and lighter tones for protruding surfaces. Key Tip: Refer back to your original observation often to ensure accuracy and consistency. Step 7: Clean Up Unnecessary Lines Once satisfied with the development of your drawing, erase any remaining construction lines. If needed, clean up any smudges or unwanted marks with a kneaded eraser. Step 8: Final Touches & Review Lastly, add finishing touches like final shading, blending, or coloring if applicable. Step back from your artwork and review it critically. Look for balance, harmony, and coherence between shapes, lines, values, and colors. Correct any inconsistencies or errors spotted during the review. Conclusion: Drawing using shapes may seem challenging initially but becomes increasingly manageable with practice. By mastering this foundational skill, artists can develop confidence in their ability to render complex subjects realistically while enhancing their problem-solving abilities and creativity. Happy drawing! Before the middle of the sixth century BCE, the land was held by the Medes. The first march was on March 7, 1965. Police officers, and people they had chosen to help them, attacked the marchers with clubs and tear gas. They threatened to throw the marchers off the Edmund Pettus Bridge. Title: Learning Made Easy: An Introduction to Batch Normalization for Grade Schoolers Imagine you're playing a game where you need to pass a ball around with your friends. When the ball comes to you, you might want to make sure that you don't hold onto it too tightly or let go of it too quickly. You want to keep the ball moving smoothly so everyone can enjoy the game! In the world of artificial intelligence, we play a similar game using something called "neural networks." These networks are made up of many layers that work together to learn things and make predictions based on data, just like how you and your friends communicate during the ball game. Now imagine one day someone tells you that passing the ball becomes easier if it has a certain temperature - neither too hot nor too cold, but just right. This way, everyone gets to participate without getting hurt or losing interest. That's exactly the concept behind "batch normalization," which was introduced to improve communication between different layers inside these AI games (or neural networks). When information moves from one layer to another, batch normalization checks its "temperature", making sure it's always at a comfortable level-not too big or too small, but just right. By doing this, the whole system works more efficiently and helps computers understand complex tasks faster than before! It's like having a referee who ensures everything runs smoothly among all players involved in the game. So next time you hear about batch normalization, remember our fun ball game example! Just think of it as a helpful tool that keeps things running smoothly in the background while computers learn new skills and tackle exciting challenges. no, that don't come off just these. Cancer is a type of disease where cells grow out of control, divide and invade other tissues. In a person without cancer, cell division is under control. In most tissues, healthy cells divide in a controlled way and copy themselves to create new healthy cells. With cancer, this normal process of cell division goes out of control. Cells change their nature because mutations have occurred in their genes. All the daughter cells of cancer cells are also cancerous. Oh boy wait, let's cover this, because we're finished with this now, right. Title: How to Create a Stunning Rainbow Braid **Things You'll Need:** 1. Hair extensions or hair chalk in all colors of the rainbow (red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet) 2. A comb 3. Hair ties or rubber bands 4. Hair clips 5. Hairspray (optional) Before you start, remember that patience is key when creating an intricate hairstyle like a rainbow braid. This tutorial may take more time than usual due to the coloring process, but the final result will be worth it! Step 1: Preparation Brush your hair thoroughly to remove any knots or tangles. It's important to have tangle-free hair before starting this style as it can become increasingly difficult to brush out once you've begun braiding and adding color. Step 2: Sectioning Off Your Hair Divide your hair into six equal sections - one for each color of the rainbow. Use large hair clips to separate these sections; they'll help keep the division clear while you work with individual portions. Step 3: Applying Color Choose your first color (let's say red). If using hair extensions, attach them according to the product instructions. If using hair chalk, slide the piece of chalk down the length of a section of hair until saturated with dye. Be sure to cover the entire strand from root to tip. Repeat this step for all six sections. Allow some time for the hair dye to dry completely if necessary. Key Tip: When applying color, consider where you want each shade to appear in the finished braid. For example, placing red nearer the crown creates a traditional 'rainbow rising' effect. Step 4: Creating the Initial Braid Take the furthest left section (should still be red after our initial color placement), cross it over the middle section (originally second from left), then under the new middle section (originally far right). Now repeat this step, always crossing the outer sections over/under the inner ones. Continue until you reach the end of your hair. Guideline: Three strand braids involve alternating between three steps: Cross Strand A over Strand B, Under Strand C, Over Strand D. Memorize this pattern for easier braiding. Step 5: Adding More Colors Once you've created the foundation braid, release another colored section and incorporate it into the braid. To do so, add it into the existing braid just above where you last crossed two sections. Then continue Step 4's braiding pattern across all seven strands instead of three. Step 6: Maintaining Consistent Spacing Ensure consistent spacing between colors by holding each newly added color alongside its neighbor and crossing both simultaneously during the weaving stage. Keep checking the overall appearance of the braid regularly to ensure even distribution of color. Step 7: Finishing Touches When you reach the end of your hair, secure the braid with a tight hair tie. Apply hairspray if desired to hold the style in place and prevent frizziness throughout the day. Remember, practice makes perfect. Don't worry if your first attempt isn't flawless; every stylist starts somewhere! With patience and persistence, you'll master the rainbow braid in no time. Happy styling! " They both have wooly coats just like their baby lamb. " It looks like you've got lunch on your sleeve. Do you know what's in here? Looking Closer at the Moon When you see the Moon in the sky at night, it looks smooth and white. And for thousands of years, that's what everyone thought the Moon was really like. No one had ever seen the Moon up close. The Moon looks very different through a telescope. It isn't smooth at all. When early scientists first saw the mountains and craters on the Moon, they were very surprised. They saw darker areas of the Moon, too. Some thought these might be oceans or seas. They didn't know there was no water on the Moon. When astronauts finally landed on the Moon, it didn't look white. It was grayish-brown and covered with dust. And, of course, there was no water. The astronauts explored the surface of the Moon and did experiments to find out more about how the Moon was formed. They even brought back moonrocks to study, so that scientists on Earth-and you, too-can look even closer at what the Moon is made of. - A telescope is a tool that helps people see things that are far away. Telescopes are made of curved pieces of glass or mirrors inside a tube. People can use them to look at stars. - What is the surface of the Moon like? [anno: The surface of the Moon has mountains and craters and is covered with grayish-brown dust.] - What did the astronauts bring back from the Moon? Metamorphic Rock Process III Written for the KidsKnowIt Network by: Scientists call the third way metamorphic rocks form a tectonic process. Sometimes I think scientists like to use long words to make it harder for non-scientists to figure out what they are talking about. Here's what they are talking about: The large plates that make up the crust of the earth are always moving. Sometimes they slam into each other. Sometimes they grind past each other. Sometimes the plates are pulling apart forming large cracks called rifts. When plates pull apart it is called a divergent boundary. On some boundaries one plate dives under another plate. Geologists call this type of boundary a convergent boundary. When the plates bump and grind past each other, the geologists call this boundary a transform fault boundary. A famous example of this type of fault in the United States is called the San Andres Fault. At that plate boundary, the North American plate is moving mostly south and the Pacific plate is moving mostly north, which means that in 15 million years Los Angeles and San Francisco will be neighbors. I wonder if that will solve the debate about which is the better town. In any case, metamorphic rocks are formed from the pressure and heat caused by the plates crashing into each other. The San Andreas Fault in California. So what rocks are metamorphic rocks? Examples of these rocks are marble, schist, slate, gneiss (pronounced "nice"). All of these rock types are formed by heat and pressure. So what makes them different? The answer is simple. Metamorphic rocks are formed from different rocks. Marble is made from sedimentary rock called limestone and sometimes dolomite. Gneiss is formed from ingenious rock, like granite. Schist is formed from sedimentary rocks like mudstone or siltstone. Slate is formed from shale. As a matter of fact, slate and shale look so much like each other, the best way to tell them apart is to lightly tap them with a metal object like a coin. Slate and shale make different sounds when tapped. When tapped, slate has a slightly more metallic sound than shale. Shale makes a kind of hollow thumping sound. Because of its beauty, marble is used for many purposes. Metamorphic rocks are one of the three main types of rocks and are the most common of rock on the continental plates. Fossils may be found in metamorphic rocks, but only if the metamorphic rock was formed from a sedimentary rock that already had the fossil in it. However, the fossil is most likely going to be crushed, warped, or somehow changed because the process that changes sedimentary rock into metamorphic rock will change the fossil, too. Marble is a metamorphic rock used by artists to create sculptures and to decorate buildings and other things. Slate was used to make chalkboards and is still used to make pool tables. Keep your eyes open! Metamorphic rocks are everywhere. And what kind of adventures do they have? Indie's a little doggie and Sheba's a big doggie. Like I said I think a cookie is pretty memorable once you've had one. [Nina is still talking about the girl on her card] [pointing to picture on cover of the book] Date: 01/16/97 at 19:49:00 From: Fran Sherwood Subject: Scientific notation How do you write a number in scientific notation? Date: 01/17/97 at 12:51:17 From: Doctor Wallace Subject: Re: Scientific notation Hi Fran! Scientific notation is usually used to express numbers that are either very very large, or very very small. It's a way of writing these numbers so that they don't take up a lot of space. A number written in scientific notation is made up of two parts, a decimal part, and a power of 10. For example, 2.1 x 10^6 is a number written in scientific notation. The 2.1 is the decimal part, and the 10^6 is the power of ten (the ^ symbol here means that the 6 is the exponent). Numbers in scientific notation are really multiplication expressions. We would read the above number as "2.1 times 10 to the sixth." Since 10^6 is 10 x 10 x 10 x 10 x 10 x 10 = 1,000,000, then 2.1 times this is 2,100,000. Now for your question. How do we write a number in scientific notation? It's easier than it looks. Say we have a number like 43. To write this or any other number in scientific notation, we have to "pull out" the power of ten inside the number. What power of 10 is in 43? Well, it's 10^1 or 10. To keep the value of 43, when we pull out this 10^1, we need to decrease 43 by 1/10, or divide it by 10. We get 43 divided by 10 = 4.3 when we do this. So 43 in scientific notation is 4.3 x 10^1. Written another way, this is 4.3 x 10 = 43. Now, what if we had 430 to write in scientific notation? Well, we'd pull out the next higher power of 10, 10^2 (which is 100), and divide 430 by 100 and get 4.3 again. So 430 = 4.3 x 10^2. Have you spotted the easy way to figure out which power of ten to pull out yet? If you haven't, think about it for a bit before reading further. Here's how: Take your number, and move the decimal point to the left until it stops between the first two digits in your number. Count how many places you have to move it, and that's the power of 10 you need. Here's an example: 1,244 is what in scientific notation? We move the decimal point to the left until it's between the first two digits, so we have 1.244. Then we count how many places it moved: 3. So, 1,244 = 1.244 x 10^3 Here's another example: 1,544,433 = 1.544433 x 10^6 This method will only work for changing numbers that are greater than 1. If you need to change a very small number like .032454 into scientific notation, then the procedure is just a little different. Maybe you can figure it out on your own. Thanks for writing! -Doctor Wallace, The Math Forum Check out our web site! http://mathforum.org/dr.math/ Search the Dr. Math Library: Ask Dr. MathTM 1994-2013 The Math Forum Carbon dioxide is a greenhouse gas. Title: How to Cancel Email Subscriptions Introduction: Email subscriptions can be an excellent way to stay informed about your favorite brands, news outlets, or services. However, over time, you might find yourself receiving more emails than you would like, leading to cluttered inboxes and unnecessary distractions. This comprehensive guide will walk you through canceling email subscriptions, helping you regain control of your inbox. Step 1: Identify Unwanted Emails First, go through your inbox and identify which email subscriptions are no longer relevant or desirable. These could include promotional offers, newsletters, or updates from various companies. Pay attention to senders that consistently end up in your spam folder as well, as they may still be sending unwanted emails even after unsubscribing. Key Tip: Create filters or rules within your email client to automatically categorize subscription-based emails into specific folders. This organization method makes identifying unwanted emails easier while keeping important messages separate. Step 2: Access the Email's Opt-Out Page Open one of the identified marketing emails and locate the opt-out link, typically found at the bottom of the message under headings such as "Unsubscribe," "Manage Preferences," or "Update Profile." Click on this link to proceed with canceling the email subscription. Guideline: If you cannot find the opt-out option within the email itself, check if there is a contact address (usually located near the top or bottom) where you can directly request removal from their mailing list. Sending a polite, straightforward email asking to be removed should suffice. Step 3: Confirm Your Decision You will likely be redirected to a confirmation page upon clicking the opt-out link. Here, ensure you have selected all available options related to unsubscription - some websites offer granular choices, enabling you to remain subscribed to certain topics or categories but removing others. After making your selections, click the appropriate button (often labeled "Confirm" or "Submit") to finalize your decision. Key Tip: Be cautious when presented with additional forms or surveys during this process; these elements aim to gather information about why you wish to unsubscribe, potentially influencing future decisions regarding user engagement strategies. Only fill out optional survey questions if comfortable sharing personal data with third parties. Step 4: Monitor Your Inbox After unsubscribing, continue monitoring your inbox for any lingering emails from the same sender(s). Some organizations take several days (or even weeks) to fully remove individuals from their mailing lists due to processing delays or technical issues. Should you receive further unsolicited emails beyond the expected waiting period, consider reaching out again via customer support channels to reiterate your desire to unsubscribe. Step 5: Maintaining Control Over Future Subscriptions To avoid accumulating excessive new email subscriptions moving forward, evaluate incoming requests carefully before granting permission for businesses or platforms to send promotional materials. When signing up for online accounts or purchasing products, pay close attention to pre-checked boxes indicating consent for marketing communications and adjust accordingly based on personal preferences. Additionally, regularly review existing subscriptions by following steps outlined above, ensuring your digital space remains organized and manageable. [gasp] look at this what about this? [replies and makes a suggestion to MOT] Question: A clever treasure hunter discovered an Ancient Egyptian tomb after a treacherous journey through the dark corridors of a pyramid. He saw six bags of intricately carved gold pieces. The inscriptions on the wall of the chamber warned would-be grave robbers that five of the bags contained false gold and that the trip back would be impossible with more than one bag. The hieroglyphs also showed that each of the real gold pieces was 1.1 Egyptian units of measurement and the false gold was one unit each. The tomb contained a special scale that would work only once, and when the amount to be weighed was placed on the scale, no pieces could be added or removed to watch the weight fluctuate. The writings warned that if the scale was misused the tomb would collapse. How could the treasure hunter use the scale to determine which bag to take? Answer: Place one piece from bag one, two pieces from bag two, three pieces from bag three...on the scale. If the resulting weight is 21.1, the real gold was in bag one. If the weight is 21.2 it was in bag two. 21.3 would be bag three, 21.4 would be bag four, 21.5 would be bag five, and 21.6 would be bag six. Title: How to Boot Your Computer in Safe Mode Safe Mode is a diagnostic mode of a computer operating system (OS). It can be used to troubleshoot issues with your computer because it starts up your system using only the most essential system files and drivers. This guide will walk you through the process of booting your Windows or Mac computer in Safe Mode. **Windows Users:** Step 1: Restart Your Computer The first step to booting your Windows PC in Safe Mode is to restart your computer. Once it has fully shut down, press the power button to turn it back on. Tip: If your computer doesn't respond when you try to restart it normally, you may need to hold down the power button until it turns off completely. Then wait a few seconds before pressing the power button again to turn it back on. Step 2: Access the Advanced Startup Options Menu As soon as your computer begins to start up, start tapping the F8 key on your keyboard repeatedly. This should bring up the Advanced Boot Options menu. Key Guideline: The timing here is crucial; if you miss the window to press F8, you'll have to try again next time you reboot. Step 3: Select 'Safe Mode' from the List Using the arrow keys on your keyboard, navigate to 'Safe Mode' and press Enter. Step 4: Log Into Your Account You will now log into your account just like normal, but this time with limited software and services running. Tips while in Safe Mode: - Only necessary programs are loaded, making it easier to identify problematic applications or drivers causing conflicts. - System performance might increase due to fewer background processes. - Viruses and malware are less likely to run in Safe Mode, facilitating virus removal procedures. To exit Safe Mode once you're done troubleshooting, simply restart your computer as usual - it should automatically boot up in Normal Mode unless you manually select Safe Mode again during startup. **Mac Users:** Step 1: Shut Down Your Mac Click on the Apple logo at the top left corner of your screen and then click 'Shut Down'. After your Mac shuts down, press the power button to turn it back on. Step 2: Hold Down the Shift Key Immediately after pressing the power button, start holding down the Shift key. Keep holding it down until you see the login screen appear. Guidelines: Make sure there aren't any USB devices connected that could interfere with the boot process. Also, ensure you release the shift key after seeing the login screen but before logging into your user account. Step 3: Login Routine Log into your user account as you usually would. You'll notice that some icons in the Dock may be grayed out, indicating they won't open since you're in Safe Mode. Benefits of Using Safe Mode on a Mac: - Helps diagnose issues related to third-party fonts, kernel extensions, and other startup items. - Allows users to repair their disk or perform other maintenance tasks more smoothly. When ready to leave Safe Mode, save whatever work you were doing, close all applications, and then choose 'Restart' from the Apple menu. Your Mac should start up normally without entering Safe Mode. Remember, both these methods require administrative privileges, so make sure you know your administrator password before attempting either method. In the summer it had tasty fruits that the animals would eat. In the autumn the leaves turned golden. In the winter, the tree was huge and places to hide. One day, a little girl was walking by the tree and saw something strange. It was an old dusty book, tucked between the roots of the trunk. The little girl picked it up and opened it. Inside, the most beautiful pictures were on every page. The little girl couldn't read the words on the page, but she looked at the pictures carefully, seeing how one page was connected to the next. She liked the pictures so much that she took the book home. Every morning she would come to the tree and read the book. Eventually, she found that she could remember the stories without looking at the book. And the more she read, the more she learned about the world around her. The moral of the story is that we can learn something from everything, even if we can't read the words. A huge tree can teach us a lot if we take the time to look and explore. On maintaining the bridge against him depend our lives, as well as our hope to reclaim our laws and liberties.--There shall at least one Scottishman die in their defence.--Let any one who loves his country follow me!" The multitude had turned their heads in the direction to which he pointed. The sight of the glittering files of the English Foot-Guards, supported by several squadrons of horse, of the cannon which the artillerymen were busily engaged in planting against the bridge, of the plaided clans who seemed to search for a ford, and of the long succession of troops which were destined to support the attack, silenced at once their clamorous uproar, and struck them with as much consternation as if it were an unexpected apparition, and not the very thing which they ought to have been looking out for. But he had left an appetite in his audience for further oratory, and one of them seemed to express a general sentiment as he hurried immediately to Felix, and said, "Come, sir, what do you say? " Felix did at once what he would very likely have done without being asked--he stepped on the stone, and took off his cap by an instinctive prompting that always led him to speak uncovered. The effect of his figure in relief against the stone background was unlike that of the previous speaker. He was considerably taller, his head and neck were more massive, and the expression of his mouth and eyes was something very different from the mere acuteness and rather hard-lipped antagonism of the trades-union man. Felix Holt's face had the look of habitual meditative abstraction from objects of mere personal vanity or desire, which is the peculiar stamp of culture, and makes a very roughly-cut face worthy to be called "the human face divine." Everyday, they used to stir the earth to plant the seeds. But one day Bee wanted to plant some spicy seeds, while Bird wanted to plant regular ones. Bee said to Bird, "Let's plant these spicy seeds; they will make the earth taste delicious!" Bird disagreed, "Regular seeds will make more flowers!" Bee and Bird started to argue, but eventually they found a compromise. Bee said, "Let's plant both kinds of seeds, the regular ones and the spicy ones." Bird agreed, and so they planted all kinds of seeds in the earth. When the flowers bloomed, they were beautiful and more delicious than either Bee or Bird had imagined. Bird said, "See how much better our garden looks when we work together?" Bee nodded and smiled, understanding that with two minds, the work had become twice as tasty. The lesson of this story is that working together is always better than working alone. We can always accomplish more when we work together. This is the beauty of friendship and collaboration. A linear function, we have seen is a function whose graph lies on a straight line, and which can be described by giving its slope and its y intercept. There is a special kind of linear function, which has a wonderful and important property that is often useful. These are linear functions whose y intercepts are 0 (for example functions like 3x or 5x). This means their graphs pass right through the origin, (the point with coordinates (0, 0)). Such functions are called homogeneous linear functions. They have the property that their values at any combination of two arguments is the same combination of their values at those arguments. In symbols this statement is: f(ax + bz) = af(x) + bf(z) Do ordinary linear functions have any such property? They do. Any linear function at all has the same property when b is 1 - a. Thus for any linear function at all we have f(ax + (1 - a)z) = a f(x) + (1 - a) f(z) But be careful, linear functions that are not homogeneous do not obey the general linearity property stated several lines above. Properties like these mean that once you know the value of a linear function at two arguments you can easily find its value anywhere else it is defined. The property here described is often called the property of linearity. This is not really a sensible way to describe it because perfectly good linear functions which have y intercept that is not 0 do not obey the more general version of the property (the first one above.) Anyway, realize that most functions DO NOT have either of these properties. 3A Describing Linear Functions on a Spreadsheet Suppose we have a linear function, say, f(x) = 5x + 3. We now address the following questions: 1. How can we evaluate this function at an arbitrary argument, x, on 2. How can we evaluate it at a whole lot of arguments? 3. How can we plot it? Will see that once the first of these questions is addressed, the rest are quite easy to do. They were harder in the old days. One nice feature of what you can do is that if you set this up once, you can change the linear function at will and watch how the plot changes instantly, as in the applet. Just in case you want to keep what you are doing you will be wise to give it labels so at some future time you will know what you have. So as a preliminary, you might enter in box A1 the title: Linear Functions. Some more preliminaries: in A2 write the word slope, and in B2 enter the number 5 (later on you can change this to anything else you want) In A3 enter the words: y intercept, and in B3 enter the number 3. In A4 enter: starting argument and in B4 enter -1 In A5 enter: spacing and in B5 enter .01. (When you want to plot your function, you can only do it over a finite interval, and these last lines are useful for creating an interval.) Now you are ready to start. In A9 enter the symbol x and in B9 enter f(x). These are labels for the columns In A10 enter =B4 In B10 enter =B$2*A10 +B$3 You now have the answer to the first question. The number that appears in box B10 will be the value of your function at the argument given in B4 (at this point that argument is -1, and with function 5x + 3 the value in B10 should be -2.) You can evaluate this function anywhere else you please, by changing the entry in B4 to whatever you please. Suppose I want to change the slope or the y intercept of my function? You can do that by changing the entries in B2 or B3. The value of the changed function at the argument in A10 will appear in B10. What are these funny dollar signs that I have put in A10 and B10? To answer the second and third questions above we are going to copy the instruction in B10 into other boxes as well. When we do that, the references which do NOT have dollar signs in front of them will change . Those with dollar signs will stay the same. How do the references change? What do you mean? Suppose we copy B10 to B11. Then what will appear in B11 will not be exactly what is in B10, but instead it will be =B$2*A11 +B$3. Because the A10 had no dollar sign in it, when we copied it down one row the 10 turned into an 11. The other terms did not change because we put dollar signs in front of them. What happens if you copy to a different column? The same kind of thing will happen. That is, if you copy what is in B10 to C11, you will get =C$2*B11 +C$3. All the column indices that do not have dollar signs in front of them will shift over one column, because you shifted over one column. The same goes for shifting any number of rows or columns. This property is what allows us to look at a function over a range and plot it by copying. Our plan is: have the argument increase by d from row to row, which can be accomplished by putting one entry in A11 and copying it down the A column Then copying B10 down the B column. That is all there is to answer the second question. OK, what goes into A11? We can enter =A10+B$5. This will increase the entry in column A in each row we copy it to by the amount in B5 over what it was in the previous row. If we do this in Column A, say down to row 500, and copy B10 also down to row 500, you will have a set of pairs for your function all ready to plot. OK, how do I copy? This varies somewhat from spreadsheet to spreadsheet. For many or most you do the following: 1. Highlight the box you want to copy. 2. Press [Ctrl] and c at the same time 3. Highlight the boxes you want to copy to. 4. Press [Ctrl] and v at the same time. There is another way that is easier if you are copying several columns down from the same row at once; it is called fill or fill down on the edit menu. Try it. You can also fill sidewise. (Here you could copy B10 into B11 and then fill A11 and B11 both together down to A500 and B500.) Experiment with these things until you get them to work. If you can't get them to work on your spreadsheet, ask someone how. OK, how do I get a graph of my function? Highlight columns A and B from row 10 or 11 to row 500 (or to wherever you copied to) and click on "chart" in the insert menu. You will get to another menu with lots of options. Click on "x-y xcatter", and you will get to your plot. You will be asked about inserting labels on it and asked where you want it. You can put it anywhere, but if you put it on the same sheet as your calculation, you can change the function or domain by changing what is in B2,...,B5 and see the results immediately. There are ways to adjust the size of the graph and where it is, that you have to figure out for yourself. I generally screw them up. Once upon a time, there was a tall fox who lived in the forest. She was very curious, and every day she liked to study the forest animals. One day, she asked a rabbit who was hopping by, "Do you know what I study every day?" The rabbit looked up and said, "No, I don't know, what do you study?". The special theory of relativity is based on the prediction, so far upheld by observations, that the measured speed of light in a vacuum is the same whether or not the source of the light and the person doing the measuring are moving relative to each other. This is sometimes expressed as "the speed of light is independent of the reference frame." Africa is the second largest continent in the world. It makes up about a fifth of the world's land. The most notable rainforests are in the tropics or subtropics, mostly in the Intertropical Convergence Zone. The biggest rainforest is the Amazon rainforest, which is mostly in Brazil. Forests like this have extraordinary biodiversity. Biologists say over half of all plant and animal species live in the rainforest. Also more than 1/4 of all medicines come from here. Even though they only cover 6% of the Earth's land area they are still an important source of oxygen. Three and he tries to get the bus driver's attention. Um yeah a lot of the wild animals. Sam was a horse who lived on a farm. He liked to run and jump and play with his friends. He also liked to eat hay, which was his food. Hay was soft and sweet and filled his belly. One day, a big truck came to the farm. It had a lot of hay in the back. Sam was happy to see the hay. He thought it was for him and his friends. He ran to the truck and tried to get some hay. But the truck driver was not nice. He shouted at Sam and hit him with a stick. He said the hay was not for him. He said the hay was for other horses who were tough and strong and could work hard. Hyperboles are not literal. They're meant to paint a funny picture or make a point. Here are some examples of hyperboles to illustrate the point and to help you identify them when they come up in speech or writing. * I'm so hungry, I could eat a horse! * You snore louder than a freight train. * He sleeps like a log. This means that there are four parts in a butterfly's life. The first part is the egg. The second part is the caterpillar (sometimes called the larva). Rationale: Beginner readers have to devote the majority of their attention to decoding words. As readers become better decoders it is important that they learn how to become faster, smoother, and more expressive readers. They should be able to read silently as well as out loud. The more children read the easier the process will become. The easier it is the more a child will want to spend their leisure time reading a good book. This lesson will encourage children to use expression while reading through demonstration and practice. Materials: a short exciting newspaper article, basal readers (even if children are on different reading levels they can still read what they have been reading from in their reading groups), stuck on a word? bookmarks, slips of paper with different expressive sentences on each slip 1. Have you ever listened to a really good storyteller? What about her telling of the story made it so interesting? Raise your hand if you remember. (As children respond write their responses on the board) Reading a story is similar to telling a story. While reading it is very important to use lots of expression to keep your audience's attention. Today we are going to talk about two different ways of using more expression in your reading. 2. I am going to demonstrate an expressive reader and a non expressive reader. Pay close attention to the difference. (Read part of an article of a newspaper with expression and part without expression) Which part did you want to listen to more? Why? (discuss the differences) 3. To be an expressive reader you do not have to know every word. I have been reading for years and I am still learning new words. It is important to remember what to do when you get stuck on a word. First take a shot. You can cover up part of it to make it easier to sound out. Second you can read the rest. Read to the end of the sentence to see what would make sense. Remember you can change a guess to fit the sentence. If you still do not know the word read it again. You can start the sentence over to get back into the story. I am going to pass out bookmarks with the different things you can do when you get stuck on a word. 4. The volume of your voice can create a lot of expression. Who can tell me what volume is? Volume is how loud or soft your voice is when you read. If you talk real quietly the audience strains to listen in suspense to what is going to happen. As the reader gets louder it creates more excitement. What are some other messages that the volume of your voice can send out to your audience? (write responses on the board) 5. I would like everyone to turn to the person to your left. This person will be your reading partner. I would like for each one of you to have a turn to read with expression to your partner. I want you to concentrate on the volume of your voice. Everyone can read something in his or her reading book. (walk around and listen to the children reading. Pick a child who is reading with lots of expression- esp. with volume) While I was walking around I heard _____________ read with a lot of expression. Would you please read a little bit to the class using that same great expression. 6. A second way of using expression is to change the speed that you read. If I read real slowly it can make you think of being sleepy or sad. If I gain speed it will make you think of being excited or nervous. Can anyone think of any other ways that the speed of your voice can make listeners feel? (write responses on the board) 7. I would like for you to turn back to your partner and continue reading in your reading book. This time I want you to not only concentrate on volume but also on speed. I am going to be listening for some expressive readers. (allow children to read) I heard ___________ reading with so much expression. Would you please stand up and read to the class so that they can hear your nice expression using volume and speed. 8. For assessment: I am going to pass out to everyone a slip of paper with some sentences on it. As I call your name I would like for each person to stand up and read their sentences with a lot of expression. (discuss how each child uses different kinds of expression) Reference: Allen, R.V. Congenital heart disease refers to a form of heart problem that starts before birth (congenital). Congenital heart disease includes a large number of conditions. The formation of the heart before birth is very complex and is sometimes abnormal. For example, if a part of the main artery that comes from the heart is narrowed, that is called coarctation of the aorta. There may be holes inside the heart which keep the blood from flowing normally inside the heart. Other congenital heart problems are caused by abnormal heart valves. In some cases, congenital heart problems are discovered at birth. Other times the problems may not be detected until the person is an adult You picked up my fork, and then you put it down in the one wet spot on the table. 'Kay we need a plaque on the wall. Data compression is a set of steps for packing data into a smaller space, while allowing for the original data to be seen again. Compression is a two-way process: a compression algorithm can be used to make a data package smaller, but it can also be run the other way, to decompress the package into its original form. Data compression is useful in computing to save disk space, or to reduce the bandwidth used when sending data (e.g., over the internet). You're almost done hurry up I want XXX. "Go away. Title: How to Change a Child's Room into a Teenager's Room Introduction: Transforming a child's room into a teenager's room is an exciting project that allows you to create a space tailored to your teen's interests, personality, and need for independence. This transition involves more than just swapping out toy boxes for bean bags; it requires careful planning, communication with your teen, and an understanding of their preferences. By following this comprehensive guide, you will successfully change your child's room into a comfortable, stylish, and functional teenager's room. Summary of Steps: 1. Consult with your teen 2. Declutter and organize 3. Create a layout plan 4. Select a color scheme 5. Choose new furniture 6. Update lighting fixtures 7. Add storage solutions 8. Personalize the space 9. Install safety features Step 1: Consult with your teen Explanation: Engage your teen in the design process by discussing their vision for their new room. Encouraging their input ensures they feel ownership of the space and increases the likelihood they will maintain its organization and cleanliness. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Schedule a conversation when both you and your teen are relaxed and have time to discuss ideas. - Ask open-ended questions about their preferred colors, styles, and themes. - Discuss any additional functionality they would like, such as a study area or lounge zone. - Establish a budget together to manage expectations and prevent overspending. Step 2: Declutter and Organize Explanation: Before making significant changes, sort through your teen's belongings and purge items no longer needed or used. This decluttering phase creates a blank slate for redesigning the room and makes organizing easier. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Categorize items into keep, donate, sell, and discard piles. - Consider involving your teen in the decision-making process to teach responsibility and life skills. - Use storage bins, containers, and labels to organize remaining items before adding new furnishings. Step 3: Create a Layout Plan I think I'll be really full of this. 12. Before sowing, remove all the plants and trees on the plot. Shade and the roots of trees prevent vegetables from growing well. Roots of trees take out of the soil mineral salts which should feed the vegetables. Trees also harbor insects which may eat the vegetables. 13. Before sowing, you must also work the soil with the spade or the hoe. But do not turn over the soil. If you turn over the soil, you put on the top those layers of the soil that contain less humus and are of less good structure. If you till deeply, you must put manure into the soil so as to improve the poorer layers underneath. Working the soil well means that you let air and water into the earth, mix in manure (see paragraphs 33 and 34) and destroy weeds. If the soil is too wet, you must wait a little before tilling. 14. A bed is the name for one of the small plots on which vegetables are grown. Use a length of cord to trace out the beds and put pegs in the earth at the four corners of each bed. Each bed should be 1 metre wide. In this way you can sow and look after the plants in the middle of the bed without damaging your vegetables. Between the beds, leave room for a path. For example, leave 60 centimetres so that you can get through with the wheelbarrow. Vegetable beds with path If the plot is large and flat enough, make your beds 10 metres long. Then you will have beds 1 metre wide and 10 metres long; that is, 10 square metres (m2). For each bed of 10 m2, add 30 kilogrammes of manure to the soil (roughly the contents of a wheelbarrow). If the plot is on a slope, make the beds across the slope. Beds across the slope When you have marked out your vegetable beds, remove the stones, break up the clods of earth with the forked hoe or the rake, so that the surface is quite flat. Then firm down the earth, for example with the tamper. Beds in dry season and in the rainy season 15. Decide where in your garden you will place: - nursery beds, - compost pits, - tool shed. Put up a fence. This can be made with posts, millet stems, maize stems, palm fronds. The new government in Russia was a democratic socialist government. Unfortunately, it was ineffective, and people were still unhappy. In November 1917, a communist group called the Bolsheviks led by Vladimir Lenin overthrew the new government. They were supported by groups of workers called Soviets. The Bolsheviks created a new communist government called the Russian Soviet Federation Socialist Republic (called simply Soviet Russia or the Russian SFSR). Becoming a MySpacer =================== MySpace is a social networking platform that allows users to connect with friends, share content, and discover new music and artists. This tutorial will guide you through the process of becoming a MySpacer, from creating an account to customizing your profile and interacting with other users. By following these steps, you'll be able to make the most out of your MySpace experience. Creating a MySpace Account -------------------------- 1. **Visit the MySpace website.** Open your web browser and navigate to <https://myspace.com>. The homepage should display various options such as signing up or logging in. 2. **Click on 'Sign Up'.** You can find this option at the top right corner of the page. Clicking on it will take you to the registration form where you can create your MySpace account. 3. **Fill out the registration form.** Provide your email address, birthdate, gender, country, and zip code. Make sure to enter valid information since some fields may require verification later on. 4. **Choose a username and password.** Your username should ideally reflect your personality or interests while being unique and easy to remember. Select a strong password consisting of uppercase and lowercase letters, numbers, and symbols. 5. **Enter your first and last name.** Keep in mind that only your friends will see your real name; others will view your chosen username instead. 6. **Provide your high school or college (optional).** If you wish to connect with classmates, entering your educational institution might help you find them more quickly. 7. **Read and accept the terms of service and privacy policy.** Review the documents carefully before proceeding. Understanding these policies ensures that you are aware of how your data is used and shared within the platform. 8. **Verify your email address.** After submitting the registration form, check your inbox for a confirmation email from MySpace. Once upon a time there were two friends, Josh and Beth. Josh found a flute and he was extremely happy. He asked Beth to join him and they started to play the flute together. Josh asked Beth 'What number shall we play?' Together they counted 1,2,3 and started to play the flute. They had so much fun! Suddenly, Josh's mom came into the room and saw the flute. She said 'Uh-oh, this flute looks familiar. Have you taken it from the living room?' Josh and Beth became very quiet. Josh looked down and felt guilty. He knew he had taken the flute from the living room. He admitted it and said 'Sorry mommy'. Josh's mom gave him a hug and said 'It's ok. It's important to always tell the truth. I forgive you. Now please put the flute back.' Josh and Beth put the flute back in the living room and felt better. They promised each other to always be honest and never take things without asking. All living things need energy to survive, move, grow, and reproduce. Some can get energy from the environment without help from other living things: these are called producers, or autotrophs. Plants, algae, and some bacteria, a group of producers called photoautotrophs, use the sun's light for energy. When producers use light to make and store organic compounds, this is called photosynthesis. Some other producers, called chemoautotrophs, get energy from chemicals that come out of the ocean floor in hydrothermal vents. Other living things get their energy from organic compounds: these are called consumers, or heterotrophs. Animals, fungi, most bacteria, and most protists are consumers. Consumers can eat other living things or dead material. [about returning as she leaves the room] He's going to ask for a glass of milk. let's put all the toys back and let's do somethin' else. Title: How to Get People to Ask You to Babysit for Them ===================================================== Are you looking for ways to become a trusted babysitter in your community? Follow these steps to learn how to present yourself as a responsible, reliable, and fun option for parents who need childcare services. This guide will help you understand how to build relationships with families, showcase your skills, and create opportunities for them to ask you to babysit. ### Step 1: Identify Potential Families Start by listing friends, family members, neighbors, or colleagues who have children or are expecting. These individuals may already be familiar with your personality and trustworthiness, making them more likely to consider asking you to babysit. Key tip: Expanding your network through extracurricular activities, volunteering, or attending local events can introduce you to even more potential families. ### Step 2: Build Relationships Spend time getting to know the families on your list. Attend their kids' birthday parties, sports games, recitals, and other gatherings where possible. Engage in conversations about parenting, hobbies, work-life balance, and any challenges they face regarding child care. Show genuine interest in both the adults and the children. Building rapport is crucial because people usually hire someone they feel comfortable around and trust. Guideline: Consistency and sincerity go a long way when building relationships. Make an effort to stay connected without being overbearing. ### Step 3: Establish Your Credibility Let others know that you take babysitting seriously by obtaining relevant certifications such as first aid and CPR training. Consider taking a babysitting course at a local Red Cross chapter or online. Additionally, share information about your experience caring for children-whether it was informally helping out relatives or formally working at a daycare center. Having proper qualifications demonstrates your commitment and responsibility towards child safety. Key tip: Keep track of your completed courses, trainings, and experiences in a document so that you can quickly provide proof if needed. ### Step 4: Communicate Availability Make sure the families on your list are aware of your availability. Let them know which days and hours you can potentially watch their children. Be flexible whenever possible, but also set boundaries to avoid burnout. Parents appreciate honesty and transparency regarding scheduling constraints. Guideline: When discussing availability, emphasize your willingness to accommodate last-minute requests since emergencies do arise, and showing flexibility could lead to future opportunities. ### Step 5: Offer References If asked directly whether you babysit, confidently express your enthusiasm for the job and offer references from previous experiences. Provide contact details of satisfied customers who would vouch for your reliability, professionalism, and ability to handle various situations involving children. Positive reviews from past clients significantly increase the likelihood of new families considering hiring you. Key tip: Request permission before sharing personal contacts; maintain confidentiality and respect privacy. ### Step 6: Demonstrate Responsible Social Media Use Bell married Mabel Hubbard on July 11, 1877. He died of diabetes at his home near Baddeck, Nova Scotia in 1922. Before Judas Priest recorded their first album, a second guitar player, Glenn Tipton, joined the band. The first album, Rocka Rolla, came out in 1974. It was not very successful, probably because it didn't have many of the songs that the band were known for playing in live concerts back in those days. They would later be on the band's next album, Sad Wings of Destiny in 1976. That album was followed up by Sin After Sin in 1977. These three albums had a hard rock style that was familiar to bands like Led Zeppelin, and had a psychedelic feel. Learn more physics! What exactly is color? - Dan (age 18) Light is what scientists call an electromagnetic wave. A light wave is just like the wave you would see at a beach. Sometimes the waves have peaks that are close together, and sometimes the waves have peaks that are far apart. This happens with light too except light waves are very very tiny. Some light waves have peaks that are closer together than other light waves (for an electromagnetic wave, the "peak" is just a point at which the strength of the electromagnetic field is the strongest). The color of light depends on the size of the wave. If the peaks are further apart (longer wavelength), the color looks more red. And if the peaks of the light wave are closer together (shorter wavelength), the light looks more blue. (published on 10/22/2007) Follow-up on this answer. Are you going to be a dropout Mark?
