[sits on floor and looks in mirror] There's a doll and there's the lid. I am teaching this lesson to help students learn a new, important vowel correspondence. This will help to further their knowledge of the alphabetic principle by providing them with more insight into how phonemes and graphemes work together to form words. I am teaching short vowels first because there are less instances of irregularity and therefore more uniform and easier to remember. The students will learn to identify the short /i/ correspondence in both spoken and written words through practice and actual reading. It is important for students to accomplish this goal so that they can continue their journey to automatic reading. This correspondence is in many words students need to know, therefore, they need to automatically recognize it to be fluid readers. 1)Large picture card with a pig on it, 2)letterbox letter set for each child with the letters: r, i, p, g, s, t, n, h, k. 3)A copy of the book "Sick Pig" for each child, 4)Sheet with letterboxes for each child, 5)pencils, 6)primary paper, 7)large piece of white paper and marker for teacher, 8) glue. Review: "We have already discussed the vowels short /a/ and short /e/. Each of these vowels has its own sound, /a/ and /e/. The same is true for the vowel we will study today, the short /i/." 1."Everyone repeat after me, /i/. Good now, everyone say pig, now say sick pig. Good job. Notice, when you say /i/, your mouth is wide like you are smiling". 2. I will then put up the large white paper on the board and write "short /i/" on the top of it with a marker. I will ask the children, "Tell me which words you hear the /i/ sound in: tin or tan, big or bog, fish or snail, is or as?" When the children answer, I will write the /i/ words they chose on the piece of paper to hang up in the classroom. 3.I will have the children take out a piece of their primary paper. I will model how to write the short /i/ or lowercase /i/ on the lines on the blackboard. I will ask the children to watch me make my /i/ and then we will all try one together. After they watch me form my first one then I will ask them to pick up their pencils and try it with me, "Start at on the bottom line and go up just over the middle line. Then pick up your pencil and form a dot above the line you just made. Good job. Now every one, take about five minutes and practice writing your own all by yourself". 4. When the children are finished I will instruct them to add their practice sheet they just made to their alphabet book for future reference. Then we will all take out our copy of "Sick pig". I will say, "I am going to read this book out loud to you and I want you to follow along and take note of the words that have the short /i/ in them. You can even use your pencil to make a mark beside those words". I will proceed to read the book out loud to the class. 5. Assessment: "Now, class, you have seen the short /i/ writing in word form and you have heard it spoken. Now we are going to do an activity, so take out your letterbox sheets and your letters out of the baggy. I want you to form the words I call out in the appropriate letter boxes. Arrange the letters so that they form the word I say. This is just like what we do in small group reading. Now, after you are sure that the words are spelled correctly, glue the letters into the boxes and turn your sheets in. Remember to put your name on them". The words will be: is (2), hit (3), pig (3), rip (3), tin (3), pink (4). 6. When the children are finished with their assessment sheets then they will be instructed to individually reread "Sick Pig" and again silently make note of the words with short /i/. Murray, Bruce. However, according to the Basic Law of Saudi Arabia adopted by royal decree in 1992, the king must comply with Sharia (Islamic law) and the Quran, while the Quran and the Sunnah (the traditions of Muhammad) are declared to be the country's constitution. The primary source of law is the Islamic Sharia derived from the teachings of the Qur'an and the Sunnah (the traditions of the Prophet). Saudi Arabia is unique among modern Muslim states in that Sharia is not codified and there is no system of judicial precedent, giving judges the power to use independent legal reasoning to make a decision. Saudi judges tend to follow the principles of the Hanbali school of jurisprudence (or "fiqh") found in pre-modern texts and noted for its literalist interpretation of the Qur'an and hadith. Title: Finding Coefficients in Polynomial Expressions: A Fun Activity Grade school students love playing with building blocks. Let's imagine we have different colored building blocks representing x, x , x , ..., up to x . We are asked to find the number of ways to stack these blocks to make a tower that is 19 stories high using five identical sets of our building blocks. This problem is actually similar to finding the coefficient of x nine in the expansion of (x + x + x + x + x + x ) ! First, let's understand the given expression: (x + x + x + x + x + x ). It means that we have six types of building blocks: one story tall, two stories tall, three stories tall, and so on. Our goal is to build a 19-story tower by repeating the process of selecting and stacking one block from each set without worrying about their colors (or variables). Let's try visualizing this task step-by-step instead of diving into complex calculations. When combining blocks from all six sets, some towers will be too short, like those reaching only eight stories, while others may exceed the desired height of nineteen stories. However, when done correctly, we should reach exactly twenty unique heights that include our target of x . Now comes the fun part - counting the possibilities! To create a tower of height 19, consider various combinations that result in this total. Some examples could be: * Taking the sixth-power block once (x ), then twice the cube (2x ), followed by four times the first-power block (4x): (x * 2x * 4x = 16x ) * Picking the fifth-power block once (x ), thrice the square (3x ), followed by twice the second-power unit (2x ): (x * 3x * 2x = 6x ) Continue examining other possible combinations until you obtain ten outcomes where the exponent adds up to 19. Remember not to double count since order matters here - changing the sequence results in a different arrangement. After listing down all valid options, add up their coefficients outside the parentheses. The final sum represents the sought-after coefficient of x within the expanded equation! In summary, finding the coefficient of a specific term in polynomial expressions doesn't require advanced knowledge but rather clever thinking and systematic exploration. By relating it to familiar concepts like stacking building blocks, young learners grasp abstract mathematical ideas more easily, making learning enjoyable and engaging. Student Guide: Coin Toss-up If you toss a coin, there is a fifty-fifty chance it will land tails-side up. But what if you toss it five times: can you predict how often you'll get one tails and four heads versus three tails and two heads? In this activity, use a coin and some graph paper to explore how the accuracy of predictions is influenced by sample size. - Probability: A number that describes how likely an event is to happen. - Independence: Two events, A and B, are independent from each other if, when A happens, it does not affect the probability of B happening. - Random process: A process, like flipping a coin several times, where there can be several different possible results, and the different results may or may not be equally likely. - Histogram: A graph that shows the distribution of data. To do this activity you will need: - A coin (one you choose as the best for flipping) - Data tables (below) - Pencil (1) - Graph paper (2 sheets) - Practice flipping the coin you choose. Try to flip it the same way each time. You will use this coin for the duration of the experiment. - Flip the coin five times and record if it's heads or tails in line 1 of Table 1. For example, a result in this series of heads, tails, heads, tails, and tails could be written as HTHTT. |Coin flip data| - Repeat step 2 nine more times so that lines 1 through 10 of the table record the results of each series of five coin flips. - After you finish flipping the coin, notice that there are six combinations possible when flipping a coin. You can have: five heads; four heads and one tails; three heads and two tails; two heads and three tails; one heads and four tails; and five tails. - Count how many you have of each combination, and record the information in the table below under the first column. |4 Heads, 1 Tails| |3 Heads, 2 Tails| |2 Heads, 3 Tails| |1 Heads, 4 Tails| |Count data for each combination of heads and tails| - Using a sheet of graph paper, make a bar graph of the data from this table. Label the x-axis Combination, and the y-axis Count. This kind of graph is called a histogram. - What does the graph look like? Which combination had the highest count? - Now flip the coin five times again, repeating for ten additional series (#11 through #20), recording the heads/tails data in lines 11 through 20 of Table 1. Count how many you have of each combination, and record those numbers in Table 2 under the second column. - In Table 2, add the numbers for each combination from the first series column and the second series column, then put the sum in the third column (Total Count), for a total of 20 series. - Using a sheet of graph paper, make a bar graph of the data from the third column in Table 2. Label the x-axis Combination, and the y-axis Count. What is the graph shaped like? - Do you see a difference between the histogram with ten series and the histogram with 20 series? What does this tell you about making predictions based on a small number of series? Downloads and Links - Coin Toss-up Student Guide PDF Do you know who I love from you too? Title: How to "Ipodize" a Photo Introduction: The term "Ipodize" refers to adding a border around an image or photo to give it the appearance of being displayed on an iPod's screen. This can be a fun way to add some personality and flair to your photos before sharing them with others. With just a few simple steps using popular graphic design software like Adobe Photoshop or GIMP, you too can learn how to "Ipodize" any photo! Step 1: Choose Your Image Select the photo that you would like to edit and open it up in your preferred graphic design software. Make sure that the resolution is high enough so that when you crop it down to fit within the borders of the iPod display, there will still be sufficient detail remaining. Key Tip: Aim for at least 720x720 pixels as this is roughly equivalent to the size of the original iPod Touch's display. If you plan on printing out the final product, consider starting off with even higher resolutions. Step 2: Create Guides Using the ruler tool (in both Photoshop and GIMP), create vertical and horizontal guides where they intersect at the center point of your canvas. These lines should divide your workspace into nine equal sections. The middle section represents the actual viewing area of the iPod screen. Guideline: Keep these guide lines visible throughout the entire process, as they serve as reference points for positioning elements accurately. Step 3: Add Background Color Choose a color that matches the back casing of the iPod model you want to emulate - black or white are usually safe bets. Fill the outer two rows and columns created by the guides with this chosen background color. Key Tip: Use the paint bucket tool to quickly fill large areas with solid colors. Step 4: Design the Border Now we need to add details that mimic the look of an iPod's click wheel and home button. Using the ellipse selection tool, draw a circle slightly smaller than the space reserved for the home button. Then, feather the edges of the selection to soften its outline. Afterwards, fill the selected area with gray. Repeat this process to create the metallic ring surrounding the home button. Guideline: To ensure consistency across multiple images, save these shapes as custom brushes or patterns for easy reuse later on. Step 5: Position the Home Button Align the home button properly within the designated space below the central square. Remember to maintain alignment relative to the guide lines drawn earlier. Step 6: Draw the Click Wheel Next, let's move onto creating the click wheel. Start by drawing four circles along the edge of the bottom row using the ellipse selection tool. Two should be larger than the other two; arrange them accordingly to represent the 'previous', 'play/pause', 'next' buttons and the touch-sensitive surface of the click wheel itself. Again, remember to feather the edges of the selections. Step 7: Final Touches Lastly, place your main image inside the central square. You may wish to apply certain effects such as drop shadows or gradients to enhance realism. Save your work once satisfied. Conclusion: With patience and practice, anyone can master the technique of "Ipodizing" their favorite photos. Not only does this provide a unique twist to ordinary pictures, but also allows one to express creativity through digital manipulation. Enjoy experimenting with different designs and variations! Ma XXX got me a new XXX so it will't leak [? Break]! I think I keep it for myself. Heisenberg did not make the grids like this. He just did the math that would let him get the intensities he was looking for. But to do that he had to multiply two amplitudes (how high a wave measures) to work out the intensity. (In classical physics, intensity equals amplitude squared.) He made an odd-looking equation to handle this problem, wrote out the rest of his paper, handed it to his boss, and went on vacation. Dr. Born looked at his funny equation and it seemed a little crazy. He must have wondered, "Why did Heisenberg give me this strange thing? Why does he have to do it this way?" Then he realized that he was looking at a blueprint for something he already knew very well. He was used to calling the grid or table that we could write by doing, for instance, all the math for frequencies, a matrix. And Heisenberg's weird equation was a rule for multiplying two of them together. Max Born was a very, very good mathematician. He knew that since the two matrices (grids) being multiplied represented different things (like position (x,y,z) and momentum (mv), for instance), then when you multiply the first matrix by the second you get one answer and when you multiply the second matrix by the first matrix you get another answer. Even though he did not know about matrix math, Heisenberg already saw this "different answers" problem and it had bothered him. But Dr. Born was such a good mathematician that he saw that the difference between the first matrix multiplication and the second matrix multiplication was always going to involve Planck's constant, h, multiplied by the square root of negative one, i. So within a few days of Heisenberg's discovery they already had the basic math for what Heisenberg liked to call the "indeterminacy principle." By "indeterminate" Heisenberg meant that something like an electron is just not pinned down until it gets pinned down. It is a little like a jellyfish that is always squishing around and cannot be "in one place" unless you kill it. Later, people got in the habit of calling it "Heisenberg's uncertainty principle," which made many people make the mistake of thinking that electrons and things like that are really "somewhere" but we are just uncertain about it in our own minds. That idea is wrong. It is not what Heisenberg was talking about. Having trouble measuring something is a problem, but it is not the problem Heisenberg was talking about. He was very scared, but the lion was very big and hot. "Please don't be scared," said the lion. "I won't hurt you. I'm just here to help you." Ben was a little bit relieved, but he was still scared. He thought about what the lion had said and decided that he would always think before he peeked. Once upon a time, there was a little train. The train loved to chug along its track. One day, the train saw a big bucket. The bucket was empty, so the train decided to fill it up with water. The train chugged and chugged, until the bucket was full. But then, the train saw a little bird. The bird had hurt its wing and couldn't fly. The train felt sad for the bird. So, the train decided to take the bucket of water and pour it on the ground. The train made a big puddle and the bird was happy. The train felt good for helping the bird. From that day on, the train always looked for ways to help others. In the water and this elephant [laughs]. Up Up and Away With Reading Independence and Fluency By: Laura Lee Nevins Reading Fluency is the ability to read at a fast, smooth pace. One proven way to improve reading fluency is to reread decodable words in connected texts several times. In many instances, the more a student works with a particular text, the more familiar they become with it, and therefore the more fluent they will become which is the purpose of this lesson. This lesson focuses on students' development of the ability to read quickly, smoothly, and expressively. <!1. Fluency Literacy checklist for each student (see below) 2. Whiteboard with the sentence, The girl hits the ball fast, written on it 3. Pencils for each student 4. Cover-up critters for each student (Popsicle stick per student with two googly-eyes) 5. One copy per child of Kite Day at Pine Lake by Shelia Cushman & Rona Kornblum c1990 6. Assessment checklist given by the teacher to check speed, smoothness, and fluency. The checklist will contain the following information: how many words the child read in one minute, how many words the child read accurately, what words the child struggled with Fluency Literacy Checklist: Name: ____________________ Evaluator: _________________Date: ___________ I noticed that my partner After 2nd After 3rd Remembered more words like the elephant _______ _______ Read Faster like the cheetah _______ _______ Read Smoother like the swan _______ _______ Read with expression like the monkey _______ ______ "Today we are going to work on a reading skill called fluency. Fluency is when you can read fast, smoothly, and also with expression. If we want to become fluent readers we must practice. I am going to read this sentence on the board for the first time, so I will not be reading it fluently. "Th-e g-ir-l h-i-t-s th-e b-a-ll f-a-s-t." I read that slowly and it was hard to understand, now listen to me read it fluently. "The girl hits the ball fast." That was faster and easier to understand. The difference between reading the two sentences shows the importance of fluency. I got better because I learned all the words. After I read it over and over, I got better. Have the children practice reading the sentence, until they hear how they went from non fluent reading to fluent reading. Sometimes when we are reading, we will come to words that we do not know. When that happens remember to use your cover up critter, or cross check by reading the rest of the sentence. To use your cover up critter, cover up all the letters except the vowel, for example when decoding the word "duck" cover the d and ck, and start with the u sound then add the other letters. Now I will pass out the book Kite Day at Pine Lake to each student. Has anyone ever flown a kite before? I love to fly kites. This book is a story about children who love to fly kites. They have kites of all shapes, sizes, and colors. One child is upset though because he does not have a kite. I wonder what will happen. Let's read and find out! Students will read the book on their own. Afterwards, we will discuss the story as a class and ask questions to see their comprehension and understanding of the text. They will each have a chance to read it by themselves before reading it aloud with a partner. After they have read and comprehended the story once individually, I will divide the class into pairs. Each pair will be given two check lists. One partner will be the reader and the other one will be the recorder. The reader will read the book three different times. The recorder will write down a report after the reader has read the book for the second and third time. The report will be marking on the checklist. The recorder can either mark that the reader remembered more words like the elephant read faster like the cheetah, read smoother like the swan, or read with expression like the monkey. The partners will then switch roles and do the same thing again. In order to assess each student, I will call them up to me one by one and have them read from the same book for one minute to check their fluency. I will have a checklist to record their speed, smoothness, and fluency. The checklist will contain the following information: how many words the child read in one minute, how many words the child read accurately, what words the child struggled with. By doing this, I will know the areas that the child is having difficulties with. While each student is doing their assessment with me, the rest of the students will be completing their fluency checklist with their partners. Cusman, Shelia and Kornblum, Rona. Title: Find North with a Stick and a Watch: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: In this extensive guide, we will walk you through the process of finding north using nothing more than a stick and a watch. This method, known as the shadow-stick method or Scott's Shadow Method, is an easy yet accurate way to determine directions without any specialized equipment. By following these steps, you can confidently find your bearings during outdoor activities such as hiking, camping, or orienteering. Materials Needed: 1. A stick (approximately 30 cm or 12 inches long) 2. An analog watch (digital watches are not suitable for this method) Step 1: Prepare Your Materials Gather your materials - a stick and an analog watch. If possible, choose a clear day with minimal cloud cover for optimal results. Make sure that your watch is set correctly to local standard time; if necessary, adjust for daylight saving time. Key Tip: Avoid using a watch with hands that glow in the dark, as they may affect accuracy due to artificial light interference. Step 2: Plant the Stick Plant the stick vertically into level ground so that its top does not exceed knee height. The end pointing towards the sky should be unobstructed by leaves or branches above. Mark the tip of the stick's shadow with a small stone or other marker. Explanation: As sunlight moves across the sky, it casts shadows from objects on the ground. We mark the initial position of the shadow created by the stick, which indicates roughly westward direction at solar noon. Step 3: Wait for Approximately 15 Minutes Allow around fifteen minutes for the sun and Earth's rotation to cause the shadow's tip to move noticeably. During this waiting period, avoid disturbing the stick or moving away from the marked spot. Step 4: Draw a Line Through the Shadows Using another stick or a piece of string, draw a straight line connecting the first mark (the original shadow tip) and the current tip of the shadow. You now have an approximate east-west line. Explanation: Since the sun rises in the east and sets in the west, creating a line between two consecutive positions of the shadow allows us to estimate the general direction of those points on the compass. Step 5: Locate North With the east-west line established, point the hour hand of your analog watch directly at the sun. Bisect the angle formed by the hour hand and the twelve o'clock mark on your watch face. The resulting line points approximately north-south. Explanation: When using a properly functioning analog watch, aligning the hour hand with the sun creates an imaginary line connecting the center of the watch and the sun. Dividing this angle in half provides an estimated north-south axis based on the earth's rotational orientation relative to the sun. Step 6: Confirm Direction Using Common Sense Double-check your findings against common sense observations such as recognizing nearby landmarks, observing prevailing winds, or noting patterns of vegetation growth. Adjust accordingly if needed. Conclusion: By carefully following these steps, you can accurately find north using only a simple stick and an analog watch. Remember that while this method offers reasonable precision under favorable conditions, factors like atmospheric refraction and seasonal variations might introduce minor discrepancies. Nonetheless, mastering this technique enhances self-reliance and navigation skills when venturing outdoors without advanced tools or instruments. Do you think you could make me some dinner? And at the bottom there's a bow. But once they came up with a plan. While the settlement was growing in New South Wales, it was also growing in Tasmania. True, a lesson is supposedly left for you to use by the teacher, but sometimes ... you need a filler or a replacement. Here's one that will come in handy sooner or later. It works best for grades 5-10 but can be used in grades 3-12. It was a dark and stormy night ... 55 minutes: If the desks are not already lined up in rows, direct the kids to quickly push their desks into rows. Tell the kids to get out some paper and a pencil. Direct your students to write their name at the top of their paper. Then, write down the following - It was a dark and stormy night in (subject) when suddenly .... Tell the kids they each have 1 minute to continue this story. When time is up, they are to hand their story to the person behind them who will have 1 minute to continue the story. (The last person in the row brings their story to the first person in the row each round.) Continue this until all stories end up in their original place. Authors will then have 2 minutes to quickly read what is there and finish the story. Sound the clock, and go. When this activity is finished, ask if anyone would like to share "their" story with the class. If nobody volunteers, pick somebody. The stories are hysterical. Adjusting for a shorter period of time: If you only have 10-15 minutes, adjust like this. Do not rearrange the desks. Do not tell them to write their names at the top. They don't need them. Tell the kids to get out some paper and a pencil. Direct your students to write down the following - It was a dark and stormy night in (subject) when Tell the kids they have 1 minute to start this story. Then, they are to quickly hand their paper off and get one from someone else. You will help grab and distribute. Speed counts. You're on the clock. The next person has 1 minute. The last person finishes the story. This is a slightly different approach, but it works as well. Here are more tips to help make your experience a Anyway so it was pretty clear to everybody. Dry ice exposed to normal air gives off carbon dioxide gas that is odorless and colorless. A female butterfly will lay her eggs on or near the food plant of the caterpillar (the food plant is the plant that the caterpillar feeds on). The female will choose a place to lay her eggs using smell, taste, touch, and sight. Most species will lay just one egg on the food plant. Others will lay groups of five to over 100 eggs on the food plant. Most species will lay their eggs on the leaves of the food plant. In some places, the word "bungalow" is used for any house that is all on one level. The word came from India and for a long time was used for a house that is built all on one level and has a verandah where people can sit or work outdoors, but under a shady roof. A bungalow often has a hall down the middle of the house to let the breeze blow through. Bungalows are often seen in countries with hot summers, in India, South East Asia, South Africa, parts of the United States, South America, Australia and New Zealand. In regions with flooding, a bungalow is often built up on wooden "stilts" or a high basement. In the 1800s, bungalows were nearly always built of wood, but from the 1920s it became fashionable to build them of brick as well. [the mom mimics the sound of the child.] The term identity means the mental image someone has of themself as well as some kind of similarity with others in some particular way. Gender identity, specifically, refers to the experiences someone has that are similar to a person of a particular gender. This gender identity affects how a person views themself as well as how they interact with others. Developmental psychologists have found that the development of an identity begins in early adolescence. Learning about what it means to be a particular gender also starts at an early age. During adolescence, children begin to internalize the gender roles in their surroundings and "gender intensification" occurs. Gender intensification means that the child feels more pressure to conform to socially sanctioned gender norms, resulting in deeper gender role differentiation for boys and girls. Biological, can affect gender identity development as well. Research done with animals has shown that pre-natal sex hormones affect the sexual differentiation of the brain. [holding index finger up as Mr brown] Growing Independence and Fluency While students are learning to read, they must also learn to read with consistency, fluency, and while using expression. Students will be doing three different one minute reads one the same text. Students will keep track and record their progress. 1. A stop watch for every group of students 2. A class set of Sam's Trip of the Doc 3. 1minute read charts for everyone 4. Progress chart 6. A Popsicle sticks with eyes on it for every child in the class to help them follow along in the text. 1."Today we are going to learn about reading fluently. When you are a fluent reader you read with expression, and also smoothly. If you can do both of these things as you read it will help you comprehend what you are reading. Just as a sailboat sailing through the ocean, we want our reading to be nice and smooth." 2."When you first see a sentence it might be hard to read because it is unfamilier. The first time reading a sentence might go: ttthhheeeeyyyyyy gggggeeeeettttttt tttthhhhheeeee dddddoooocccccccc ffffaasssssssttttttttt. The second read would be even better: tthheeyy ggeettt ttooo ttthheee ddoocc ffaasstt. Then by the time the third read happens the words are going to be very familiar and will also be easier to read: They get to the doc fast." 3."We are all going to practice using our cover up critters together. When you come to a word that you don't know we are going to decode it. Using your cover up critter I want everyone to decode the word itch. Slowly uncover your word as you recognize the sounds that the letters make. Blend the sounds together to read your unfamiliar word. 4."Now I want everyone to take the stopwatch and get with your partner. I want everyone to take turns reading Sam's Trip to the Doc. The first person will read the story while the other partner times to see how many words they can read in 1 minute. You will do these three times while recording your data. As soon as the first person finishes reading three times, then y'all can switch roles and do it again. I want everyone to make sure there partner is reading with expression, and smoothly (Model a read aloud that is smooth and demonstrates expression). Now we are going to read Sam's Trip to the doc. I wonder why Sam had to go to the doctor. Let's all begin reading to find out. The students will be using their one minute read aloud charts to check their own improvement. The students will also be assessing each other based on the fluency chart. Each student will be told to read smoothly and with expression. 1. The reading genie website (developing Reading Fluency) 2. Title: How to Deal with a Hypochondriac Hypochondria, also known as illness anxiety disorder, is a condition where a person is excessively worried about having a serious illness, despite having no or only mild symptoms. Dealing with a hypochondriac can be challenging, but there are ways to support them while maintaining your own well-being. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to deal with a hypochondriac: ### Step 1: Educate Yourself Before you do anything else, take some time to learn about hypochondria. Understanding the condition will help you empathize with the person and respond appropriately to their concerns. Read books, articles, and reliable online resources about hypochondria, its causes, symptoms, and treatments. This knowledge will enable you to provide better support and avoid unintentionally exacerbating their anxieties. Key Tip: When researching, look for reputable sources such as medical journals, academic websites, and mental health organizations. Avoid relying solely on anecdotal evidence or personal blogs. ### Step 2: Validate Their Feelings When someone with hypochondria expresses their fears, validate their feelings instead of dismissing them outright. Acknowledge that they're scared or anxious and offer comfort. Let them know you understand why they might feel this way, even if you don't believe their worries are warranted. Validation doesn't mean you agree with their assessment; it simply means you recognize their emotions are genuine. Key Guideline: Use phrases like "I can see why you're concerned," "That sounds really scary," or "It makes sense that you're feeling this way." These responses show empathy without reinforcing any catastrophic thinking. ### Step 3: Encourage Professional Help Gently suggest seeking professional help from a mental health provider who specializes in anxiety disorders. Explain that therapy can teach coping strategies and cognitive behavioral techniques to manage hypochondriasis. If they're already seeing a therapist, encourage them to discuss their fears openly during sessions. Key Tip: Be supportive and non-confrontational when bringing up professional help. Emphasize that therapy isn't a punishment or a sign of weakness but rather a tool for improving overall well-being. ### Step 4: Set Boundaries [continues to play with the cups and plates] Now let's get into this oatmeal. In the United States, a high school is a school that students go to usually for grades 9 through 12, from the ages of about 14-15 to about 17-18. It is also the last school that the law requires the student to go to. However, students with behavioral issues that are too much for the school to handle are not required to attend. This is called expulsion. Some states have an age or certain grade where a student does not have to attend school, this is called the compulsory age, for example in New York, students below 17 years of age are required to attend school unless the child is excused. Some people may choose to take some tests called GED (General Education Development) as another way to finish high school. A student is allowed by law to stop going to high school after a certain age without having graduated. In most this is 16, however, in a few states, it is 18 or higher, for example, Wisconsin. Title: How to Complete an Aerial (Football) An aerial or head ball is a common technique used in football where players use their heads to control, pass, or shoot the ball. Mastering this skill can significantly improve your performance as a player. This article will provide you with a comprehensive guide on completing an aerial, including detailed steps, helpful tips, and important safety guidelines. **Step 1: Assess the Situation** Before attempting an aerial, assess the situation carefully. Consider factors such as distance from other players, angle of approach, speed of play, and positioning of both the ball and yourself. If there are multiple options available, choose one that provides better chances of success while minimizing risks of injury. *Key Tip*: Always keep your eyes open when performing aerials. It's crucial for maintaining balance, judging distances accurately, and anticipating potential collisions. **Step 2: Position Your Body Correctly** Proper body positioning sets the foundation for successful aerials. Stand straight with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent. Lean forward at about 15 degrees relative to the ground. Keep your weight centered over your hips rather than shifting it towards your toes or heels. *Guideline*: Avoid leaning back; doing so could cause loss of balance and increase the risk of injury. **Step 3: Approach the Ball Properly** As the ball approaches, start running towards it while keeping your eye on its trajectory. Maintain your posture and continue looking up until contact. Try to match the ball's pace by adjusting your stride length and frequency. *Key Tip*: Start sprinting early enough so you don't have to rush or lunge during impact. **Step 4: Prepare for Impact** Once close enough, lift your dominant leg off the ground, bending it at the knee. At the same time, extend your arms outwards for balance. The non-dominant arm should be raised higher than the dominant one for stability. *Guideline*: Don't swing your arms excessively - controlled movements help maintain balance and reduce strain. **Step 5: Make Contact With the Ball** Aim to hit the middle of the ball using your forehead. To do this, tilt your chin down just before making contact. Use your neck muscles to propel the force required for controlling, passing, or shooting. *Important Safety Measure*: Never aim for any part of the body other than the ball. Striking another player intentionally or unintentionally may lead to severe injuries. **Step 6: Follow Through** After hitting the ball, allow your momentum to carry you through. Continue moving after landing to avoid being caught off guard by opponents. Remember to cushion your fall by bending your knees upon landing. *Key Tip*: Practice following through consistently to ensure accuracy and power in your aerials. **Step 7: Recover Quickly** Immediately get back into playing mode once you land. Scan the field, regain possession if possible, and communicate effectively with teammates. *Guideline*: Stay alert even after executing a successful aerial since subsequent plays often occur rapidly. Remember, practice makes perfect. Regular training sessions focusing specifically on aerials can greatly enhance your proficiency. However, always prioritize safety above all else. Consult coaches, trainers, or medical professionals if unsure about specific techniques or experiencing discomfort during practice. I wish I could stick it up on the wall. Title: Extend the Life of Your Phone Battery: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: A phone's battery life is crucial for maintaining connectivity, productivity, and entertainment. This guide will provide you with an in-depth understanding of various methods that can help extend your phone battery's lifespan. By following these steps, you ensure optimal performance while reducing wear and tear on the battery over time. Section 1: Understanding Battery Types and Their Lifespans Before diving into the ways to prolong battery life, let us first understand different types of batteries used in smartphones and their typical lifespans. The two primary battery technologies are lithium-ion (Li-ion) and lithium polymer (LiPo). Both have similar characteristics but slightly varying chemistries. On average, they last between 300 to 500 charge cycles before reaching 80% of their original capacity. One charge cycle refers to charging a battery from 0% to 100%. Keep this information in mind as we discuss extending battery life since some techniques involve managing charge cycles effectively. Section 2: Adjusting Display Settings Step 1: Lower Screen Brightness Smartphone screens consume significant power. Reducing screen brightness can significantly impact overall battery usage. Most devices offer automatic brightness adjustment based on ambient lighting conditions; however, manually lowering the brightness setting when indoors or in dimly lit areas could save more energy. Key Tip: Use auto-brightness during daylight hours to balance visibility and power consumption efficiently. Step 2: Shorten Screen Timeout Duration Screen timeout determines how long your device stays active after a period of inactivity. Setting shorter durations reduces unnecessary display wakefulness, thus saving battery life. Navigate through your phone settings to find 'Display' or 'Screen Timeout' options and select the shortest viable duration for your needs. Guideline: Choose a timeout duration that suits your habits without causing frequent interruptions due to screen locking. Section 3: Managing Background App Refresh and Location Services Step 1: Disable Background App Refresh for Unused Applications Background app refresh allows apps to update content even when they aren't actively being used. While convenient, this feature consumes considerable battery resources. To manage background app refresh: a. Go to your phone's settings menu. b. Locate 'General' or 'Battery & Device Care' sections. A row or terraced house (see the picture in the introduction) is a house that is part of a row of houses joined at the side walls. Many cities and towns have thousands of row houses because they are a good way to build lots of houses close together. Many row houses have two rooms downstairs and two rooms upstairs, with a kitchen or wash area at the back. Houses like this were built for poor factory workers and coal miners in many cities and towns. "Terrace houses" are a type of row house where each house has a similar front to its neighbours. Doc on top Beginning Reader Lesson Anne Kimbell Grant Reading fluency is very important to improvement of reading comprehension, which is the ultimate goal of reading instruction. In order for a student to become a fluent and skillful reader, the student must learn to recognize correspondences in words. In this lesson, students will be learning the o=/o/ correspondence. They will learn to recognize o=/o/ in words. Mastering short vowel sounds is imperative before moving on to more difficult correspondences, and to becoming a more fluent reader. 1. Elkonin letterboxes 2. letter manipulatives (o, f, f, n, i, t, l, c, k, p, h, s, r, g, b, x) 3. primary paper 5. The Big Top 6. poster board with Doc had a sock on the top of the pot. 1. First, I will review the short vowel correspondences to be learned before o=/o/. I will do this by playing a game with the children. I will tell them that I am looking at a b-a-t. Can anyone tell me what this is? Then I will continue until I feel that the children have a grasp on the short vowels. I will ask the students to tell me what short vowel sounds we have learned so far, and have them give me an example. 2. Today, we are going to learn a new short vowel sound. It is the /o/ sound. The letter o makes the /o/ sound. You make this sound when the doctor tells you to open your mouth wide and say /o/. Try it with me. Open your mouth really wide and make the /o/ sound with your throat. Ready.../o/. Great job! 3. I will have poster board with a tongue twister written on it. Now, we are going to learn a tongue twister with our new sound /o/ in it. Listen closely as I say it. Olly the octopus yelled olly olly oxenfree. Now, everyone say it with me and make sure to make the /o/ sound you make at the doctor. Olly the octopus yelled olly olly oxenfree. Great job! Now, let s stretch out the /o/ sound. Ooolly the oooctopus yelled ooolly ooolly oooxenfree. Good work! I really heard the /o/ that time. 4. Everyone take out your letterboxes and your letters. I will remind you how this works. I will call out a word, and you spell it on your letterbox. I will model the example b-o-x for them. The following are the words I will use for the letterbox: 2 [on, off, it] 3 [lock, pot, hop, box, fit] 4 [frog, stop, flip] I will tell the students how many boxes to have showing. Then, I will say the words that are appropriate for that number of boxes. I will walk around and check to see how everyone is doing and scaffold as needed. 5. Next, I will spell the words for the children on the whiteboard and have them read the words together for assessment and understanding. 6. I will tell them to get out your primary paper and pencil because we are going to write a message. We are going to write about frogs hopping. You can write anything you want about them, but remember that when you write the o in frog that it makes the /o/ sound. Try to think of other words that have the /o/ sound in them in your message or somewhere else on your paper. I will also have the students to copy the words from our letterbox lesson that are on the boards onto their primary paper. 7. Then I will pass out copies of The Big Top to every pair of students. I will have them take turns reading to each other as I walk around the room. I will provide help (scaffold) as needed. After they have finished, I will have each group of children write two sentences about the book. 8. I will have the students come to the front of the room and sit down. I will reread The Big Top to them. I will tell them that when they hear a word with the doctor sound /o/ in it to raise their hands. I will observe the students throughout the lesson. I will have a checklist on what to look for in every student s understanding. I will also take up their journal and check to see if they copied the words correctly and to make sure they made all of their letters correctly. That way I can work with a student if he/she is not printing correctly. -best effort given Eldredge, J. Lloyd. Teaching Decoding in Holistic Classroom. Prentice Hall Publishing Company. Upper Saddle River, NJ: 1995. Murray, B. A. and T. Lesniak. The Letterbox Lesson: A Hands-on Approach for Teaching Decoding. The Reading Teacher. 1999. 644-650. What to do - Write the letters H and h on the board; make them at least a foot tall. Alternatively, use letter cards large enough for the whole group to see easily. - The sound for these two letters is the same. What's the sound for this letter? Point to the lowercase h. Good. So what's the sound for this letter? Point to the uppercase H. Right! This is called a capital letter. Remember, when you say /h/ (Say the /h/ sound as in hot.), your mouth is open and air comes out: /h/. Again: what's the sound? - Look for students who are not saying the sound. Ask them: What's the sound? Look for students who are making the wrong sound and model the sound for them until they have it right. Well done everyone. - We use the /H/ sound to begin words like hand, help, her, him, house. Can you tell me some other words that begin with /H/? - Erase H and h. Now write 12 letters on the board (arrange them randomly): 4 of the letters should be H and they should be interspersed with 8 other letters dissimilar in appearance to H, such as y and u. Don't include lowercase h. - When I point to the letter we just learned, say its sound. When I point to any other letter, you have to stay quiet. My turn first. Point to a series of letters and either say the sound or make a performance of saying nothing, as appropriate. - Your turn. Ready? Point to letters randomly, holding on each one for a few seconds. - If a student says the sound for one of the other letters (not H), point to H and say: You only need to make a sound for this letter. When I point to any other letter, stay quiet. Ready? Look for individuals who are saying nothing when you point to H. Have those students try letters individually until they have it (but don't call only on struggling students). Keep going until everyone has it. [looking in a pocket on one of the pages] [removes a toy car from the container] Title: How to Buy a Puppy Online from Bullie Pups R Us Introduction: Buying a puppy online can be an exciting experience, but it's essential to ensure that you are purchasing from a reputable breeder. This tutorial will guide you through the process of buying a puppy from Bullie Pups R Us, a trusted source for quality Bulldogs and Frenchies. By following these steps, you'll bring home a healthy, happy new family member while supporting responsible breeding practices. Step 1: Research Breed Information Before making any decisions, familiarize yourself with the breed standards, temperament, exercise needs, grooming requirements, and potential health issues of both English Bulldogs and French Bulldogs. Understanding these factors will help you determine which breed is best suited for your lifestyle. Key tip: Consider consulting resources like the American Kennel Club (AKC) or the Canadian Kennel Club (CKC) for accurate information. Step 2: Visit the Bullie Pups R Us Website Navigate to <https://bulliepupsrus.com/> to explore available litters and learn more about their breeding program. Take note of the variety of colors and patterns offered, as well as any champion bloodlines. Browse customer testimonials and trustworthy reviews to gauge the credibility of the breeder. Step 3: Choose Your Perfect Puppy Narrow down your options based on gender, color, and pattern preferences. Keep in mind that certain coat colors may come with higher price tags due to rarity or demand. Remember to prioritize the overall health and temperament of the pup over its appearance. Step 4: Contact Bullie Pups R Us Reach out to Bullie Pups R Us via email or phone to express your interest in a specific puppy. Provide them with your name, contact details, and the puppy you have chosen. A representative will get back to you promptly to discuss availability and shipping logistics. Step 5: Complete Necessary Paperwork Once your puppy is reserved, you'll need to complete necessary paperwork, including a sales agreement and medical records release form. Be prepared to provide proof of age, identity, and residence. Review all documents carefully before signing to ensure understanding of terms and conditions. Step 6: Payment Options Payments can typically be made via credit card, PayPal, or bank transfer. Avoid sending cash or wire transfers, as these methods are not secure. Once payment has been processed, your puppy's microchip registration and AKC/CKC limited registration papers will be mailed to you. Step 7: Prepare for Shipping Your puppy will arrive via air transportation in a climate-controlled, pressurized cabin designed specifically for pet travel. To prepare, purchase an appropriate carrier, line it with soft bedding, and include familiar items such as toys or blankets. Make arrangements with a local veterinarian for a check-up upon arrival. Step 8: Welcome Home Your New Furry Friend! Upon pick up at the airport, greet your new furry friend calmly and quietly. Offer water immediately and allow time for adjustment to their new surroundings. Schedule playtime, cuddle sessions, and training exercises throughout the day to begin bonding with your new companion. Conclusion: By following this comprehensive tutorial, you're now equipped to confidently buy a puppy online from Bullie Pups R Us. Not only will you welcome a loving new addition to your household, but also support ethical breeding practices dedicated to preserving the integrity and health of the beloved Bulldog and Frenchie breeds. And then out of the sky they saw this spaceship thing land. Title: How to Find Trendy Clothes on a Budget Introduction: Shopping for trendy clothes doesn't have to break the bank. With some planning, creativity, and knowledge of resources, you can stay stylish without overspending. This guide will walk you through various steps to help you find fashionable clothing at affordable prices. Step 1: Define Your Style Determine your personal style by analyzing your current wardrobe, browsing fashion magazines or websites, and observing what styles you are naturally drawn to. Knowing your style will prevent impulse buying and ensure that every purchase aligns with your aesthetic. Key Tips: - Consider your lifestyle when defining your style; aim for versatile pieces suitable for work, casual outings, and special events. - Be open to experimentation but remain true to yourself. Step 2: Set a Realistic Budget Decide how much money you can afford to spend on new clothes monthly or annually. Allocate funds according to priority items (e.g., shoes, tops, jackets) based on your needs and preferences. Remember to factor in sales tax and shipping costs if applicable. Guidelines: - Review income, expenses, and savings goals before setting a budget. - Adjust spending limits as needed. Step 3: Shop Off-Season Sales Retailers often discount seasonal items once they go out of stock. By shopping during off-peak seasons, you can save significantly on high-quality garments. For example, buy winter coats in late February or early March and summer dresses in August. Key Tips: - Plan ahead by anticipating future trends and weather conditions. - Check store policies regarding returns and exchanges. Step 4: Utilize Thrift Stores and Consignment Shops Thrifting allows you access to gently used brand name and designer clothing at fractioned prices. Similarly, consignment shops offer previously owned high-end garments sold on behalf of their original owners. Both options provide an opportunity to discover hidden gems while supporting sustainable practices. Guidelines: - Allocate time for thorough searching since inventory changes frequently. - Inspect items carefully for wear, stains, or damage before purchasing. BURNEY Oh, Author of my being!-far more dear To me than light, than nourishment, or rest, Hygeia's blessings, Rapture's burning tear, Or the life-blood that mantles in my breast! If in my heart the love of Virtue glows, 'T was planted there by an unerring rule; From thy example the pure flame arose, Thy life, my precept,-thy good works, my school. Could my weak pow'rs thy num'rous virtues trace, By filial love each fear should be repress'd, The blush of Incapacity I'd chace, And stand, Recorder of thy worth, confess'd: But since my niggard stars that gift refuse, Concealment is the only boon I claim; Obscure be still the unsuccessful Muse, Who cannot raise, but would not sink, thy fame. Writing a Biography - Mark Twain Students will write a biography of Mark Twain. This lesson is for middle school age students, but fifth graders could do this, too. First, you will copy the worksheet so that each student will have a copy. Then, you will talk to the students about biographies. After that, you will have the students do research on Mark Twain. Title: How to Get the Hiccups Introduction: Hiccups are an involuntary contraction of the diaphragm muscle, followed by closure of the vocal cords, which produces the characteristic "hic" sound. While hiccups can be bothersome or even painful at times, they're generally harmless and resolve on their own within a few minutes to a couple of hours. However, if you're looking for ways to intentionally trigger hiccups, this guide will provide several methods with detailed steps and explanations. Please note that these techniques should be used responsibly and infrequently, as excessive induction of hiccups may lead to potential discomfort or health concerns. Once upon a time, there was a little bird named Tweet. Tweet liked to fly high in the sky and sing songs. One day, Tweet saw a big storm coming. Tweet started to panic because he didn't want to get wet. Tweet flew around in zigzag patterns, trying to find a safe place to hide. But the storm was too strong and Tweet felt weak. Suddenly, Tweet saw a big tree with lots of leaves. Tweet flew to the tree and hid under the leaves. The storm raged on for a long time, but Tweet was safe and dry under the tree. When the storm was over, Tweet flew out and sang a happy song. From that day on, Tweet knew that when he panicked, he just needed to find a safe place to hide. When they arrived, Clarice gasped with delight at all of the different kinds of desks. She couldn't decide which one to pick. Then, her Mom knelt down so they could look at them eye level. With her new desk, Clarice was able to have the space she needed to learn. She used her desk to look up the answers to her questions, draw her own answers and keep all of her supplies organized. To ensure an enjoyable and successful experience while playing StarCraft II, certain preparations are necessary. This comprehensive guide will help you get ready for your journey into the Koprulu sector, covering everything from system requirements and account creation to understanding the game's mechanics and strategies. Playing the game on a system below the minimum specifications may result in performance issues and hinder your enjoyment. Bypassing YouTube's regional filter can be a bit tricky, but with this detailed guide, you'll be able to access videos that are restricted in your region. This technique involves using a Proxy or Virtual Private Network (VPN) service to mask your IP address and make it appear as if you're browsing from a different location. **Disclaimer:** It is important to note that bypassing regional filters may violate YouTube's terms of service. Always respect copyright laws and licensing agreements. Use this information responsibly and ethically. **Step 1: Understand What a Proxy/VPN Service Is** A proxy server acts as an intermediary between your computer and the internet. When you connect to a website through a proxy, the request first goes to the proxy server which then forwards it to the target site. The response returns via the same path. A VPN does something similar but provides more security because all data traveling between your device and the VPN server is encrypted. **Key Tip**: Using a trusted, paid VPN service is recommended over free options due to better speeds, reliability, and stronger privacy policies. **Step 2: Choose a Suitable Proxy/VPN Service** There are numerous providers available; some popular ones include NordVPN, ExpressVPN, CyberGhost, etc. Look for features like high-speed servers, strong encryption, no logging policy, user-friendly interface, and support for multiple devices. **Guideline**: Before subscribing, check their refund policy so you can test the service without risk. Also, ensure they have servers located in countries where the video you want to watch isn't geo-blocked. **Step 3: Download & Install the Selected Service** Once chosen, download the appropriate app for your operating system from the provider's official website. After installation, open the application and sign in using your credentials. **Step 4: Connect to a Server Outside Your Region** In the list of servers provided by the VPN/proxy service, select one located outside the region restricting the YouTube video. Wait until the connection is established successfully. **Key Tip**: Servers closer to your physical location usually provide faster connections. If speed becomes an issue, try connecting to a nearby foreign server instead of one far away. **Step 5: Verify Your New IP Address** To confirm whether your IP has changed, visit websites like whatismyipaddress.com. Check if the displayed IP location matches the country you connected to via the VPN/proxy service. **Step 6: Access the Restricted Video on YouTube** Now go to YouTube and search for the previously inaccessible video. Instead of being blocked, you should now be able to play it. **Troubleshooting**: If it still doesn't work, clear your browser cache, cookies, and history, then restart your browser before trying again. **Step 7: Disconnect Once Done** After watching the video, remember to disconnect from the VPN/proxy server to save bandwidth and maintain privacy. Remember, while these steps allow you to circumvent regional restrictions, misuse could lead to account termination or other penalties. Therefore, always ensure any content you access complies with local laws and regulations. When did somebody throw a rock at your eye? A literally she had it in her mouth the whole time. The caterpillar sounded like me because I like to eat a lot. WORD PROCESSING TUTORIAL EXAMPLE: REPEAT PATTERNS Pattern is an underlying structure that organizes surfaces or structures in a consistent, regular manner. Pattern can be described as a repeating unit of shape or form, but it can also be thought of as the "skeleton" that organizes the parts of a composition. To enable the learners discover and exploit the patterns through nature and the objects around them. Use ICT as a different way in which their patterns can be This will enable the learner to gain skills in creating patterns from objects he/she is familiar with to form a harmonised piece of artwork. Qualities of a Good Repeat Pattern 1. A Repeat Pattern must have an objective and an aim. 2. It should solve a problem of it should be useful in one aspect or another. 3. It should be able to repeat or flow. Sources of inspiration for patterns can be: Animal skins (domestic and wild) Fruits and plants Musical Instruments (traditional Human activities like hunting, digging, Artists create patterns and textures using lines and shapes. 1. Students go out and draw any two types of fruits they wish to choose. These sketches are made in their sketch books in order to keep a record of their original work. 2. They can draw the basic shapes of the fruits applying textures 3. They could include the leaves to give their drawing a background. 4. After this, they can select from what they have drawn and make a neat and final drawing on a squared piece of paper. This can be 12cm by 12cm and is usually called a motif. This paper is to help them in achieving accurate dimensions when repeating their pattern. Hand made Repeat Pattern 1. On a large piece of paper, they can accurately divide it into 12cm by 16cm portions using a faint pencil. 2. After this, they can tress their motif drawings into the small portions on the large paper. Remember to rub out all the pencil marks when all is done. Using Word to make a Repeat Pattern 1. Using a scanner, scan the motif and save it as "Motif.bmp". 2. Open the file from MGI PhotoSuite. 3. From Edit menu, click on Select All. 4. From the Edit menu, click on Copy. 5. Open MS. Word. 6. From the Edit menu, click on Paste. 7. Repeat the Paste action four times. 8. Click and drag the images aligning them next to each other. 9. Holding down the Shift key down, click on each image you have pasted to select them all at once. 10. Still holding the Shift key, right click the mouse and select Grouping and click Group while pointing to the selected 11. Repeat the procedures the number of times you want. Changing the colours of the image 1. Scan the motif in the Gray mode. 2. Repeat the steps above. 3. Print out a copy of your work using a coloured printer. Note: Remember to save your work. Make a few quick drawings of a flower to use in the motif. Experiment with different textures and shades on your flower drawings and then transfer them onto the motif. Repeat the motif on a larger piece of paper, preferably coloured. A local Art museum has requested you to design new curtains for their windows, but they would like to see a sample piece of work that tyou could do. As an art student, design the sample curtain on paper using the repeat pattern methods. Print your results. Did Daddy use it a long time ago? One time there was a big farting caterpillar. Title: How to Cite Movies in APA Style The American Psychological Association (APA) citation format is commonly used for academic writing in the social sciences. When incorporating movies into your research or projects, you must adhere to specific guidelines to properly cite them according to APA rules. This tutorial will provide an in-depth explanation of how to create accurate citations for films, including key tips and best practices. ### Step 1: Gather necessary information about the movie To begin creating a proper APA reference entry for a film, collect the following details: * Movie title (italicized) * Director(s) name(s) * Production company * Year of release For example: Tarantino, Q., & Miramax Films. (1994). Pulp Fiction. Key tip: If there are multiple directors, list them in the order they appear in the credits, separated by an ampersand (&). ### Step 2: Format the title Italicize the title of the movie, followed by a period. Capitalize only the first letter of the first word in the title, subtitle, and any proper nouns. For instance: Spirited Away. However, do not italicize or put quotes around the names of studios or distributors. ### Step 3: List the director(s) Write the director's last name, followed by their initial(s), if known. Separate multiple directors with an ampersand (&). Insert a comma after each director's name. End this section with a period. Example: Nolan, C. J., & Snyder, D. Key guideline: Only include directors who have made significant contributions to the film; otherwise, omit their names from the citation. ### Step 4: Specify the production company Include the full name of the production company that released the movie. Abbreviate common words like 'Films', 'Productions', etc., when appropriate. Place a comma after the production company name and end with a period. Example: Warner Bros. Pictures, ### Step 5: Add the year of release State the year when the movie was initially distributed, enclosed in parentheses. Follow it immediately with a period outside the closing parenthesis. Example: (2017). Putting everything together, here's what a complete APA citation looks like for a single director: Inception. (2010). Christopher Nolan (Director), Warner Bros. Pictures. And for multiple directors: Pulp Fiction. (1994). Quentin Tarantino & Lawrence Bender (Producers), Miramax Films. Remember, these examples serve as general templates. Adapt them based on the number and roles of contributors involved in making the movie. Additional resources: * Publication Manual of the American Psychological Association (6th ed.) * APA Style website (<https://apastyle.apa.org/>) You want to go in the swimming pool. Title: How to Convert Square Meters to Square Feet and Vice Versa Introduction: Converting square meters to square feet or vice versa is a common task when dealing with international real estate, construction, interior design, and other fields where area measurements are necessary. This guide will provide you with clear, easy-to-follow steps to perform these conversions accurately and confidently. Tools required: * Calculator (preferably one that can handle decimal calculations) * Knowledge of basic multiplication and division Step 1: Understand the conversion factor To convert square meters (m ) to square feet (ft ), multiply the number of square meters by the conversion factor 10.7639 (rounded to five decimal places). Conversely, to change square feet into square meters, divide the number of square feet by this same value. The conversion factor represents the approximate ratio between the two units based on their defined areas (1 m = 10.7639 ft ). Key tip: Memorizing the conversion factor makes quick mental estimates possible, but using a calculator ensures accuracy for more precise work. Step 2: Identify which unit you want to convert from and to Determine whether your initial measurement is given in square meters or square feet. Then decide if you'd like to express the final result in the opposite unit. Example scenario: Suppose you have a room measuring 15 m . You need to find out its size in square feet because you plan to buy carpets listed in square feet only. Step 3: Perform the calculation In our example scenario, we would multiply the given area in square meters (15 m ) by the conversion factor (10.7639): 15 m 10.7639 = 161.4585 ft So, the room measures approximately 161.46 square feet. Guideline: Always double-check your calculations, especially when working with large or important numbers. Step 4: Verify your results (optional yet recommended) Cross-verify your calculated values through an online converter tool or another reliable source to ensure accuracy. For instance, several free websites allow users to input linear dimensions (length and width) separately before automatically computing the corresponding area in both square meters and square feet. Step 5: Apply the converted value as needed Now that you know how many square feet correspond to your original square meter measurement, proceed accordingly - e.g., purchase carpeting according to the obtained figure. Remember always to consider any discrepancies due to rounding errors during conversion. Common mistakes to avoid: * Confusing square meters with regular meters or forgetting that they represent different quantities (area vs. length) * Misremembering the conversion factor or applying it incorrectly (e.g., dividing instead of multiplying) * Failing to account for potential rounding differences between initial and final measurements By following this comprehensive tutorial, you should be able to competently convert square meters to square feet and vice versa while avoiding common pitfalls associated with such tasks. Happy measuring! i I is Santa Claus going to bring me? [puts a penny on the base and then removes it] Excuse me please excuse me please excuse me please excuse me please. Solve problems involving ratios. Number Framework Stage 8 Discuss a situation where the students have encountered percentages in their daily life. They will often suggest sports (for example, shooting for goal), shopping (such as discounts or GST), and, in country areas, calving or lambing percentages. Tell the students that the % sign comes from the "out of" symbol, /, and the two zeros from 100. It means, "out of 100". Problem: "In a game of netball, Irene gets in 43 out of her 50 shots. Sharelle takes 20 shots and gets in 17. Who is the better shot?" Tell the students that percentages are used to compare fractions. In Irene's case, the fraction is 43/50 . Doubling 43 calculates the shooting percentage because 43/50 is equivalent to 86/100 (86 out of 100). Represent this on a double number line. Ask the students to work out what Sharelle's shooting percentage was for the same game. Represent this on a double number line to show that fi nding a percentage is like mapping a proportion onto a base of 100. Pose the students a percentage problem that can be modelled with the percentage strips. For example: "Tony got in 18 out of his 24 shots. What percentage did he shoot?" Mapping 18 out of 24 onto a base of 100 gives 75%. Pose similar problems that the students can solve by aligning differently based strips with the 100-base strip. Examples might be: 16 out of 32 (50%) 9 out of 36 (25%) 10 out of 25 (40%) 12 out of 16 (75%) 12 out of 40 (30%) 4 out of 20 (20%) Show the students the base strip, but have the percentage strip aligned to it and turned over so they can't see the beads. Give the students "out of" problems and have them estimate the percentage by visualising. For example, pose six out of 16. Mark six with a paper clip. The students should estimate the percentage as just below 40% or greater than 33.3% (one-third). A calculator can be used to work out the exact percentage by keying in 6 16%. The percentage strip can then be turned over to check the estimate. Ask how else they could have estimated the percentage if there had been no strips. Look for ideas like "Six out of 16 is the same as three out of eight, and that is half of three out of four" or "There are over six sixteens in 100. Six times six is 36, so it will be more than 36 percent." Pose similar imaging problems like: 8 out of 20 (40%) 15 out of 25 (60%) 4 out of 16 (25%) 32 out of 40 (80%) 20 out of 32 (62.5%) 14 out of 36 (39%) Focus on strategies based on the numbers involved that could have been used to estimate the percentages. The students can play the game of Percents (see Material Master 7-5) to consolidate Using Number Properties Give the students percentage problems to solve. Pose these problems in contexts of sports scores, shopping discounts or mark-ups, or lambing percentages. Pose some problems where duplication of the base onto 100 is not easy. For example, 25 is easily mapped onto 100 through multiplying by four, whereas 40 is not so easily mapped (although students should be encouraged to recognise that 2.5 _ 40 = 100). Examples that involve percentages greater than 100 should also be used. Some examples might be: 18/24 = ? %? (75%) 25/40 = ? %? (62.5%) 18/27 = ? %? (66.6%) 8/32 = ? %? (25%) 24/16 = ? %? (150%) 55/20 = ? %? (275%) Get the students to record their thinking using double number lines or ratio tables, e.g., 27/36 = 75%. Use brochures from local retailers. Tell the students that one shop has a "25% off" sale, another has a "40% off" sale, and a third has a "one-third off" sale. Give the students an arbitrary budget to spend at the three shops. In the paradox of Achilles and the Tortoise, Achilles is in a footrace with the tortoise. Achilles allows the tortoise a head start of 100 metres, for example. Suppose that each racer starts running at a constant speed, one very fast and one very slow. After some finite time, Achilles will have run 100 metres, bringing him to the tortoise's starting point. During this time, the slower tortoise has run a much shorter distance. It will then take Achilles some further time to run that distance, by which time the tortoise will have advanced farther. It will then take still more time for Achilles to reach this third point, while the tortoise again moves ahead. Thus, whenever Achilles reaches somewhere the tortoise has been, he still has farther to go. Therefore, because there are an infinite number of points Achilles must reach where the tortoise has already been, he can never overtake the tortoise. This guide will walk you through the process of adding ChanServ to your IRC channel with clear and practical steps. Step 1: Choose an IRC network Before you can add ChanServ to your IRC channel, you need to select an IRC network that supports services like ChanServ. Popular IRC networks include Freenode, QuakeNet, and EFnet. Each network may have slightly different procedures or commands for using ChanServ; this tutorial focuses primarily on Freenode but should still be helpful as a general guideline for other networks. Key tip: Research various IRC networks to find one that aligns with your interests or community's needs. Some networks are tailored towards specific topics or user groups. Step 2: Register your nickname To utilize ChanServ, you must first register your nickname with NickServ, another service provided by many IRC networks. This ensures that only you can use your chosen nickname and provides additional security features. To do so, join your preferred IRC network and type the following command: ```sql /msg NickServ REGISTER password email@example.com ``` Replace `password` with a secure password and `email@example.com` with your actual email address. You will receive an email containing a confirmation code. Enter the command below to complete registration: ```python /msg NickServ VERIFYREGISTER code ``` Replace `code` with the verification code received via email. Guideline: Use strong and unique passwords for better account security. Consider enabling two-factor authentication if available. Step 3: Identify yourself upon connection Each time you connect to the IRC network, identify yourself to NickServ to claim ownership over your registered nickname. Type the following command: ``` /identify password ``` Replace `password` with the same password used during registration. Alternatively, link your nickname to an SSH key or XMPP JID for automatic identification. Step 4: Create a new channel Join an unused channel name prefixed with a "#" symbol, e.g., #mynewchannel. If the channel does not exist, you will automatically create it when joining. For example, enter the following command: ``` /join #mynewchannel ``` You can change "mynewchannel" to any desired name that follows naming conventions set forth by the particular IRC network you joined. Step 5: Set up ChanServ flags Flags determine who has access to perform certain actions within the channel. Common flags include founders ("f"), admins ("a"), operators ("o"), halfops ("h"), and voices ("v"). Assign these flags with the following syntax: ``` /cs SET #channel FLAGS target flag[+|-] ``` For instance, to give someone operator status, use: ```perl /cs SET #mynewchannel FLAGS username +o The Olympic Games () is an important international event featuring summer and winter sports. Summer Olympic Games and Winter Olympic Games are held every four years. Originally, the ancient Olympic Games were held in Ancient Greece at Olympia. The first games were in 776 BC. They were held every four years until the 6th century AC. The first "modern" Olympics happened in 1896 in Athens, Greece. Athletes participate in the Olympics Games to represent their country. Those languages are called Romance languages, and they are still spoken today. The five Romance languages with the largest number of speakers are Spanish, French, Portuguese, Italian, and Romanian. The Romance languages are very similar to one another, and speakers of one Romance language can understand many words and sentences (in both texts and spoken conversations) from another Romance language. For example, speakers of Portuguese can often understand Spanish. It can be said that the Romance languages are modern dialects of Latin. "And I have been called an egoist," murmured Gwynne.
