So is often used when we are talking about a very high degree of some quality. So can be used in most situations where very is also possible. - Why are you so unkind? - He is so stupid. So is not used with adjective + noun. - She is such a beautiful girl. (NOT She is a so beautiful girl.) So can be followed directly by that-clauses. Structures with very are not possible in this case. - He was so ill that we had to call a doctor. (NOT He was very ill that we had to call a doctor.) So far means as far as this. - Now that we have come so far, we may as well go all the way. (= Now that we have come as far as this, we may as well go all the way.) So long as means on condition that - You may borrow the book, so long as you keep it clean. So far from means instead of - So far from helping us, he hindered our progress. (= Instead of helping us, he hindered our progress.) So as to means in order to. - I will hurry, so as to not delay you. So can be used instead of a word or phrase. - I told you so! (= That is what I told you.) In constructions like the following so can mean also. - He is learning English and so am I. (Note the inverted word order after so.) So can also mean therefore. - She asked me to go, so I went. [Nina is ringing the bell on top of the school house.] And at some point, we'll be wishing you didn't talk. Title: How to Communicate with Body Language Introduction: Effective communication is not just about speaking and listening; non-verbal cues play an essential role too. Understanding body language can help you interpret people's true feelings and intentions, build stronger relationships, and improve your overall interpersonal skills. This comprehensive guide will teach you how to communicate using body language effectively. Step 1: Maintain Eye Contact Why It Matters: Making eye contact shows that you are engaged, interested, and trustworthy. Avoiding eye contact may imply dishonesty or disinterest. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Look directly into the person's eyes when they speak to show active listening. * Balance eye contact with blinking and looking away occasionally to avoid appearing intense or aggressive. Step 2: Use Appropriate Facial Expressions Why It Matters: Your facial expressions convey emotions and reactions to what others say and do. Authentic smiles and frowns can make conversations more meaningful and genuine. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Smile genuinely during positive interactions to display warmth and friendliness. * Show concern through furrowed brows and sympathetic faces during serious discussions. Step 3: Adjust Posture and Gestures Why It Matters: Open postures (e.g., uncrossed arms) suggest receptiveness, while closed ones might indicate defensiveness or skepticism. Likewise, gesturing naturally adds emphasis and engagement to speech. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Keep hands open and relaxed by your sides or use them to emphasize points. * Face the speaker squarely without turning away or crossing your arms over your chest. Step 4: Regulate Physical Distance Why It Matters: Personal space varies across cultures but maintaining appropriate distance signals comfort and respect. Too close proximity could feel invasive, whereas standing too far apart might seem distant. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Match the physical distance typically used within your culture and social context. * Respect personal boundaries by observing any signs of discomfort from the other party regarding closeness. Step 5: Mirror Body Language Why It Matters: Subtle mirroring of another person's body language creates rapport and demonstrates empathy and understanding. However, excessive mimicry can appear insincere or mocking. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Gradually match their stance, movements, or facial expressions subtly after establishing comfortable interaction. * Discontinue mirroring if noticed consciously as this could lead to misinterpretation. Step 6: Control Touch Intentionally Why It Matters: Touch communicates support, agreement, or affection, yet its interpretation depends heavily on cultural norms, relationship dynamics, and individual preferences. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Seek consent before touching someone unfamiliar, even casually. * Limit professional touch to handshakes unless explicitly invited otherwise. Conclusion: Mastering body language takes practice and patience. By following these steps and being mindful of your own non-verbal cues and those around you, you can significantly enhance your ability to connect and engage with others. Remember always to be authentic, considerate, and attentive to create positive experiences through effective body language communication. Although the monarchy was restored after his death, the Crown slowly became the secondary power, and Parliament the first. It will look so pretty!" Papa warned Maple to be careful, for the lake was dangerous. Maple said, "Okay Papa, I will be careful." So Papa and Maple worked together to restore the boat. Title: How to Drink a Margarita Like a Pro Introduction: A margarita is a classic cocktail that has been enjoyed by many around the world. It's a refreshing blend of tequila, lime juice, and orange liqueur, served with salt on the rim of the glass. This tutorial will guide you through the process of drinking a margarita like a pro, ensuring maximum enjoyment while following proper etiquette. Step 1: Choose Your Glassware Key Tip: Selecting an appropriate glass can enhance your experience. Traditionally, margaritas are served in stemmed glasses known as coupe or cocktail glasses; however, they may also be presented in wide-brimmed margarita glasses. For this tutorial, we recommend using a standard rocks (old fashioned) glass if other options aren't available. Step 2: Rimming the Glass Key Guideline: A crucial aspect of enjoying a margarita is salting the rim of the glass. This adds both flavor and aesthetic appeal. a. Pour some kosher or sea salt onto a small plate or shallow bowl. b. Cut a wedge from a lime and run it along the outer edge of the glass, moistening it evenly. c. Gently roll the outside of the glass into the salt until fully coated. Shake off any excess. Step 3: Prepare Your Ingredients Key Tip: Fresh ingredients make all the difference when crafting a delicious margarita. Always opt for freshly squeezed lime juice over pre-made mixes whenever possible. Ingredient List: - 2 oz Tequila (blanco or reposado work best) - 1 oz Lime Juice (freshly squeezed) - 1 oz Orange Liqueur (such as Cointreau or Triple Sec) - Ice Cubes - Lime Wedges (for garnish) Step 4: Mix Your Margarita Key Guideline: Combining your ingredients correctly results in a well-balanced drink. Here, we provide two methods - shaking and stirring. Both techniques yield excellent results but serve different purposes based on personal preference. a. Shaking Method: - Fill a shaker halfway with ice cubes. - Add the tequila, lime juice, and orange liqueur. The feet of the fish are called fins. [he was counting two bunches of cars. Title: Understanding Different Ways to Solve Problems Hello young learners! Today, let's talk about something fun - solving problems in different ways. Imagine you want to find all the possible combinations of letters to make words or arrange items in your room. There are several methods to do this, just like there are various strategies to solve math problems or complete puzzles. Two common techniques are called "Rejection Sampling" and "Enumeration." Let's explore these two ideas using things you see every day! **Rejection Sampling:** Imagine having a bag full of jelly beans of different colors. Now, suppose you close your eyes and pick one; this represents generating a random sample. You then check its color based on certain criteria, say, red jelly beans. If it matches, great! Keep it. Otherwise, put it back and try again until you get a red jelly bean. This process demonstrates Rejection Sampling - where you generate multiple samples and reject those not meeting specific conditions. It helps answer questions like "How many times must I draw a card before getting a queen?" However, this approach may need more attempts when our desired outcome is rare, making it less efficient. But don't worry, another technique exists that sometimes works better! **Enumeration:** Now imagine organizing toys in your room. Instead of randomly placing them around and checking if they fit somewhere later, why not list down each toy first? Then systematically place them one by one according to their size, shape, or any other feature until everything fits perfectly. Sounds familiar? That's Enumeration - listing all possibilities and going through them step-by-step. This strategy ensures no possibility is missed while being systematic. However, it becomes challenging when dealing with large numbers since manually going through each item takes time. So which one should you choose? Well, that depends on the problem itself. Some situations require quick answers, so picking randomly (Rejection Sampling) would work best. Other scenarios demand thoroughness over speed, hence requiring us to examine every option (Enumeration). As you grow older, you'll encounter similar decision points when choosing between different problem-solving techniques. Remember, understanding the nature of your challenge leads to selecting the most suitable solution method. Happy exploring! A time signature is usually located at the beginning of a piece of music. The top usually states how many beats are in a measure and the bottom states what kind of note gets one count. Types of notes and rests Notes Let's say for now that everything is in common time. In common time ( which you'll learn about in a few seconds) a whole note gets four counts, a half note gets two counts, a quarter note gets 1 count, an eighth note gets half a count, and a sixteenth note gets half of a half of a count. Makes sense doesn't it? All other time signatures relate to this pattern. Rests Rests can serve in place of a note on a staff so that time can be kept when not playing a note. Different Time Signatures 4 This is often called common time because it is the most often used time signature. Here there would be 4 beats in a measure and the quarter note would get one count. 4 Here there would be 3 beats in a measure and the quarter note again get one count. 4 Here there would be 2 beats in a measure and the quarter note would get one count and so on. 2 This is cut time because it is half of common time. Everything works in proportion to common time but it is little different. Here there are two beats in a measure and the half note gets one count. So the quarter note would get half a count and so on. 8 Here there are 6 beats in a measure and the eighth notes gets one count. So the quarter note gets two counts and so on. Extracting yourself from an uncomfortable situation or place is an important skill that can help you maintain your physical and emotional well-being. Whether you find yourself in a dangerous environment, feeling socially awkward, or trapped in a conversation you'd rather escape, this guide will provide you with practical steps and strategies to extract yourself gracefully and confidently. ### Step 1: Assess the Situation Before taking any action, take a moment to assess the situation and determine whether extraction is necessary. Consider factors such as personal safety, mental health, time constraints, and social obligations. This evaluation will allow you to make informed decisions about how best to proceed. Key Tip: Always prioritize your personal safety above all else. If you feel physically threatened, do not hesitate to call for assistance or remove yourself immediately from the area. ### Step 2: Identify Possible Escape Routes If you have determined that leaving the situation or location is indeed required, begin by identifying possible exit routes. Look for doors, windows, staircases, or other means of egress. Familiarize yourself with their locations and accessibility. Guideline: When scanning for exits, be discreet so as not to draw attention to yourself or alert others to your intentions. ### Step 3: Plan Your Departure Once you have identified potential escape routes, create a plan for departing the scene. Determine what items you may need to gather before leaving (e.g., personal belongings, transportation arrangements) and consider any logistical challenges that might arise during your departure. Key Tip: Aim to leave calmly and unobtrusively, avoiding sudden movements or actions that could attract unwanted attention. ### Step 4: Communicate Politely but Firmly In some cases, it may be appropriate to communicate your intention to leave directly to those around you. Be polite yet firm in expressing your desire to depart, offering brief explanations if needed. Avoid engaging in lengthy discussions or debates, as these can prolong the uncomfortable situation. Guideline: Use phrases like "I really must be going," "It was nice speaking with you," or "Excuse me, I have something I need to attend to" to signal your intent to leave without causing offense. ### Step 5: Implement Your Exit Strategy With your plan in place, execute it smoothly and efficiently. Gather any necessary items, navigate toward your chosen exit route, and make your way out of the uncomfortable situation or location. Key Tip: Maintain composure throughout the process, even if you are feeling anxious or uneasy. Displaying confidence can help prevent further complications or confrontations. ### Step 6: Reach Out for Support If Needed After successfully extracting yourself from the uncomfortable situation, consider reaching out to trusted friends, family members, or professionals for support. Sharing your experiences and emotions with someone who cares can help alleviate stress and promote healing. Guideline: Establish a network of supportive individuals whom you can contact whenever you need assistance or encouragement. By following these steps and implementing the provided tips and guidelines, you will become more adept at extracting yourself from uncomfortable situations or places. With practice, this skill can enhance your overall sense of self-assurance and empowerment, enabling you to navigate life's challenging moments with greater ease and finesse. Title: How to Celebrate Father's Day at Home ============================================= Father's Day is a special occasion to show appreciation and love towards fathers and father figures. With the ongoing pandemic situation, celebrating this day at home has become more relevant than ever before. This wiki-style guide will provide you with an in-depth explanation of various steps to celebrate Father's Day meaningfully within your household. ### Step 1: Plan Ahead **Key Tip:** Start planning early to ensure that everything falls into place smoothly. Discuss ideas as a family about what activities or gifts would make Dad feel valued and loved. Planning ahead allows you to organize all necessary items, allocate budget (if any), assign tasks among family members, and avoid last-minute rushes. It also ensures that everyone gets involved and contributes to making the day memorable for dad. ### Step 2: Create Personalized Gifts **Guideline:** Brainstorm gift ideas based on his hobbies, interests, or favorite things. Remember, homemade presents often carry more sentimental value. Personalized gifts can range from handcrafted cards expressing gratitude, photo albums documenting family moments, DIY craft projects, baked goods using his preferred flavors, etc. These types of gifts require effort rather than money, which makes them even more meaningful and heartfelt. ### Step 3: Prepare a Special Meal **Explanation:** Cooking or ordering food he enjoys sends a strong message of thoughtfulness and consideration. Ask him about his favorite dish or cuisine, then either prepare it together as a family activity or order from his preferred restaurant if cooking isn't feasible. Set up a cozy dining area complete with decorative elements such as candles, flowers, tablecloths, etc., enhancing the overall experience. ### Step 4: Organize Fun Activities **Tip:** Consider both indoor and outdoor games depending upon available space and weather conditions. Activities could include board games, card tournaments, movie marathons, backyard barbecues, gardening projects, painting sessions - anything that aligns with his interests and comfort level. The goal here is to engage everyone while spending quality time together. ### Step 5: Dedicate Time For Relaxation **Reason:** Providing him some downtime shows respect for his needs and promotes relaxation. This might involve setting up a quiet reading nook, arranging a massage session by a family member, or simply letting him enjoy uninterrupted TV time. Enforcing 'dad hours,' where no one disturbs him unless absolutely necessary, conveys respect and understanding for his personal space and need for rest. ### Step 6: Express Gratitude Verbally **Importance:** Openly communicating feelings strengthens familial bonds. Take turns sharing stories about why you appreciate him, expressing thanks verbally. Encourage children to articulate their thoughts too; they may share insights that surprise and delight him! By following these steps meticulously, you are bound to create a memorable Father's Day celebration right at home. Despite its simplicity, remember that it's the intention behind every action that truly counts! Adam that little boy who lives at the h where we're going to move. The word market can also have a more general meaning in the economy. It can mean the way things are bought or sold. "There is a big market for dishwashers" means that lots of people want to buy dishwashers, so therefore a business that makes dishwashers is likely to be able to make a lot of money. When things are sold, people buy the product, and this "stimulates the economy" (helps people to spend and earn money). The market needs to balance supply and demand. There is no point in supplying (making) lots of dishwashers if people do not want them. In the market prices may change quickly if supply or demand changes. [noise of blocks drowns out last few words] Coping if You Get Globalled from Wikia Getting globalled on Wikia can be a frustrating experience, but there are steps you can take to cope with this situation. This guide will provide you with a comprehensive plan to help you move forward and continue contributing to online communities. **Step 1: Understand What It Means to Get Globalled** Getting globalled refers to having your account globally blocked by Wikia staff. This means that you cannot edit any Wikia wikis using that account. This usually happens when a user repeatedly violates Wikia's Terms of Use or Community Guidelines. Key Tip: Remember that getting globalled is not a personal attack against you; it is a consequence of breaking rules designed to maintain a positive environment across all Wikia communities. **Step 2: Take a Break** After being globalled, it's essential to take some time away from editing on Wikia. Use this break as an opportunity to reflect on why your account was globalled and consider whether you may have unintentionally broken any rules. Guideline: A few days to a week off should give you enough perspective to come back refreshed and ready to contribute constructively. **Step 3: Review Your Past Edits** Go through your past edits and identify any patterns that might have led to your global ban. Look for instances where you could have been less confrontational, more respectful, or adhered better to community standards. Key Tip: Be honest with yourself during this review process - admitting mistakes is crucial for growth and improvement. **Step 4: Apologize If Necessary** If your actions caused harm to other users or the community, consider apologizing publicly (e.g., via a discussion forum post). An authentic apology shows maturity and willingness to learn from past errors. Guideline: Keep your apology simple, sincere, and focused on what you did wrong rather than making excuses or blaming others. **Step 5: Create a New Account (Optional)** If you wish to continue contributing to Wikia after serving your global block, create a new account under a different username. Make sure to familiarize yourself with the Terms of Service and Community Guidelines before resuming your activities. Key Tip: Avoid creating a new account immediately after being globalled, as doing so may lead to further penalties for evading a ban. **Step 6: Engage in Positive Contributions** As you return to editing, focus on making helpful and valuable contributions to the community. Show respect towards other users, follow established norms, and avoid engaging in disputes or arguments. Guideline: Adopting a collaborative mindset can go a long way in rebuilding trust within the community and demonstrating that you have learned from your past mistakes. **Step 7: Seek Support From Other Users** Reach out to experienced users who can offer guidance and support as you navigate the challenges of returning to Wikia after being globalled. Building strong relationships with these mentors can help ensure long-term success. Key Tip: Choose mentors carefully, ensuring they have a good understanding of Wikia policies and practices and are known for their positive contributions to the platform. By following these steps, you can effectively cope with getting globalled from Wikia and position yourself as a responsible and valued member of the community upon your return. Title: How to Check Recalls on Medications Introduction: Before taking any medication, it's crucial to ensure that it hasn't been recalled by the manufacturer or regulatory authorities due to safety concerns. This article will guide you through the process of checking recalls on medications in a simple, clear, and practical way. By following these steps, you can protect your health and make informed decisions about the drugs you take. Step 1: Identify the Drug Name and Manufacturer First, locate the drug name and its manufacturer. You can find this information on the prescription bottle label, over-the-counter packaging, or from your healthcare provider. Knowing the exact name and manufacturer is essential as different versions of the same drug may have separate recall statuses. Key Tip: If possible, obtain both the brand (trade) name and generic name of the medication, which are often printed together on the package. For example, if you are using Advil, the active ingredient is Ibuprofen. Sometimes, only one form of the drug might be affected by a recall while others remain safe. Step 2: Access Reliable Websites Visit official websites of trusted organizations dedicated to tracking medication recalls. These include: * U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA): <https://www.fda.gov/> * The European Medicines Agency (EMA): <https://www.ema.europa.eu/> * Health Canada: <https://healthycanadians.gc.ca/> * He's going to sit right here for a minute. Code Your First Program in C++ Step 1: Install a Compiler Before you can start coding in C++, you need a compiler that translates your code into machine language so that your computer can understand it. There are many compilers available online for free such as GCC (GNU Compiler Collection), Microsoft Visual Studio, or Clang. For this tutorial, we will be using GCC which is widely used and compatible with most operating systems. Key Tip: Make sure to choose a reputable source when downloading your compiler to avoid any potential security risks. Step 2: Create a New File Open up your text editor (Notepad on Windows, TextEdit on Mac) and create a new file. Save the file with a .cpp extension; this tells the system that it's a C++ program. Naming convention isn't crucial at this stage but keeping it simple like "main.cpp" would suffice. Guideline: Always save your work regularly to prevent loss of data due to unexpected shutdowns or errors. Step 3: Write Your First Program Every programming language requires specific syntax for writing code. In C++, all programs must have a function named 'main'. Here is an example of what your first program should look like: ```cpp #include <iostream> int main() { std::cout << "Hello, World!"; return 0; } ``` Let's break down what each line does: - `#include <iostream>` : This statement includes the iostream library which allows us to perform input and output operations. - `int main()` : This declares the entry point of our program where execution begins. - `std::cout << "Hello, World!";` : Displays the string inside double quotes on the console screen. The symbol '<<' is known as insertion operator. - `return 0;` : Indicates successful completion of the program. An integer value other than zero indicates abnormal termination. Step 4: Compile Your Program Now go back to your terminal or command prompt, navigate to the directory where you saved your .cpp file and type `g++ -o helloworld helloworld.cpp`. Replace 'helloworld' with whatever name you chose earlier. This command instructs the compiler to convert the .cpp file into executable format. Step 5: Run Your Program After compilation, there should now be a newly created file without any extension. Type `./helloworld` in your terminal and press enter. You should see "Hello, World!" displayed on the screen. Congratulations! You just wrote and ran your first C++ program. Key Tips: - Pay close attention to spelling, punctuation, and capitalization since C++ is case sensitive. - Comment out sections of your code by preceding them with // or enclosing them within /* */ if they span multiple lines. Comments are ignored by the compiler and provide useful context for others reading your code. - Use consistent indentation throughout your code for better readability. In conclusion, learning to code takes time and practice. Don't be discouraged if things don't click right away. Keep practicing and challenging yourself with more complex projects. Happy coding! [reads a line from the book, as GRF says something to Row] [1w Nina picks the small baby off the floor] Rationale: Before they can begin to read, students must understand that words are made up of different sounds. The smallest unit of sound in a word is called a phoneme. It is important that students understand phonemes because studies have found that the ability to discriminate phonemes is one of the best predictors of first-year reading achievement (Adams). In this lesson, the students will learn the phoneme /m/. By the end of the lesson, they will be able to identify the phoneme /m/ in spoken words. They will also understand the relationship between the /m/ phoneme and the letter m. Finally, they will practice finding /m/ in words. Materials: A set of flashcards, chart with the phrase "Max the Monster made a mess in my room on Monday" on it, primary paper, pencils, chalkboard, chalk, the book My Monster Mama Loves Me So by Laura Leuck, a class set of cards with the letter m on them, worksheet (attached) 1. First, the teacher should review the phonemes the class has learned so far. These would include the phonemes for all of the vowels and the consonants b-l. She should do this by using the set of picture flashcards. She should hold up one picture at a time. The class will first say the name of what the picture is and then say the first phoneme in the word. For a picture of a bat, the class would say "Bat, /b/." 2. The teacher should begin the new lesson by posting a chart with the phrase "Max the Monster made a mess in my room on Monday" on it. The class will read the phrase together. "What sound do we hear the most in this sentence?" After a student answers /m/, the teacher continues. "Right! What do you usually say when you taste something that is really good? Right! Mmmmm! That is the same sound that is in some of the words that we just read. When we make this sound, our lips should be pressed together and our mouths vibrate. (Model this for class) Let s read the sentence again. This time I want us to stretch out the /m/ sound when it is in a word. Instead of just saying Max, I want us to say Mmmmmax. Let s begin." The class then reads the sentence together. 3. "Now let's get out our pencils and paper. By looking at the sentence we just read, what letter do you think stands for the /m/ sound? Right! It is the letter m. Today we are going to make this letter on our paper. Start at the fence and go down to the sidewalk. Without picking your pencil up off of the paper, go back up to the fence and make one hump on your paper. Then make another hump just like the first one. (While talking, model this on a chalkboard for the class.) These humps are like the humps on a camels back. After you have made the second hump, you have made an m! I want you to practice by making four more of these on your paper. Remember that the letter m spells the /m/ sound." 4. Next the teacher will ask the students to raise their hands and answer the following questions. "Do you hear the /m/ sound in the word cat or mouse? Do you hear /m/ in none or some? In sing or hum?" The class will raise their hands and answer these questions. Then the teacher will hand out a card with the letter m on it to everyone in the class. As the teacher slowly reads the book, My Monster Mama Loves Me So, each student will lift up their m card each time they hear the /m/ sound. 5. Finally, the students will be given the attached worksheet. On the worksheet, the children will circle each word that they believe has /m/ in it. The teacher will call out the name of each picture and give the children enough time to circle it if they believe the word has the /m/ sound in it. The words are (beginning at top, left and ending at bottom, right): camel, man, apple, moon, elephant, and lamp. This will be the assessment for the lesson. Adams, Marilyn Jager. (1990). Following several years of work, Louis Essen from the National Physical Laboratory (Teddington, England) and William Markowitz from the United States Naval Observatory (USNO) determined the relationship between the hyperfine transition frequency of the caesium atom and the ephemeris second. Using a common-view measurement method based on the received signals from radio station WWV, they determined the orbital motion of the Moon about the Earth, from which the apparent motion of the Sun could be inferred, in terms of time as measured by an atomic clock. As a result, in 1967 the Thirteenth General Conference on Weights and Measures defined the second of atomic time in the International System of Units (SI) as Aldous Leonard Huxley (26 July 1894 22 November 1963) was an English writer, and one of the most prominent members of the famous Huxley family. Title: How to Deal With Catty Coworkers ===================================== Catty coworkers can make your work environment unpleasant and unproductive. Dealing with their negative behavior requires patience, tact, and assertiveness. Here is an in-depth guide on how to handle catty coworkers while maintaining professionalism and fostering a positive workplace culture. ### Step 1: Understand what constitutes cattiness Before dealing with catty coworkers, you need to recognize the signs of cattiness in the office. This might include gossiping, backstabbing, spreading rumors, undermining others' efforts, or making sarcastic comments meant to belittle or hurt someone's feelings. Recognizing these behaviors will help you address them effectively. Key tip: Keep in mind that sometimes people may be acting out due to stress, personal issues, or feeling threatened by change. While this does not excuse bad behavior, understanding its root cause can help inform your response strategy. ### Step 2: Stay calm and composed When confronted with cattiness, maintain your composure. Responding emotionally could escalate the situation and damage relationships further. Instead, take deep breaths and collect yourself before addressing the issue. Guideline: Practice active listening skills - pay attention to what they are saying without interrupting and try to understand their perspective. Show empathy but avoid getting defensive or engaging in tit-for-tat responses. ### Step 3: Address the behavior directly If possible, talk privately with the individual exhibiting cattiness. Describe specific instances where their actions made you uncomfortable and explain why. Use "I" statements rather than blaming language (e.g., say "I felt disrespected when..." instead of "You were being mean"). Tip: Be clear about your boundaries and expectations for respectful communication at work. For example, let them know that discussing confidential information or speaking negatively about colleagues behind their backs is not acceptable. ### Step 4: Set consequences if necessary Multiplication Facts for 2, 5, and 10 Once children feel comfortable with skip counting by 2's, 5's, and 10's, introduce the new operation of multiplication and its relationship to skip-counting. You may wish to use the lists of twos, fives, and tens as developed from the number line. Encourage children to share their thoughts and strategies as the lesson progresses. Materials: countable objects (bottle caps, paper clips) to be placed on an overhead projector for all to see; 10 index cards per child; paper and pencil for each child Prerequisite Skills and Concepts: Children should know how to skip-count by 2's, 5's, and 10's. - Say: Today we will learn how to multiply. Multiplying is really not new to you. It is just a new word that makes addition easier. You can use skip-counting to learn what multiplication is all about. Place 3 groups, with 2 paper clips in each group, on the overhead projector for all to see. - Ask: How can we find the total number of paper clips? Children should realize that they can count the clips by ones, skip-count by twos, or add 2 three times. Record each method on the board. - Say: Good! You have just used addition or counting to do a multiplication problem. The total number of clips shows 3 groups, 2 in each group, or 3 times 2. We write it as "3 2 = 6." It means 3 groups of 2 equals 6. Write 3 2 = 6 in vertical and horizontal format on the board. Note that the first factor is the number of groups and the second factor is the number in each group. (It may not be necessary to stress this difference between factors.) - Ask: How else can we make 6? Lead children to realize that 6 is 1 group of 6, 6 groups of 1, 2 groups of 3, or 3 groups of 2. Lead them to realize that each way represents a multiplication fact. Write each fact on the board so there are 4 math facts, each with an answer of 6. - Ask: How can we use skip-counting to get 8? What multiplication facts are suggested by skip-counting? Children should realize that there are 4 groups of 2, 2 groups of 4, 1 group of 8, or 8 groups of 1 in 8. Point out that skip-counting by 4's or by 2's will make 8. Show these groupings on the overhead using paper clips. Together the methods suggest 4 2 = 8, 2 4 = 8, 1 8 = 8, and 8 1 = 8. Write each multiplication fact on the board. - Say: Look at the multiplication facts on the board. I need a volunteer to choose one fact and show how it could be written as repeated addition. Have the volunteer write the repeated addition near the multiplication fact. Then ask the volunteer to draw a picture or diagram of the addition. Lead the volunteer to draw a diagram showing rows and columns. - Ask: That picture is called an array. It represents repeated addition. What picture should be drawn to represent multiplication? Children should understand that the same picture drawn to represent repeated addition is representative of multiplication. - Ask: Who can find 2 times 6? Draw an array and hold it above your head. After allowing time for all children to find an answer, choose several children to draw their arrays on the board. Emphasize that "two times six is twelve." - Repeat with other multiplication facts in which two is a factor. Make sure children feel familiar with repeated addition, skip-counting, and arrays. - Say: Now you know how to multiply. Let's write the multiplication table for twos. Start with 2 0, then find 2 1, then 2 2, and so on until 2 10. These answers are called products. Have volunteers write the facts on the board. Ask a volunteer to explain the method used to find the product. Then distribute 10 index cards to each child. Write one fact on the front of each index card. Write the corresponding product on the back of the card. Use the cards as flash cards. - Spend another lesson finding facts for fives. Begin by using skip-counting with fingers. For example, have 3 children hold up their fingers to find 5 6. Then continue to find the other facts for five. Be sure to review arrays, repeated addition, as well as skip-counting. - Spend another lesson finding the facts for ten. Use children's toes in a similar strategy for skip-counting. Wrap-Up and Assessment Hints Multiplication needs a lot of practice. Remind children that it took time to learn the addition and subtraction facts. Have them remember how they learned those facts and apply it to the multiplication facts. Encourage the use of flash cards on a daily basis. As you assess each child, check if he/she has a clear understanding of the meaning of multiplication, through repeated addition, skip-counting, and arrays. [deep then high pitch on 'get' as she pounces; 2.5 sec S giggling and shrieking] Title: How to Get a Baby to Clap Their Hands Introduction: Clapping is an essential motor skill that babies develop as they grow. It's also a fun way for parents and caregivers to interact with their little ones. This guide will provide you with detailed steps and helpful tips on encouraging your baby to clap their hands. Remember, every child is unique and may reach this milestone at different times. Patience and positive reinforcement are key. Step 1: Understanding Developmental Milestones Typically, babies start clapping between 7-12 months old. However, some children might take longer. Before attempting to teach clapping, ensure your baby has developed enough strength and control in their hand muscles. You can observe this by checking if they can grasp and hold objects firmly. Step 2: Demonstrating Clapping Hold your baby securely in your lap or sit them down where they can see you clearly. Begin clapping slowly near them so they can hear the sound and watch what you're doing. Make sure to maintain eye contact and use a cheerful tone while saying, "Look, I'm clapping!" Step 3: Encouraging Hand Movement Once your baby seems interested in your hand movements, gently place one of their hands into yours. Then, guiding both of your hands together to make a clapping motion. Say something like, "Let's try clapping hands!" Repeat this several times until your baby begins to anticipate the movement. Key Tip: Keep sessions short (around 5 minutes) to prevent boredom or frustration. Step 4: Prompting Independent Claps After repeated guided practice, gradually reduce the amount of assistance you give your baby during clapping motions. Place their hands apart slightly further than usual when prompting a clap. Over time, increase the distance between their hands until they begin to bring them together independently. Guideline: Celebrate even small successes to motivate your baby. Smile, praise, and say things like, "Yay! You did it!" Step 5: Introducing Toys or Songs Incorporate toys or action songs that involve clapping. For example, shake maracas or rhythm instruments together during music time. Sing familiar rhymes such as "Pat-a-Cake" or "If You're Happy and You Know It," emphasizing the parts involving clapping. Step 6: Provide Plenty of Opportunities Create situations throughout the day where clapping is appropriate, such as celebrating mealtimes, diaper changes, or achieving minor goals (like picking up a toy). Regularly engaging in these activities will help solidify the clapping motion in your baby's muscle memory. Step 7: Monitor Progress and Adjust Approach Every baby learns at their own pace. If your child isn't showing interest in clapping after trying the above steps consistently over a few weeks, consider consulting a pediatrician or early childhood development specialist. They can offer guidance tailored to your specific situation. Conclusion: Teaching a baby to clap takes patience, consistency, and understanding. By following this comprehensive guide and keeping communication lines open with healthcare professionals, you'll be well on your way to helping your baby master this exciting new skill. Adverb clauses provide additional information about how something is done. They are much like adverbs in that they tell the reader when, why or how someone did something. All clauses contain a subject and a verb, adverb clauses are introduced by subordinating conjunctions. For example, Tom helped the student with the homework because he didn't understand the exercise. ... because he didn't understand the exercise explains why Tom helped and is an adverb clause. Begin by studying adverb clauses which are often called "time clauses" in English grammar books and follow specific patterns. Take a look at the chart below to study the various usage of different time expressions. When an adverb clause begins the sentence, use a comma to separate the two clauses. Example: As soon as he arrives, we will have some lunch. When the adverb clause finishes the sentence, there is no need for a comma. Example: He gave me a call when he arrived in town. Adverb Clauses with TimeWhen - He was talking on the phone when I arrived. - When she called, he had already eaten lunch. - I washed the dishes when my daughter fell asleep. - We'll go to lunch when you come to visit. 'When' means 'at that moment, at that time, etc.'. Notice the different tenses used in relationship to the clause beginning with when. It is important to remember that 'when' takes either the simple past OR the present - the dependent clause changes tense in relation to the 'when' clause. - We will finish before he arrives. - She (had) left before I telephoned. 'Before' means 'before that moment'. It is important to remember that 'before' takes either the simple past OR the present. - We will finish after he comes. - She ate after I (had) left. 'After' means 'after that moment'. It is important to remember that 'after' takes the present for future events and the past OR past perfect for past events. - She began cooking while I was finishing my homework. - As I was finishing my homework, she began cooking. 'While' and 'as' mean 'during that time'. 'While' and 'as' are both usually used with the past continuous because the meaning of 'during that time' which indicates an action in progess.By the time - By the time he finished, I had cooked dinner. - We will have finished our homework by the time they arrive. 'By the time' expresses the idea that one event has been completed before another. It is important to notice the use of the past perfect for past events and future perfect for future events in the main clause. This is because of the idea of something happening up to another point in time. - We waited until he finished his homework. - I'll wait till you finish. 'Until' and 'till' express 'up to that time'. We use either the simple present or simple past with 'until' and 'till'. 'Till' is usually only used in spoken English. - I have played tennis since I was a young boy. - They have worked here since 1987. 'Since' means 'from that time'. We use the present perfect (continuous) with 'since'. 'Since' can also be used with a specific point in time. As soon as - He will let us know as soon as he decides (or as soon as he has decided). - As soon as I hear from Tom, I will give you a telephone call. 'As soon as' means 'when something happens - immediately afterwards'. 'As soon as' is very similar to 'when' it emphasizes that the event will occur immediately after the other. We usually use the simple present for future events, although present perfect can also be used. Whenever, every time - Whenever he comes, we go to have lunch at "Dick's". - We take a hike every time he visits. 'Whenever' and 'every time' mean 'each time something happens'. We use the simple present (or the simple past in the past) because 'whenever' and 'every time' express habitual action. The first, second, third, fourth etc., next, last time - The first time I went to New York, I was intimidated by the city. - I saw Jack the last time I went to San Francisco. - The second time I played tennis, I began to have fun. The first, second, third, fourth etc., next, last time means 'that specific time'. We can use these forms to be more specific about which time of a number of times something happened. More Adverb Clauses One day, a little cat named Kitty was very hungry. She went outside to look for some food. She saw a big, red ball. Kitty thought, "I can play with the ball and find food later." Kitty used her paw to push the ball. It rolled and rolled. The ball hit a bush, and out came a little mouse with a long tail. The mouse saw Kitty and said, "Why did you push the ball into my home?" Kitty said, "I'm sorry, Mouse. I was hungry and wanted to play. Can we be friends?" The mouse nodded, and they played with the ball together. After playing, they found some food and shared it. Kitty and Mouse were happy and full. Now it's time to get this stuff on here. And now he's gettin' ready for bed. The B-b-b-b-bouncing Basketball Rationale: This lesson was designed to teach kindergarteners how to detect the phoneme /b/ in spoken words. Students will learn to recognize /b/ in spoken words by learning a meaningful representation (sound of a ball bouncing) and the letter symbol, practice finding /b/ in words, and apply phoneme awareness with /b/ by having students perform a hand gesture when they hear /b/. This lesson will guide students to understand the correspondence of b = /b/ and assist them in reading and writing with this correspondence. Word Cards: ball, bug, bath, cat, & bus Primary paper and pencil White Board and Dry Erase markers The book, "Little Basketball" By: Brad Herzog Say: Did you know that the language that we speak, read, and write is much like a secret code? The tricky part is what the letters stand for and the way our mouth moves as we say words. See how my lips are pressed together as I make the /b/ sound? Today we are going to become secret agent code breakers by spotting the mouth move /b/. We spell /b/ with the letter Bb. Have you ever heard a basketball being dribbled on the ground? It sounds like this b-b-b-b-b (doing the hand gesture when saying the sound). Does anyone notice what your mouth is doing? Right, your lips are pressed together when you say the b sound. Let's practice the /b/ phoneme while doing the hand gesture dribbling the ball...B-b-b-b-b. Great job! Let me show you how I would check to see if the /b/ is in a word or not. I am checking for a /b/ like the sound we hear when a basketball is being bounced on the floor: What about /b/ /a/ /l/ /l/, I do hear /b/ at the beginning of ball. Let me check bug: /b/, /bug/ - there' s the /b/ in bug. Now I am going to check cat: /ca/, /cat/. There is no /b/ in cat. Let' s try a tongue tickler [on poster]. "Bobby bounced the basketball into the bush". Everyone say it together. Now say it again, and this time, stretch the /b/ in each of the words /bbbbb/. "Bbbbobby bbbbounced the bbbbasketball into the bbbbushes." Try it again, and this time break it off the word: "/B/obby the /b/ounced the /b/asketball into the /b/ushes." Great job! [Have the students take out primary paper and pencil.] We use the letter B to spell /b/. [Model on the board how to write the letter as you walk the students through the process verbally.] To write a Capital B, you go straight down the sidewalk; around for his big chest, and around for his big tummy. Let's write the lowercase letter b. First start at the roof, go down, b-bbounce up and around. I want to see everyone's b's. After I check it and give you a sticker, I want you to make nine more just like it. Now let's see if we hear the /b/ sound in spoken words. Do you hear /b/ in cap or bat? [Call on student for each set of words.] bus or dog? bed or tap? nest or bell? Let's see if you can spot the mouth move in some words. I want everyone to do the bouncing ball hand gesture with your hand every time you hear the /b/: cap, bat, bus, dog, bed, tap, nest, bell, nest. Now let's read the book, Little Basketball. This book has lots of good rhyming words, as well as fun riddles. As I read, I want you to do the hand gesture every time you hear /b/. When we are done reading, I am going to let you draw a picture of something that starts with the /b/ sound.[Pass out paper and crayons, and after they are finished then display their work.] Show BED and model how to decide if it is bed or rod. The B makes me do the hand gesture as if I am bouncing a basketball, /b/, so the word with the correct sound is bbbbb-e-d. Now you try BET: sat or bet? BET: get or bat? bad: met or bad? For assessment, distribute the worksheet. http://www.abcteach.com/documents/phonics-letter-b-matching-picture-to-word-printable-worksheet-letter-theme-day-b-prek-kindergarten-grade-1-52638 Students are to match the correct B word with the correct picture. If time permits, the students can color the pictures that have circled. Call the students individually to read the phonetic cue words from step 8. Little Basketball; Herzog, Brad. Within a billion years (that is at about 3.6 billion years ago) the first life evolved, in the Archaean era. Some bacteria developed photosynthesis, which lets plants make food from the Sun's light and water. This released a lot of oxygen, which was first taken up by iron in solution. Title: How to Get Mild Scene Hair Scene hair is a hairstyle that originated from the emo and punk scenes, characterized by its messy yet stylish appearance. A mild version of scene hair involves softer layers and less dramatic contrast compared to traditional scene styles. This tutorial will guide you through achieving a mild scene hair look with detailed steps and helpful tips. ### Step 1: Assess Your Current Hair Type and Length Mild scene hair works best with straight or slightly wavy hair. If your hair has tight curls, consider using a relaxer or flat iron before proceeding. Ideal length should be between ear-length and shoulder-length. Key tip: Avoid cutting your own hair if possible; seek help from an experienced friend or visit a professional stylist who can provide guidance based on your face shape and features. ### Step 2: Choose the Right Layering Technique Discuss layering options with your stylist. For a mild scene style, ask for choppy layers instead of blunt ones. Choppy layers create more texture and movement which suits this type of hairstyle better than uniform cuts. Guideline: Request longer bangs (fringe) around eyebrow level for versatility in styling. The rest of the layers should gradually become shorter towards the back, creating a subtle graduation effect. ### Step 3: Prep Your Hair Properly = = = children/CHIDES_NA/Parent Child/SLI/PC-hm/hin39.cha = = = Once upon a time, there was a smart pirate called Jack. At this time many composers felt that everything had already been done by the composers of the past, so they wanted to find new ways of composing. Claude Debussy, Arnold Schoenberg and Igor Stravinsky, in particular, found new ways of writing music which was not necessarily tonal (in any particular key). Classical music was influenced by jazz, especially with American composers. Later in the century people such as Pierre Boulez and Karlheinz Stockhausen experimented in many other ways, including with electronic music (tape recorders etc.). Today's composers have combined some of these ideas to develop their own styles. I'm not doing one side XXX whole John Hancock. Do you want to say some babas you want to do some flirting? [5.5 sec, S makes a small sound 2 sharp blows; D lifts S] Title: How to Present a Book Like a Pro Introduction: Presenting a book can be an exciting yet nerve-wracking experience. Whether you're presenting your own published work or discussing someone else's book at a club meeting, knowing how to effectively present a book will help engage your audience and leave a lasting impression. This comprehensive guide provides clear steps with expert advice to ensure that your next book presentation is successful. Step 1: Understand Your Audience Before diving into the content of the book, take some time to understand who your audience is. Are they familiar with the genre? Have they read the book already? Knowing this information allows you to tailor your presentation accordingly, ensuring relevance and engagement. Key Tip: Research your audience demographics ahead of time if possible. If it's a public event, consider asking the organizer for details about attendees. Step 2: Read the Book Thoroughly To deliver an insightful presentation, reading the entire book from cover to cover is crucial. Pay attention to themes, characters, plot twists, writing style, and symbolism. Highlight or note down passages that strike you as particularly meaningful or interesting. Guideline: Allow ample time to read and reread sections as needed. A thorough understanding of the material leads to a more confident and engaging presentation. Step 3: Outline Key Points Organize your thoughts by creating an outline of main points to cover during your presentation. These might include major themes, character development, unique aspects of the author's style, historical context, etc. Remember to keep your points concise and relevant to your audience. Key Tip: Use sticky notes while reading to mark pages containing important ideas or quotes. It makes referencing easier when outlining your presentation. Step 4: Prepare Visual Aids Visual aids like slides, posters, or handouts can greatly enhance your presentation. They provide visual interest and reinforce key concepts. However, avoid overloading them with text; instead, use bullet points, images, and pull quotes sparingly but effectively. Guideline: Follow the 6x6 rule for slide design - no more than six lines of text per slide, and never fewer than six words per line. Step 5: Practice Makes Perfect Rehearse your presentation multiple times until you feel comfortable. Practicing aloud helps solidify your delivery and timing. Try recording yourself to identify areas needing improvement. Key Tip: Time yourself to make sure your presentation fits within the allotted time frame. Trim unnecessary parts if necessary. Step 6: Engage With Your Audience During the presentation, maintain eye contact with your audience and speak clearly and enthusiastically. Encourage questions and discussions to create a dynamic atmosphere. Guideline: Body language plays a significant role in communication. Stand tall, move around occasionally, and use gestures appropriately to emphasize points. Step 7: Handle Questions Gracefully Be prepared for unexpected questions. Respond honestly and respectfully even if you don't know the answer immediately. Offer to find out and get back to the person later. Key Tip: Repeat or rephrase the question before answering to ensure everyone understands what was asked. Conclusion: Delivering a captivating book presentation requires careful planning, deep understanding of the subject matter, effective communication skills, and adaptability. By following these steps and guidelines, you'll be well on your way to mastering the art of book presentation. Happy presenting! You're USTA having a lot of noise around. Title: How to Grab Properly a Tennis Racket Introduction: Holding a tennis racket correctly is crucial for controlling your shots, generating power, and preventing injuries. This guide will walk you through the process of properly gripping a tennis racket, with clear explanations, helpful tips, and guidelines. By following these steps, you'll be able to improve your game significantly. Step 1: Choosing the Right Grip Size Before learning how to grip a tennis racket, ensure that you have the right grip size. Hold the racket with your dominant hand using all your fingers and thumb. The space between your ring finger and palm should accommodate one or two fingertips from your other hand. If there isn't enough room, consider going up half an inch; if it feels too tight, go down half an inch. A proper grip size allows optimal control and reduces strain. Step 2: Understanding Hand Orientation Your hand has three main parts when holding a racket - heel (base), middle (center), and beam (knuckles). Pay attention to where each part rests on the handle as this affects shot placement and control. Step 3: Positioning Your Index Knuckle Place your index knuckle directly over the first bevel (the edge separating two sides) of the racket handle. For most players, this means aligning their index kninkle with the third bevel, counting clockwise starting at the top of the grip. This position is known as the Continental Grip, suitable for serves, volleys, slices, and drop shots. Other grips require shifting your index knuckle accordingly. Step 4: Placing Your Heel Position your heel below the throat of the racquet so that the base of your palm touches the flat side of the handle. Make sure your wrist remains straight. Keeping contact between your hand and the grip here improves stability during swings. Step 5: Wrapping Your Fingers Around the Handle Wrap your remaining four fingers around the handle tightly but comfortably. They should cover about two inches above the point where your index knuckle meets the handle. Maintain even pressure across all fingers. Step 6: Adjusting Your Thumb Rest your thumb lightly along the opposite side of the handle, forming a 'V' shape with your thumbprint meeting the intersection of two bevels. Your thumb should run parallel to the strings without crossing them. It provides additional leverage and directional input for shots. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Loosen Up: Relax your grip before serving or hitting. Too much tension can lead to inconsistent strikes and early fatigue. - Practice Changing Grips: Master different grips like Eastern, Western, Semi-Western, Double-Handed Backhand etc., by practicing quick transitions while keeping eye contact with the ball. - Use Overgrip: An extra layer of grip material adds cushioning, absorbent sweat, enhances durability, and assists in repositioning your hold mid-play. - Regular Inspection: Check your grip regularly for wear and tear, especially after several hours of play or extreme weather conditions. Replace worn out grips promptly to maintain good handling. Conclusion: Properly grabbing a tennis racket involves selecting the correct grip size, understanding hand orientation, placing body parts appropriately, adjusting thumb alignment, and maintaining flexibility. With consistent practice and adherence to our guidelines, you're well on your way to mastering various grips and improving your overall performance. And um we'll see how it comes out tonight. Usually this happens with participles. For example, the present participle "interesting" and the adjective "interesting" look the same. Verbs are different from adjectives, though, because they cannot be modified by "very", "more", or "most". For example, you can say "That is very interesting," so you know interesting is an adjective here. But you cannot say "My teacher is very interesting me in math" because in this sentence "interesting" is a verb. On the other hand, if you cannot change the 'be' verb to 'seem' or 'become', it is probably a verb. I can put it underneath here and XXX... If very high, it can cause acute complications. In type 1 diabetics, one of these complications is "diabetic ketoacidosis" which is a medical emergency and can often be detected by a fruity smell on the breath. Another acute complication, more common in type 2 diabetics, is "non-ketotic hyperosmolar coma" which is also very dangerous. During the next year, your baby will learn to say about 300 new words. [tape off in middle of word at 3144] Are you going to cover me too? Bouncing the Basketball with B By Marianna Waits Emergent Literacy Design Rationale. This lesson will help children identify /b/, the phoneme represented by B. Students will learn to recognize /b/ in spoken words by learning a meaningful representation (bounce the ball) and the letter symbol B in phonetic cue reading by distinguishing rhyming words from beginning letters. Materials. Primary paper and pencil; chart with "Bill and Betty baked brown bread for Barbara's baby"; drawing paper and crayons; Bubble Bear by Scholastic Press (February 2001); cards with BAD, BALL, BAG, TOY, BUN, and RAKE; worksheet to assess identification of pictures with /b/ (look below for URL). 1. Say: The language that we write is a secret code. The part that is tricky is learning what letters stand for-the mouth moves we make as we say words. Today we are going to work on watching the mouth move /b/. We spell /b/ with letter B. B looks two balls on a stick, and /b/ sounds like the sound when we bounce a ball. 2. Now, let's pretend to bounce the ball, /b/, /b/, /b/. [pretend to bounce a ball] Notice how your lips start out together, then they open and a puff of air comes out and you voice box is on. 3. Let me show you how to find /b/ in the word cub. I'm going to say it in slow motion and listen for the ball bouncing. Cc-u-u-ub. Slower: Cc-u-u-u-bb. Did you hear it? I heard the ball bounce. 4. Now let's try a tongue twister [use the chart]. "Bill and Betty baked brown bread for Barbara's baby." Let's all say it together two times. The third time we will stretch the /b/. "Bbbill and bbetty bbbaked bbbrown bbbread for Bbbarbara's bbbabby." This last time break the /b/ off the word: "/b/ ill and /b/ etty /b/ aked /b/ rown /b/ read for /b/ arbara's /b/ aby. 5. [Students should get their primary paper and pencil out]. We use the letter B to spell /b/. Capital B looks like two balls on a stick. Let's write the lowercase letter b. Start at the roof top, go down, b-bbounce up and around. I want you to make a total of 10. 6. Call on the students to tell you how they know the answer: Do you hear /b/ in brake or maze? Rat or band? Bob or Rob? Sun or dab? Say: Let's see if you can spot the mouth move /b/ in some words. Bounce the ball if you hear /b/: The, big, buff, bear, chased, the, bold, tigers. 7. Say: "Let's read a book about a Bear's bubbles. Bear loves to blow bubbles. But when Badger tries to spoil Bear's fun, he blows a bubble you won't believe!" Have the children draw the bear blowing a bubble. And let them display their work. 8. Show BAD and model how to decide if it is bad or sad: The B tells me to bounce the ball, /b/ so this word is bbb-ad, bad. You try some: BALL: ball or mall? BAG: bag or rag? TOY: boy or toy? BUN: bun or run? RAKE: rake or bake? 9. For assessment, give the worksheet to the students. Students are to draw a line from each of the butterflies to a picture that begins with the sound of the letter. Reference to a source that can tell us more. Book: Bubble Bear:Letter B Scholastic Press, 2001, 16pp. Internet site: assessment worksheet Bruce Murray, Brush Your Teeth with F. to the Projects Index Actually chili with meat is rather quite disgusting. Make a Neuron Create a model of a neuron by using clay, playdough, styrofoam, recyclables, food or anything else you can get your hands on. Use pictures from books to give you an idea of where the components of a neuron should go and what shape they should be. Use different colors to indicate different structures. Make a neural circuit with a few of the neurons. Create sensory or motor systems. Eat your model if you made it out of food!! If you would like to use this "build a neuron" as a classroom activity, here is a lesson ready to go. Need some play dough but don't have any? Here is a recipe that you can use to make your own: Mix: 1 cup flour, 1/2 cup salt & 2 teaspoon cream of tartar Cook over a low heat, stirring constantly until it thickens. Let it cool a bit and then knead it. Keep in an airtight container so it doesn't dry out. You can also add color to the play dough by adding some poster paint with the liquid instead of food coloring. Try to use as little paint as possible to get the desired color or it will make the play dough sticky. Get out those beads and make a neuron! This neuron with seven dendrites requires 65 beads: 42 beads for the dendrites, 10 beads for the cell body, 12 beads for the axon and 1 bead for the synaptic terminal. String the beads using the pattern in the diagrams below. The string can be yarn, rope, or for the best result use flexible wire. You can also create your own pattern or use a different colored bead for a nucleus in the cell body. Get out those pipe cleaners and make a neuron! This neuron pipe cleaners of 5 different colors: one color each for the dendrites, cell body, axon, myelin sheath and synaptic terminal. Any colors will do. If you have ever played any "string games," then this neuron model should be easy for you to make. Follow the steps on this page to make a neuron from string.
